Abstract:
<jats:p>Digital test scenario generation technology, a key support for intelligent algorithm verification and application, has broken through with the development of LLM. The paper identifies the core requirements of digital test scenario generation—including diversity and automation—then introduces the technical framework behind two key components: layout construction based on generative algorithms and intent reasoning powered by large models. It also conducts a comparative analysis of models such as Dungeon Alchemist and CityDreamer4D. To verify the effectiveness of these models, the paper employs both quantitative metrics and intelligent algorithm testing. Additionally, the paper points out existing shortcuts of the technology, such as its inadequate handling of complex scenario details, and offers insights into the generation of scenario’s future prospects, especially in enhancing reliability, adding efficiency, and advancing application in The field of digital twin technology. It also pays attention to personal local deployment and usability of the model. The study explores generative models that fit the needs of independent game developers and media producers, and outlines a vision for the development of such lightweight models moving forward.</jats:p>

## 1. GenCAD-Self-Repairing: Feasibility Enhancement for 3D CAD Generation
Valid Text Names:
'tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 1-2', 'tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 2-4', 'tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 4-5', 'tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 5-7', 'tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 7-8', 'tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 8-8'

BibTex:
@article{tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement,
    author = "Tsuji, Chikaha and Medina, Enrique Flores and Gupta, Harshit and Alam, Md Ferdous",
    title = "GenCAD-Self-Repairing: Feasibility Enhancement for 3D CAD Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.23287",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.23287",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.23287"
}


Abstract:
With the advancement of generative AI, research on its application to 3D model generation has gained traction, particularly in automating the creation of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) files from images. GenCAD is a notable model in this domain, leveraging an autoregressive transformer-based architecture with a contrastive learning framework to generate CAD programs. However, a major limitation of GenCAD is its inability to consistently produce feasible boundary representations (B-reps), with approximately 10% of generated designs being infeasible. To address this, we propose GenCAD-Self-Repairing, a framework that enhances the feasibility of generative CAD models through diffusion guidance and a self-repairing pipeline. This framework integrates a guided diffusion denoising process in the latent space and a regression-based correction mechanism to refine infeasible CAD command sequences while preserving geometric accuracy. Our approach successfully converted two-thirds of infeasible designs in the baseline method into feasible ones, significantly improving the feasibility rate while simultaneously maintaining a reasonable level of geometric accuracy between the point clouds of ground truth models and generated models. By significantly improving the feasibility rate of generating CAD models, our approach helps expand the availability of high-quality training data and enhances the applicability of AI-driven CAD generation in manufacturing, architecture, and product design.

## 2. Agentic AI for Computer Vision: A Review
Valid Text Names:
'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 1-2', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 2-3', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 3-5', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 5-8', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 8-9', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 9-11', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 11-12', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 12-14', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 14-16', 'ulhaq2025agenticaifor pages 16-16'

BibTex:
@article{ulhaq2025agenticaifor,
    author = "Ulhaq, Anwaar",
    title = "Agentic AI for Computer Vision: A Review",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "EngRxiv",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.31224/5832",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.31224/5832",
    publisher = "Open Engineering Inc"
}


Abstract:
<jats:p>General Computer vision models operate as passive systems that produce one output and stop, while agentic AI for computer vision represents a shift toward autonomous visual decision-making systems. In such systems, visual input is used to plan actions, decide next steps, and refine results through feedback. It allows the model to select or design the optimal processing steps and improve its output over time. This review maps current research on agentic approaches in computer vision and examines how autonomy is implemented in practice. This search is based on standard databases, including IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, Scopus, Web of Science, arXiv, and OpenReview, and only studies where the final task was a computer vision outcome were included. Papers that used agents for non-visual tasks were excluded. Screening, selection, and data charting were conducted using the PRISMA Scoping Review (PRISMA ScR) guidelines. A scoping review was chosen instead of a systematic review because the field is new, and preprints dominate the evidence base. Each included study was examined for its autonomy design, visual modality, datasets, and evaluation method. The results indicate that agentic approaches are promising for tasks that involve multiple steps, self-correction, or interaction with an environment. This review clarifies the emerging landscape of agentic AI for computer vision and identifies research gaps, including the need for stronger definitions of autonomy, shared testing environments, and reproducible evaluation methods.</jats:p>

## 5. Unlocking resilience and sustainability with earth-based materials: a principled framework for urban transformation

}


Abstract:
This paper introduces a transformative “living” hypothesis in architecture and engineering, proposing a paradigm shift from conventional design to regenerative, ecologically interconnected resilient systems. At the heart of our hypothesis is the integration of earth-bound materials and bioreceptive surfaces through metabolic exchanges that can be directly monitored via bioelectricity using advanced computational models and cooperative governance structures. This innovative approach that links the living world with natural materials and digital computing, aims to foster sustainable urban development that dynamically and meaningfully responds to ecological shifts, thereby enhancing social sustainability and environmental resilience. Founded on an active relationship with Earth Based Materials (EBMs) our work operationalises the foundational link between organic life and inorganic matter, e.g., minerals, to establish a dynamic relationship between building materials, and ecological systems drawing on the foundational metabolisms of microbes. To enable this ambitious synthesis, our work builds upon and diverges from traditional foundations by operationalizing actor-network theory, new materialism, and regenerative design principles through the application of bioelectrical microbes to “living” materials and digital twins. We propose a novel resilience framework that not only advocates for a symbiotic relationship between human habitats and natural ecosystems but also outlines practical pathways for the creation of adaptive, self-organizing built environments that are informed by data collection and metabolic feedback loops. These environments are fundamentally regenerative, dynamic, and environmentally responsive in ways that can be understood and engaged by human engineers and designers, transcending current sustainability and resilience targets through a methodology rooted in interdisciplinary collaboration. We address challenges such as regulatory barriers, lack of standardization, and perceptions of inferiority compared to conventional materials, proposing a new standardization framework adaptable to the unique properties of these materials. Our vision is supported by advanced predictive digital modelling techniques and sensors, including the integration of biofilms that generate action potentials, enabling the development of Digital Twins that respond to metabolic signals to enhance sustainability, biodiversity, and ultimately generate environmentally positive socio-economic outcomes. This paper reviews existing methodologies to establish an overview of state-of-the-art developments and offers a clear, actionable plan and recommendations for the realization of regenerative and resilient systems in urban development. It contributes a unique perspective on sustainable urban development, emphasizing the need for a holistic approach, which integrates the foundational metabolism of microbes, assisted by big biological data and artificial intelligences that act in concert to respect both the environment and the intricate dynamics of living systems.

Abstract:
Additive manufacturing (AM) has revolutionized the design and production of complex geometries by offering unprecedented creative freedom over traditional manufacturing. Despite its growing prominence, AM lacks automated and standardized design rules tailored to specific AM processes, resulting in time-consuming and expert-dependent manual verification. To address these limitations, this research introduces a novel design for additive manufacturing (DfAM) framework consisting of two complementary models designed to automate the design process. The first model, based on a decision tree algorithm, evaluates part compliance with established AM design rules. A modified J48 classifier was implemented to enhance data mining accuracy by achieving a 91.25% classification performance accuracy. This model systematically assesses whether input part characteristics meet AM processing standards, thereby providing a robust tool for verifying design rules. The second model features an AM design rule engine developed with a Python-based graphical user interface (GUI). This engine generates specific recommendations for design adjustments based on part characteristics and machine compatibility, offering a user-friendly approach for identifying potential design issues and ensuring DfAM compliance. By linking part specifications to various AM techniques, this model supports both researchers and engineers in anticipating and mitigating design flaws. Overall, this research establishes a foundation for a comprehensive DfAM expert system.

Abstract:
3D printing technology has become increasingly popular in healthcare settings, with applications of 3D printed anatomical models ranging from diagnostics and surgical planning to patient education. However, as the use of 3D printed anatomical models becomes more widespread, there is a growing need for regulation and quality control to ensure their accuracy and safety. This literature review examines the current state of 3D printing in hospitals and FDA regulation process for software intended for use in producing 3D printed models and provides for the first time a comprehensive list of approved software platforms alongside the 3D printers that have been validated with each for producing 3D printed anatomical models. The process for verification and validation of these 3D printed products, as well as the potential for inaccuracy in these models, is discussed, including methods for testing accuracy, limits, and standards for accuracy testing. This article emphasizes the importance of regulation and quality control in the use of 3D printing technology in healthcare, the need for clear guidelines and standards for both the software and the printed products to ensure the safety and accuracy of 3D printed anatomical models, and the opportunity to expand the library of regulated 3D printers.

Abstract:
The integration of 3D printing technologies in automated manufacturing systems marks a significant progression in the manufacturing industry, enabling elevated degrees of customization, efficiency, and sustainability. This paper explores the synergy between 3D printing and automation by conducting a critical literature review combined with case study analysis, focusing on their roles in enhancing production lines within the framework of Industry 4.0 and smart factories. Key opportunities presented by this integration include mass customization at scale, reduced material waste, and improved just-in-time manufacturing processes. However, challenges related to quality control, scalability, and workforce adaptation remain critical issues that require careful consideration. The study also examines the emerging role of hybrid manufacturing systems that combine additive and subtractive processes, alongside the growing need for standardized regulations and frameworks to ensure consistency and safety. Case studies are highlighted, showcasing real-world applications of automated 3D printing technologies and AI-driven print optimization techniques. In conclusion, this paper contributes to advancing the scholarly understanding of automated 3D printing by synthesizing technical, organizational, and regulatory insights and outlining future trajectories for sustainable and agile production ecosystems.

Abstract:
Abstract Due to the high freedom of design, additive manufacturing (AM) is increasingly substituting conventional manufacturing technology in several sectors. However, the knowledge and the awareness for the suitable design of additively manufactured components or assemblies ensuring manufacturability and fully realizing its potential is still lacking. In recent years, approaches and tools have emerged that allow the incorporation of existing knowledge of Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM) into the design process. Nevertheless, these applications mostly do not consider the formalisation of both restrictive and opportunistic DfAM guidelines for their integration in design tools. Therefore, the following article presents a framework for the knowledge-driven adaptation of existing designs in the context of DfAM within an expert system. The novelty of the presented approach lies in the interdisciplinarity between the formalization of design guidelines and their integration and consideration within computeraided design for the semi-automated adaptation of functional non-assembly mechanisms. The application of the presented framework to a case study manufactured via Fused Layer Modeling (FLM) illustrates the applicability and benefits.

Abstract:
In Singapore, there is an increasing need for independence from manpower within the Building and Construction (B&C) Industry. Prefabricated Prefinished Volumetric Construction (PPVC) production is mainly driven by benefits in environmental pollution reduction, improved productivity, quality control, and customizability. However, overall cost savings have been counterbalanced by new cost drivers like modular precast moulds, transportation, hoisting, manufacturing & holding yards, and supervision costs. The highly modular requirements for PPVC places additive manufacturing in an advantageous position, due to its high customizability, low volume manufacturing capabilities for a faster manufacturing response time, faster production changeovers, and lower inventory requirements. However, C3DP has only just begun to move away from its early-stage development, where there is a need to closely evaluate the process parameters across buildability, extrudability, and pumpability aspects. As many parameters have been identified as having considerable influence on C3DP processes, monitoring systems for feedback applications seem to be an inevitable step forward to automation in construction. This paper has presented a broad analysis of the challenges posed to C3DP and feedback systems, stressing the admission of process parameters to correct multiple modes of failure.

Abstract:
<jats:p>By greatly increasing automation, accuracy, and flexibility at every step of the additive manufacturing process, from design and production to quality assurance, artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the 3D printing industry. The integration of AI algorithms into 3D printing systems enables real-time optimization of print parameters, accurate prediction of material behavior, and early defect detection using computer vision and sensor data. Machine learning (ML) techniques further streamline the design-to-production pipeline by generating complex geometries, automating slicing processes, and enabling adaptive, self-correcting control during printing—functions that align directly with the principles of Industry 4.0/5.0, where cyber-physical integration, autonomous decision-making, and human–machine collaboration drive intelligent manufacturing systems. Along with improving operational effectiveness and product uniformity, this potent combination of AI and 3D printing also propels the creation of intelligent manufacturing systems that are capable of self-learning. This confluence has the potential to completely transform sectors including consumer products, healthcare, construction, and aerospace as it develops. This comprehensive review explores how AI enhances the capabilities of 3D printing, with a focus on process optimization, defect detection, and intelligent control mechanisms. Moreover, unresolved challenges are highlighted—including data scarcity, limited generalizability across printers and materials, certification barriers in safety-critical domains, computational costs, and the need for explainable AI.</jats:p>

Abstract:
Additive manufacturing (AM) has impacted the manufacturing of complex three-dimensional objects in multiple materials for a wide array of applications. However, additive manufacturing, as an upcoming field, lacks automated and specific design rules for different AM processes. Moreover, the selection of specific AM processes for different geometries requires expert knowledge, which is difficult to replicate. An automated and data-driven system is needed that can capture the AM expert knowledge base and apply it to 3D-printed parts to avoid manufacturability issues. This research aims to develop a data-driven system for AM process selection within the design for additive manufacturing (DFAM) framework for Industry 4.0. A Genetic and Evolutionary Feature Weighting technique was optimized using 3D CAD data as an input to identify the optimal AM technique based on several requirements and constraints. A two-stage model was developed wherein the stage 1 model displayed average accuracies of 70% and the stage 2 model showed higher average accuracies of up to 97.33% based on quantitative feature labeling and augmentation of the datasets. The steady-state genetic algorithm (SSGA) was determined to be the most effective algorithm after benchmarking against estimation of distribution algorithm (EDA) and particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithms, respectively. The output of this system leads to the identification of optimal AM processes for manufacturing 3D objects. This paper presents an automated design for an additive manufacturing system that is accurate and can be extended to other 3D-printing processes.

## 1. LLM4CAD: Multi-Modal Large Language Models for 3D Computer-Aided Design Generation
Valid Text Names:
'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 1-2', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 3-3', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 3-4', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 4-5', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 4-4', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 5-6', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 5-5', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 6-6', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 7-7', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 7-8', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 8-8', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 9-9', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 9-10', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 10-11', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 10-10', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 11-11', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 11-12', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 12-12', 'li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge pages 13-13'

BibTex:
@article{li2024llm4cadmultimodallarge,
    author = "Li, Xingang and Sun, Yuewan and Sha, Zhenghui",
    title = "LLM4CAD: Multi-Modal Large Language Models for 3D Computer-Aided Design Generation",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "Volume 6: 36th International Conference on Design Theory and Methodology (DTM)",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1115/detc2024-143740",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1115/detc2024-143740",
    publisher = "American Society of Mechanical Engineers"
}


Abstract:

 The evolution of multimodal large language models (LLMs) capable of processing diverse input modalities (e.g., text and images) holds new prospects for their application in engineering design, such as the generation of 3D computer-aided design (CAD) models. However, little is known about the ability of multimodal LLMs to generate 3D design objects, and there is a lack of quantitative assessment. In this study, we develop an approach to enable two LLMs, GPT-4 and GPT-4V, to generate 3D CAD models (i.e., LLM4CAD) and perform experiments to evaluate their efficacy. To address the challenge of data scarcity for multimodal LLM studies, we created a data synthesis pipeline to generate CAD models, sketches, and image data of typical mechanical components (e.g., gears and springs) and collect their natural-language descriptions with dimensional information using Amazon Mechanical Turk. We positioned the CAD program (programming script for CAD design) as a bridge, facilitating the conversion of LLMs’ textual output into tangible CAD design objects. We focus on two critical capabilities: the generation of syntactically correct CAD programs (Cap1) and the accuracy of the parsed 3D shapes (Cap2) quantified by intersection over union. The results show that both GPT-4 and GPT-4V demonstrate potential in 3D CAD generation. Specifically, on average, GPT-4V outperforms when processing only text-based input, exceeding the results obtained using multimodal inputs, such as text with image, for Cap 1 and Cap 2. However, when examining category-specific results of mechanical components, while the same trend still holds for Cap 2, the prominence of multimodal inputs is increasingly evident for more complex geometries (e.g., springs and gears) in Cap 1. The potential of multimodal LLMs in enhancing 3D CAD generation is clear, but their application must be carefully calibrated to the complexity of the target CAD models to be generated.

Abstract:
The rapid advancement of technology and innovation is also impacting education across different levels. The rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) is beginning to transform education in various areas, from course materials to assessment systems. This requires educators to reconsider how they evaluate students’ knowledge. It is crucial to understand if and to what extent assignments can be completed using AI tools. This study explores two hypotheses about the risks of using code-based 3D modeling software in education and the potential for students to delegate their work to AI when completing assignments. We selected two tasks that students were able to successfully complete independently and provided the same amount of information (both textual and image) to AI in order to generate the necessary code. We tested the widely used ChatGPT and Gemini AI bots to assess their current performance in generating code based on text prompts or image-based information for the two models. Our findings indicate that students are not yet able to entirely delegate their work to these AI tools.

## 6. CADReview: Automatically Reviewing CAD Programs with Error Detection and Correction
Valid Text Names:
'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 1-1', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 1-3', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 3-4', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 4-5', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 5-6', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 6-8', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 8-9', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 9-10', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 10-11', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 11-13', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 13-15', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 15-16', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 16-19', 'chen2025cadreviewautomaticallyreviewing pages 19-19'

    title = "CADReview: Automatically Reviewing CAD Programs with Error Detection and Correction",
    year = "2025",

    volume = "abs/2505.22304",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.22304",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.22304"
}


Abstract:
Computer-aided design (CAD) is crucial in prototyping 3D objects through geometric instructions (i.e., CAD programs). In practical design workflows, designers often engage in time-consuming reviews and refinements of these prototypes by comparing them with reference images. To bridge this gap, we introduce the CAD review task to automatically detect and correct potential errors, ensuring consistency between the constructed 3D objects and reference images. However, recent advanced multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle to recognize multiple geometric components and perform spatial geometric operations within the CAD program, leading to inaccurate reviews. In this paper, we propose the CAD program repairer (ReCAD) framework to effectively detect program errors and provide helpful feedback on error correction. Additionally, we create a dataset, CADReview, consisting of over 20K program-image pairs, with diverse errors for the CAD review task. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our ReCAD significantly outperforms existing MLLMs, which shows great potential in design applications.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?"}

function.arguments: {"query": "CADSmith multi-agent programmatic geometric validation CAD generation", "min_year": 2025}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'barkley2603cadsmithmultiagentcad pages 6-7'.

Jesse Barkley, Rumi Loghmani, and A. Farimani. Cadsmith: multi-agent cad generation with programmatic geometric validation. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512. This article has 0 citations.

The CadSmith excerpt reports several failure modes, geometric errors, and quantitative results for LLM-generated CAD (CadQuery/OCCT pipeline). Major failure modes: false convergence where numerical/kernel metrics look acceptable while the shape is fundamentally wrong; generation of geometrically valid but structurally divergent solids (different construction approach than reference); and near-miss manufacturing issues such as small gaps (e.g., T3 019 "contains subtle gaps between the arms and the central hub") that evade both programmatic checks and visual inspection. Quantitative outcomes: the full multi-agent pipeline produced 100% execution success and reduced mean Chamfer Distance by 38× versus zero-shot. Per-tier median metrics (Table II) are reported: T1 (N=50) CDmed=0.32, CDmean=0.47, F1med=0.9985, IoUmed=0.9834; T2 (N=25) CDmed=0.32, CDmean=0.58, F1med=0.9979, IoUmed=0.7661; T3 (N=25) CDmed=0.96, CDmean=1.42, F1med=0.8859, IoUmed=0.9582. Some individual hard cases show low F1 (e.g., "T3 016 at F1 = 0.57, T3 024 at F1 = 0.59"). Vision is critical: removing the three-view rendered image dramatically worsens results ("increases T3 mean Chamfer Distance from 1.42 to 49.68" and "drops mean F1 from 0.85 to 0.74"). Overall, LLM-based CAD generation can produce high-level correctness on simple parts but struggles with complex multi-feature engineering parts, subtle manufacturability defects, and failures that escape kernel-only validation.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'barkley2603cadsmithmultiagentcad pages 1-2'.

Jesse Barkley, Rumi Loghmani, and A. Farimani. Cadsmith: multi-agent cad generation with programmatic geometric validation. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt identifies key failure modes and limitations of LLM-generated CAD code: LLMs commonly hallucinate dimensions and misuse APIs, produce execution errors, and create topological/geometric faults (bounding-box errors by millimeters, missing faces, incorrect face/edge/vertex counts) that make parts unusable. Prior approaches suffer either from single-pass generation with no programmatic geometric verification or from relying on visual feedback that loses metric precision. To address this, CADSmith uses kernel-level checks (OpenCASCADE measurements: bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, solid validity) plus an independent vision-language Judge to catch both numerical and high-level shape failures. Quantitatively, on the authors' benchmark CADSmith improved the execution rate to 100% (from 95%), raised median F1 from 0.9707 to 0.9846, median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629, and reduced mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74. The paper emphasizes that programmatic geometric validation (including solid validity) is necessary because visual metrics alone cannot resolve millimeter-scale dimensional errors.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeaia pages 25-29'.

R Kilpeläinen. Utilizing generative ai in 3d modeling for robotics. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt identifies several specific failure modes and limitations when LLMs generate CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD): exclusion of certain feature types (e.g., fillets) because they require referencing current model state; omission of key geometric features (e.g., a flange’s borehole); incorrect formation of complex shapes (e.g., springs’ helical structures); and simple-but-wrong simplifications (e.g., generating a circular profile instead of a hexagon). Topological and validity issues are common: not every generated operation sequence yields a valid 3D model, and the chance of invalid outputs rises for longer, more complex sequences. Part of the problem stems from training-data biases (DeepCAD’s ~170,000 models exclude some features and are biased toward rectangles/cylinders) and from LLMs’ reliance on statistical text prediction rather than explicit geometric reasoning. The excerpt also notes LLMs can perform well on simple objects (often producing syntactically correct code), and mentions iterative verification/refinement approaches (CADCodeVerify) that use visual inspection and feedback to improve outputs. The excerpt does not report quantitative metrics such as pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, or Chamfer distances.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29'.

R Kilpeläinen. Utilizing generative ai in 3d modeling for robotics. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt reports several qualitative failure modes and limitations of LLM-driven CAD code generation (CadQuery, OpenSCAD, etc.). Common geometric errors include producing incorrect primitives (e.g., generating a circular profile instead of a hexagon), omitting key features (fillets, a flange’s borehole), and failing to form complex shapes correctly (a spring’s helical structure). A core limitation is that LLMs, without geometric reasoning or specific training, lack understanding of spatial relationships and 3D constraints and typically predict statistically likely text sequences rather than guaranteed-valid model operations. Performance degrades as object complexity increases; dataset biases toward simple primitives (rectangles, cylinders) further limit robustness. Sequence-based systems also face topological validity issues—some operation sequences do not yield valid 3D models and validity declines with longer, more complex sequences. Natural language variability complicates prompt reliability. The excerpt notes iterative verification/refinement approaches (e.g., CADCodeVerify) that visually inspect and correct generated code, but does not provide numerical evaluation. No quantitative metrics (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) are reported in these pages.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'elistratov2026cadevolvecreatingrealistic pages 8-9'.

Maksim Elistratov, M. Barannikov, G. Ivanov, V. Khrulkov, Anton Konushin, Andrey Kuznetsov, and Dmitrii Zhemchuzhnikov. Cadevolve: creating realistic cad via program evolution. ArXiv, Feb 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.16317, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.16317. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt documents several failure modes, geometric errors, and evaluation choices when large models generate CAD code (CadQuery/OpenSCAD). Common failure modes include non-compiling programs and non-watertight or degenerate solids, which the authors explicitly penalize during RL training: "r = 10 · IoU if code compiles otherwise r = −10." They treat "invalid generations (non-compiling or non-watertight)" as a key error class and report an invalid-rate (IR) metric alongside Median Chamfer Distance (Median CD) and mean IoU. RL can improve geometric fidelity but may increase invalid rates due to "more frequent use of complex, collision-prone operations." The authors also note that code-level augmentations help but are insufficient without canonicalizing code style and shape size, and that synthetic dataset creation introduces a "Synthetic distribution mismatch." Rendering/export differences (MCB's higher STL tolerances) cause domain shifts that alter visual cues and affect generalization. Overall, the paper describes qualitative failure types (compilation, watertightness, degeneracy, collisions, domain-shifted silhouettes) and the quantitative metrics used (Median CD, mean IoU, IR), but the provided excerpt does not include numeric pass/feasibility/watertightness rates.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'kansala2026generativeaidrivensystems pages 59-62'.

L Känsälä. Generative ai-driven systems supporting computer-aided design and manufacturing. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt identifies several concrete failure modes and limitations when LLMs generate CAD code (OpenSCAD/CadQuery-style). Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) was limited because ChromaDB had no documents and only a gear-specific library (Getriebe.scad) consistently helped, so many runs behaved like non-RAG runs. The multi-agent refinement loop can converge prematurely—"most runs terminate after a single iteration or stop at the iteration limit without resolving the identified issue"—and improvements from multi-agent setups are probabilistic rather than guaranteed, although they provide richer diagnostics (geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, parameter checks). Geometric errors include failures on fine-grained features, spatial relationships, constraint-aware modeling, topological inconsistencies, and sensitivity to prompt phrasing. LLM-specific problems include non-deterministic outputs, hallucinations such as inventing non-existent OpenSCAD functions, misinterpreting dimensions, and inconsistently filling missing details. Multimodal processing is weak because images are passed as base64 payloads, losing fine geometric cues and only capturing coarse proportions. Evaluation limits are noted: IoU depends on voxelization pitch and the benchmark set was restricted. The excerpt does not provide numeric quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distances) for these experiments.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 15-18'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The excerpt documents a concrete failure mode when LLM-generated CadQuery code constructs arcs: using threePointArc() with control points that are nearly colinear and extremely close in magnitude can trigger an OpenCascade kernel runtime error (Standard_Failure: GC_MakeArcOfCircle::Value() - no result). The root cause is floating-point precision limits at small scales (around 10^-2), which make arc fitting numerically unstable. The authors note this as an example of a broader limitation of LLM-based CAD generation: basic primitives often work, but degenerate or numerically challenging configurations lead to runtime failures. Practical mitigations suggested include increasing sketch scale, using more robust arc primitives such as radiusArc(), or approximating arcs with splines/polylines. The text also recommends training models on more diverse, numerically challenging CAD data to improve robustness. The provided excerpt does not contain any quantitative metrics (no pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness percentages, or Chamfer distance values), so those specific numerical results are not available here.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'sadik2509humanintheloopquantitativeevaluation pages 4-5'.

Ahmed R. Sadik and M. Bujny. Human-in-the-loop: quantitative evaluation of 3d models generation by large language models. ArXiv, Sep 2509. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2509.07010, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2509.07010. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt defines a set of quantitative geometric metrics used to evaluate LLM-generated CAD outputs (OpenSCAD): Volumetric Similarity (Sv), Surface Similarity (Sa), Hausdorff Distance (Hd), PCA Alignment Score (Sp), ICP Alignment Score (Si), and a weighted aggregate Final Similarity Score (Sf). The experiment used OpenAI GPT-4.5 to produce OpenSCAD code which was rendered to STL for metric-based comparison. Observed failure modes include orientation/rotation errors (example: "the top arm of the bracket was rotated"), which manifest as degraded PCA and ICP scores; missing features, small deformations, and sharp local misalignments are detectable by Hausdorff distance. Input modality matters: three-view orthographics lack depth cues and are more ambiguous, while geometric structure descriptions and code-level correction prompts yield progressively higher fidelity. The human-in-the-loop iterative pipeline is used to identify and correct errors until plateau. The excerpt reports that code-level correction prompts produced perfect or near-perfect metrics, explicitly noting "zero Hausdorff distance." However, the excerpt does not report many requested numeric outcomes: there are no explicit pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness statistics, or Chamfer distance values included.

## 2. From Idea to CAD: A Language Model-Driven Multi-Agent System for Collaborative Design
Valid Text Names:
'ocker2503fromideato pages 1-3', 'ocker2503fromideato pages 3-5', 'ocker2503fromideato pages 5-8', 'ocker2503fromideato pages 8-10', 'ocker2503fromideato pages 10-11'

    author = "Ocker, Felix and Menzel, Stefan and Sadik, Ahmed and Rios, Thiago",
    title = "From Idea to CAD: A Language Model-Driven Multi-Agent System for Collaborative Design",
    year = "2503",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2503.04417",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2503.04417",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.04417"
}


Abstract:
Creating digital models using Computer Aided Design (CAD) is a process that requires in-depth expertise. In industrial product development, this process typically involves entire teams of engineers, spanning requirements engineering, CAD itself, and quality assurance. We present an approach that mirrors this team structure with a Vision Language Model (VLM)-based Multi Agent System, with access to parametric CAD tooling and tool documentation. Combining agents for requirements engineering, CAD engineering, and vision-based quality assurance, a model is generated automatically from sketches and/ or textual descriptions. The resulting model can be refined collaboratively in an iterative validation loop with the user. Our approach has the potential to increase the effectiveness of design processes, both for industry experts and for hobbyists who create models for 3D printing. We demonstrate the potential of the architecture at the example of various design tasks and provide several ablations that show the benefits of the architecture's individual components.

## 1. A Survey on Deep Learning in 3D CAD Reconstruction
Valid Text Names:
'lin2025asurveyon pages 1-3', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 3-4', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 4-6', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 6-7', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 7-9', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 9-11', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 11-13', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 13-14', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 14-16', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 16-18', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 19-21', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 21-22', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 22-24', 'lin2025asurveyon pages 24-25'

BibTex:
@article{lin2025asurveyon,
    author = "Lin, Ruiquan and Ji, Yunwei and Ding, Wanting and Wu, Tianxiang and Zhu, Yaosheng and Jiang, Mengxi",
    title = "A Survey on Deep Learning in 3D CAD Reconstruction",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "Applied Sciences",
    volume = "15",
    pages = "6681",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.3390/app15126681",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/app15126681",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "12",
    issn = "2076-3417"
}


Abstract:
Three-dimensional CAD reconstruction is a long-standing and important task in fields such as industrial manufacturing, architecture, medicine, film and television, research, and education. Reconstructing CAD models remains a persistent challenge in machine learning. There have been many studies on deep learning in the field of 3D reconstruction. In recent years, with the release of CAD datasets, there have been more and more studies on 3D CAD reconstruction using deep learning. With the continuous deepening of research, deep learning has significantly improved the performance of tasks in the field of CAD reconstruction. However, this task remains challenging due to data scarcity and labeling difficulties, model complexity, and lack of generality and adaptability. This paper reviews both classic and recent research results on 3D CAD reconstruction tasks based on deep learning. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first investigation focusing on the CAD reconstruction task in the field of deep learning. Since there are relatively few studies related to 3D CAD reconstruction, we also investigate the reconstruction and generation of 2D CAD sketches. According to the different input data, we divide all investigations into the following categories: point cloud input to 3D CAD models, sketch input to 3D CAD models, other input to 3D CAD models, reconstruction and generation of 2D sketches, characterization of CAD data, CAD datasets, and related evaluation indicators. Commonly used datasets are outlined in our taxonomy. We provide a brief overview of the current research background, challenges, and recent results. Finally, future research directions are discussed.

## 3. Parametric Primitive Analysis of CAD Sketches With Vision Transformer

    publisher = "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)",
    issue = "10",

Abstract:
The design and analysis of computer-aided design (CAD) sketches play a crucial role in industrial product design, primarily involving CAD primitives and their interprimitive constraints. To address challenges related to error accumulation in autoregressive models and the complexities associated with self-supervised model design for this task, we propose a two-stage network framework. This framework consists of a primitive network and a constraint network, transforming the sketch analysis task into a set prediction problem to enhance the effective handling of primitives and constraints. By decoupling target types from parameters, the model gains increased flexibility and optimization while reducing complexity. In addition, the constraint network incorporates a pointer module to explicitly indicate the relationship between constraint parameters and primitive indices, enhancing interpretability and performance. Qualitative and quantitative analyzes on two publicly available datasets demonstrate the superiority of this method.

## 4. Self-Supervised Representation Learning for CAD

Abstract:
Virtually every object in the modern world was created, modified, analyzed and optimized using computer aided design (CAD) tools. An active CAD research area is the use of data-driven machine learning methods to learn from the massive repositories of geometric and program representations. However, the lack of labeled data in CAD's native format, i.e., the parametric boundary representation (B-Rep), poses an obstacle at present difficult to overcome. Several datasets of mechanical parts in B-Rep format have recently been released for machine learning research. However, large-scale databases are mostly unlabeled, and labeled datasets are small. Additionally, task-specific label sets are rare and costly to annotate. This work proposes to leverage unlabeled CAD geometry on supervised learning tasks. We learn a novel, hybrid implicit/explicit surface representation for B-Rep geometry. Further, we show that this pre-training both significantly improves few-shot learning performance and achieves state-of-the-art performance on several current B-Rep benchmarks.

## 5. CADParser: A Learning Approach of Sequence Modeling for B-Rep CAD

Abstract:
Computer-Aided Design (CAD) plays a crucial role in industrial manufacturing by providing geometry information and the construction workflow for manufactured objects. The construction information enables effective re-editing of parametric CAD models. While boundary representation (B-Rep) is the standard format for representing geometry structures, JSON format is an alternative due to the lack of uniform criteria for storing the construction workflow. Regrettably, most CAD models available on the Internet only offer geometry information, omitting the construction procedure and hampering creation efficiency. This paper proposes a learning approach CADParser to infer the underlying modeling sequences given a B-Rep CAD model. It achieves this by treating the CAD geometry structure as a graph and the construction workflow as a sequence. Since the existing CAD dataset only contains two operations (i.e., Sketch and Extrusion), limiting the diversity of the CAD model creation, we also introduce a large-scale dataset incorporating a more comprehensive range of operations such as Revolution, Fillet, and Chamfer. Each model includes both the geometry structure and the construction sequences. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method can compete with the existing state-of-the-art methods quantitatively and qualitatively. Data is available at https://drive.google.com/CADParserData.

## 8. UniSketch: A Unified Framework for Parametric Sketch Generation and Constraint Prediction

Abstract:
<jats:p>In modern Computer-Aided Design (CAD), parametric sketches play a crucial role by capturing both the geometric structure and design intent through constraints. However, existing deep learning–based sketch methods remain restricted to simple geometric primitives and limited constraint types, hindering their application to complex real-world engineering tasks. To address this gap, we introduce the UniSketch dataset, comprising 3,836,290 sketches. It offers a comprehensive and diverse collection of 7 types of geometric primitives and 23 types of 2D constraints, all represented as unified vector sequences suitable for deep learning applications. Leveraging the UniSketch dataset, we propose a unified multi-task Transformer framework as a true foundation model for parametric sketch modeling, supporting diverse core tasks like image-to-sketch generation, constraint prediction, and unconditional sketch synthesis. Furthermore, the generated sketches can be efficiently converted to CAD-compatible formats, enabling seamless integration with industrial CAD system for re-editing and reusing. The experimental results show that UniSketch outperforms existing methods in multiple tasks, demonstrating its versatility and practical value in industrial CAD applications.</jats:p>

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000008

From text 'barkley2603cadsmithmultiagentcad pages 6-7'.

Jesse Barkley, Rumi Loghmani, and A. Farimani. Cadsmith: multi-agent cad generation with programmatic geometric validation. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes mitigation strategies used in CADSmith to address LLM-driven CAD failures. Key elements are a multi-agent pipeline (Validator/Judge and Refiner agents) that implements a self-repair iterative loop: models generate CadQuery code, the Validator receives the prompt, code, OpenCASCADE (OCCT) kernel metrics, and a three-view rendered image, then cross-references code logic, kernel measurements, and visuals to detect mismatches and produce structured feedback for the Refiner. Programmatic geometric validation uses kernel-level checks (OCCT metrics, voxelized IoU, Chamfer) to drive corrections; the system formats feedback as {"passed": bool, "feedback": "..."} and includes escalation rules to recommend fundamentally different construction approaches if repeated fixes fail. Ablations show visual feedback is critical—removing the three-view render dramatically degrades complex-part performance—because kernel metrics alone can miss false convergence or subtle gaps that make parts unmanufacturable. The full pipeline yields large execution and fidelity gains versus zero-shot, demonstrating that combined multi-agent control, vision-in-the-loop, and programmatic kernel validation form an effective self-repair strategy for text-to-CAD generation.


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'ocker2503fromideato pages 3-5'.

Felix Ocker, Stefan Menzel, Ahmed Sadik, and Thiago Rios. From idea to cad: a language model-driven multi-agent system for collaborative design. ArXiv, Mar 2503. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.04417, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.04417. This article has 24 citations.

The excerpt documents several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. It proposes a VLM-based multi-agent system with distinct roles (RequirementsEngineer, CadEngineer, QualityAssuranceEngineer) to iteratively clarify requirements, generate parametric CAD code, and verify results. Interactive clarification loops and reflexion (internal/external feedback) are used to resolve ambiguities. Prior work explored visual feedback loops (four-view visual feedback), though one study reported that visual feedback did not improve CAD-code generation. Iterative/self-repair strategies appear in frameworks that generate Python parametric CAD code and improve prompts over time based on VLM feedback; debugging and executability checks are explicitly used (LLM4CAD uses a debugger; generated code is checked for executability and VLMs are prompted to fix errors). Safeguards include requiring human confirmation before executing generated code and CadEngineer checks of documentation for potential misuse, plus QA-agent feedback loops to trigger regeneration. The excerpt does not mention DPO/RL fine-tuning, kernel-level checks, explicit watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD methodologies.

## pqac-00000010

From text 'kansala2026generativeaidrivensystems pages 59-62'.

L Känsälä. Generative ai-driven systems supporting computer-aided design and manufacturing. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt recommends multiple mitigations for LLM-driven CAD failures. Multi-agent architectures are valuable because they “improve relational reasoning, manufacturability assessment, and failure detection” and “provided richer diagnostic output, including explicit geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, and parameter verification,” though the refinement loop can “frequently converge prematurely” and is probabilistic. Strengthening the RAG subsystem is advised: populate ChromaDB, expand robust OpenSCAD libraries for common parts, and “incorporat[e] RAG into the manufacturing engineer agent” so decisions are grounded in documentation. Model-level improvements include “Fine-tuned LLMs trained on structured CAD datasets, parametric scripts, and engineering documentation” and replacing base64 image handling with “true vision language models that process image directly” to capture finer geometry. Procedural mitigations include “more structured refinement routines, clearer feedback mechanisms and optimized agent prompts” to improve convergence and richer programmatic checks (explicit geometric validation and manufacturability assessment). For evaluation and test-driven validation, the paper suggests improving metrics beyond voxel IoU with “higher-resolution voxelization, surface-distance metrics and a more diverse set of reference models.” These recommendations target improved feedback loops, grounded retrieval, higher-fidelity vision, and programmatic geometric validation.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'barkley2603cadsmithmultiagentcad pages 1-2'.

Jesse Barkley, Rumi Loghmani, and A. Farimani. Cadsmith: multi-agent cad generation with programmatic geometric validation. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes CADSmith's mitigation strategies for LLM CAD generation failures. Key strategies include a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, Refiner) and an iterative, closed-loop refinement process with two nested correction loops: an inner loop to resolve execution errors and an outer loop for programmatic geometric validation. The outer loop uses OpenCASCADE kernel-level checks — bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity — combined with an independent vision-language Judge that performs three-view rendered inspection to provide holistic visual feedback and avoid self-confirmation bias. The system uses retrieval-augmented generation over API documentation rather than fine-tuning to keep models aligned with evolving CAD libraries. Prior related systems (e.g., Query2CAD) used visual feedback (BLIP-2) for iterative refinement but lacked the kernel-precision available here. The paper frames these mechanisms (multi-agent decomposition, execution-error self-repair, kernel-level verification, independent VLM judging, and RAG) as complementary safeguards to reduce hallucinated dimensions, API misuse, and shape/dimensional mismatches.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'kansala2026generativeaidrivensystemsa pages 59-62'.

L Känsälä. Generative ai-driven systems supporting computer-aided design and manufacturing. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt recommends several practical mitigations for LLM-driven CAD failures. Multi-agent designs with explicit critic and manufacturing stages can increase detection and correction of errors and provide richer diagnostics (explicit geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, parameter verification), but require improved feedback structure and more structured refinement routines to avoid premature convergence. Retrieval-augmented-generation should be strengthened by populating ChromaDB with CAD documentation, parametric examples and design rules and by building robust OpenSCAD libraries for common parts; RAG should also be integrated into the manufacturing engineer agent so decisions are grounded in technical documentation. Model-level mitigations include fine-tuning LLMs on structured CAD datasets and parametric scripts to improve constraint reasoning and reduce prompt sensitivity, and replacing the current base64 image pipeline with true vision-language models to extract higher-fidelity geometry from sketches. Evaluation and programmatic validation should be improved beyond coarse IoU: use higher-resolution voxelization, surface-distance metrics, and more diverse benchmarks to better detect geometric and manufacturability failures. The text does not explicitly mention DPO/RL methods, kernel-level checks, watertightness-specific routines, or explicit test-driven CAD workflows by name, though the advocated programmatic validation, diagnostics, and refinement loops support similar objectives.

URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt proposes several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. It highlights LLM-centered multi-agent architectures that act as coordinating hubs managing domain-specific agents (inspired by engineering workflows like the V-model) to separate requirements, modeling, and QA responsibilities. Vision-language models (VLMs) coupled with CAD-specific tool suites (e.g., CAD-Assistant) enable iterative, closed-loop visual feedback: the VLM interprets sketches or multimodal queries, issues CAD commands, observes the evolving design state, and adjusts future actions. Programmatic geometric validation is emphasized via multiple LLM-based strategies to ensure geometric and semantic correctness, and explicit AI-based error detection/correction pipelines are noted (e.g., CADReview’s ReCAD repairer). Integration of physical simulation and test signals (e.g., PhysicsX) supplies early validation and optimization, reducing downstream testing burden. Structured program representations (DSLs) and learned generative models (VAE/GRU) for shape assembly also improve correctness by constraining outputs. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss DPO/RL fine-tuning, kernel-level CAD checks, watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD methodologies, so those mitigation techniques are not covered here.

## pqac-00000014

From text 'wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 30-32'.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt suggests several mitigation directions for CAD-generation failures. Primary proposals include automated detection and correction of design errors via trained models with real-time feedback during the design process, and incorporation of physical simulation data early to reduce testing time and improve accuracy. The text highlights kernel-level interoperability issues (proprietary CAD kernels and naming/parameter handling) and suggests exchanging via standard formats and reconstruction as a current workaround, though this complicates learning/tasks. Fundamental model-validity checks are emphasized: many AI-generated models contain geometric/topological flaws (self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges) that make them non-watertight and unusable for downstream manufacturing or FEA, indicating the need for programmatic validity/watertightness verification. The authors note the importance of embedding geometric constraints into generation (currently only partially addressed: sketch-level constraints or traditional solvers) to enable rule-based editing and parametric updates. They also call out poor support for iterative, user-driven refinement. Finally, the excerpt points to dataset limitations as a barrier to more complex, robust model generation and repair pipelines.

The excerpt describes several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. Key approaches include synthesizing symbolic CAD programs (DSLs or parametric scripts) that are executed by CAD kernels, which improves traceability and allows programmatic validation. Iterative, feedback-driven pipelines are emphasized: systems analyze rendered outputs (e.g., via VQA) and re-prompt LLMs when validation scores fall below thresholds, enabling self-repair and multi-turn refinement. VLM-based visual inspection (CADCodeVerify) asks geometric-attribute questions (e.g., “is the hole centered?”) and returns corrective feedback to resolve specification violations. Multi-agent / tool-based architectures are proposed where LLMs coordinate specialized modules (perception, planning, verification), mirroring engineering workflows and enabling division-of-labor for verification and refinement. Fine-tuning strategies are noted (e.g., fine-tuning GPT-3.5 on prompt-program pairs) to improve fidelity. The excerpt explicitly mentions compilation/execution via off-the-shelf CAD kernels but does not explicitly discuss DPO/RL training, dedicated kernel-level automated checks, watertightness verification, or explicit test-driven CAD frameworks—though the described validation loops and programmatic execution imply opportunities to integrate such checks.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Cancer remains one of the most challenging diseases to diagnose and treat, requiring the development of innovative therapeutic and diagnostic strategies. Cancer progression includes enzyme dysregulation, which orchestrates key biological processes ranging from metabolic reprogramming, epigenetic modifications to drug metabolism and immune evasion. Enzymes drive and modulate their roles in the tumor microenvironment, influencing how cancer cells adapt to stress, resist treatments, and evade immune surveillance. This review paper examines recent advances in enzyme engineering and its potential in cancer treatment. Computational tools, including artificial intelligence, have contributed significantly to enzyme optimization, enabling improvements in catalytic efficiency, stability, and specificity through structural modeling, functional annotation and generative design. Enzyme engineering can be optimized for targeted therapies, minimizing off-target effects while maximizing therapeutic potential. Challenges such as enzyme stability, delivery mechanisms, and immunogenicity persist, but recent advances offer promising solutions. Therefore, we also review the integration of computational approaches and experimental advances in enzyme engineering, thereby offering insights into future directions for optimizing enzyme engineering strategies in cancer treatment and diagnosis. In essence, this review provides an up-to-date synthesis linking the fundamental biological understanding of enzymes in cancer with the rapidly evolving field of enzyme engineering and the powerful capabilities offered by computational tools, highlighting both promising advances and remaining hurdles such as benchmarking and interpretability of machine learning offered solutions.</jats:p>

Abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) have strong capabilities in solving diverse natural language processing tasks. However, the safety and security issues of LLM systems have become the major obstacle to their widespread application. Many studies have extensively investigated risks in LLM systems and developed the corresponding mitigation strategies. Leading-edge enterprises such as OpenAI, Google, Meta, and Anthropic have also made lots of efforts on responsible LLMs. Therefore, there is a growing need to organize the existing studies and establish comprehensive taxonomies for the community. In this paper, we delve into four essential modules of an LLM system, including an input module for receiving prompts, a language model trained on extensive corpora, a toolchain module for development and deployment, and an output module for exporting LLM-generated content. Based on this, we propose a comprehensive taxonomy, which systematically analyzes potential risks associated with each module of an LLM system and discusses the corresponding mitigation strategies. Furthermore, we review prevalent benchmarks, aiming to facilitate the risk assessment of LLM systems. We hope that this paper can help LLM participants embrace a systematic perspective to build their responsible LLM systems.

## pqac-00000016

From text 'wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 6-7'.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt contrasts representation choices for CAD generation: CSG and CAD command sequences (cmdseq) versus code-based domain-specific languages (DSLs) and sketch-based workflows (e.g., sketch + extrusion). It notes that common operations such as sketch and extrusion cover many industrial cases but are insufficient for lofts, sweeps, shells, and complex multi-body assemblies, causing coverage gaps where many shapes cannot be well expressed. DSLs are highlighted as more flexible: "Parameters, geometric constraints, and procedural logic can all be explicitly encoded", making them compatible with LLMs and modern AI pipelines. However, DSL research is limited by the lack of large standardized databases and unified syntax. The CAD interpreter converts commands/DSL code into geometric operations and enforces constraints; constraints are split into dimensional and geometric types (distances/angles/radii vs. parallelism/tangency/symmetry) and are solved by numerical, graph-based, decomposition-based, or learning-augmented methods. Representative datasets listed include SketchGraph (15M sketches, sketch+constraint, graph, real), ABC (1M+, B-Rep + Mesh, real), Fusion 360 Gallery (8,625, cmdseq, B-Rep + Mesh, real), and DeepCAD (178,238, cmdseq, B-Rep, real).

## pqac-00000017

From text 'heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 22-24'.

Negar Heidari and Alexandros Iosifidis. Geometric deep learning for computer-aided design: a survey. IEEE Access, 13:119305-119334, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695. This article has 47 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt contrasts two main representation choices for CAD generation: command-sequence (sketch-and-extrude style) pipelines and direct B-Rep synthesis, and it also highlights B-Rep/graph encodings (e.g., UV-grid or face-adjacency graphs). Command-sequence approaches commonly target a small operation set ("Sketch and Extrude"), which limits model expressiveness and can produce invalid topologies for complex, long sequences (DeepCAD noted as an example). Direct B-Rep synthesis (examples: SolidGen, CADParser) avoids command-sequence constraints and is presented as a promising alternative. For representation of B-Rep data, methods like UV-Net use UV-grid sampling but suffer from permutation-invariance issues; graph-based self-supervision (UV-Net using GCL) and investigating invariances are recommended. Dataset issues: SketchGraphs (sketches) and ABC (3D models) are both from Onshape but are not paired, limiting paired sketch→B-Rep training; Fusion 360 segmentation is used frequently but often on varying subsets, complicating comparisons. The field also faces a scarcity of large annotated benchmarks and practical reproducibility barriers due to reliance on proprietary CAD kernels (e.g., Parasolid) versus more accessible OpenCASCADE.

From text 'wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 9-10'.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses representation choices for learning-based CAD generation primarily in terms of B-Rep (solid geometry) and procedural/command-sequence or parametric procedural representations. It notes that standard CAD formats like B-Reps or command sequences are not directly suitable for common neural architectures and require transformation into AI-friendly formats. B-Reps carry rich geometric/topological detail but present learning challenges due to the diversity of face and edge types. Procedural/command-sequence representations and parametric procedural formats are more structured for stepwise generation, but many available sequence-based datasets concentrate on short sequences (<20 steps), limiting model exposure to complex workflows; WHUCAD is noted for including longer sequences (>30 steps). Dataset coverage and scale vary: SketchGraphs (15M sketches) and ABC (1M+ models) are very large; DeepCAD (178k) and Fusion 360 Gallery (8.6k) are smaller; CADParser augmentation yields ~40k models; image-aligned datasets like CADDreamer produced 29k train /1k test samples, and Text2CAD produced ~600k train /32k validation. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss CSG or sketch-and-extrude representations in detail.

## pqac-00000020

From text 'lin2025asurveyon pages 9-11'.

Ruiquan Lin, Yunwei Ji, Wanting Ding, Tianxiang Wu, Yaosheng Zhu, and Mengxi Jiang. A survey on deep learning in 3d cad reconstruction. Applied Sciences, 15:6681, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/app15126681, doi:10.3390/app15126681. This article has 9 citations.

The excerpt discusses multiple CAD representation choices relevant to neural-network and LLM-driven CAD generation. Sequence (operation) representations record geometric operations in execution order and form instruction sequences (example: DeepCAD) suitable for learning construction histories and parametric/model-generation tasks. Procedural modeling and the macro-parameter method (MPA) are related approaches emphasizing rule/scripted generation and cross-platform semantic consistency. Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) builds models by Boolean operations on primitives, is represented as an expression/tree of Boolean primitives and ops (difference being important), and captures construction logic rather than explicit boundaries; it is concise and flexible but requires Boolean evaluation to produce final geometry. Boundary representation (B-rep) records vertices, edges, faces and topological relationships, emphasizing detailed surface geometry and high-precision modeling. Sketch representations include vector graphics (precise geometric elements) and graph-structured sketches (nodes = elements, edges = constraints), which map naturally to GNNs (e.g., SketchGraphs). The excerpt also references dataset resources and a table summarizing datasets' attributes; it explicitly names DeepCAD and SketchGraphs and mentions D2CSG for learning compact CSG expressions. The excerpt does not provide detailed entries for ABC or Fusion 360 Gallery within these pages.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt surveys CAD representation choices relevant to LLM-assisted CAD generation. It highlights three procedural/sequential options: constructive solid geometry (CSG) and command-sequence / DSL-style procedural modeling, 2D sketch-based workflows (sketches composed of primitives and geometric constraints) often paired with "Sketch-and-Extrude" operations, and direct geometric/B-Rep representations. Sketches capture design intent via geometric constraints and can be turned into 3D solids by extrusion, revolution, or sweeping; several studies adopt Sketch-and-Extrude. B-Rep is identified as the industry-standard CAD representation, encoding topology (faces, edges, vertices) plus parametric surface/curve definitions, making it suitable for precise CAD. Alternative pure-geometry choices include point clouds (which lack topology and need conversion), meshes (widely used with many algorithms but are discrete approximations requiring high precision and high computational cost for CAD-grade accuracy), implicit/SDF representations (level-set formulations), and subdivision surfaces (smooth but lacking simple analytical limit-surface expressions, limiting use in precision CAD). The excerpt does not provide information about datasets like DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, or SketchGraphs.

From text 'zhang2026largelanguagemodels pages 14-16'.

Licheng Zhang, Bach Le, Naveed Akhtar, Siew-Kei Lam, and Duc Ngo. Large language models for computer-aided design: a survey. ACM Computing Surveys, 58:1-39, Feb 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3787499, doi:10.1145/3787499. This article has 19 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights recurring geometric and spatial-reasoning failure modes and associated mitigation strategies in LLM/VLM CAD work. It explicitly notes “well-known spatial reasoning limitations of VLMs,” and describes common runtime failure modes: generated CAD code can be non-executable or produce objects with low similarity to ground truth, triggering iterative refinement loops (“If the code is not executable, refinement is performed until it is.”; “generated objects’ computing similarity to the ground truth is evaluated.”). Several systems implement automated verification and feedback cycles (e.g., question-answer based evaluation and human-free feedback) and rely on feeding execution errors back into LLMs for correction. Other challenges include mapping raw geometric data into parametric or sketch-extrude representations (“map input point clouds into CAD sketch-extrude sequences represented as Python code.”), building multimodal representations (rendering B-rep from multiple views), and obtaining/curating high-quality training data (filters on model-generated data; BlendNet fine-tuning). The text frames CAD code generation as a central application but emphasizes the need for robust executability, verification, spatial reasoning improvements, multimodal integration, and better datasets/benchmarks to address current failure modes.

## pqac-00000025

From text 'zhang2026largelanguagemodels pages 16-18'.

Licheng Zhang, Bach Le, Naveed Akhtar, Siew-Kei Lam, and Duc Ngo. Large language models for computer-aided design: a survey. ACM Computing Surveys, 58:1-39, Feb 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3787499, doi:10.1145/3787499. This article has 19 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several geometric and spatial reasoning failure modes and related challenges for LLM-based CAD. A key failure mode is limited numeric and parametric precision: "LLMs still face difficulties in producing perfectly accurate parametric sequences," which motivates approaches that accept partial (keys/values) rather than fully executable sequences. Spatial reasoning is a recognized difficulty; one work "mapped the 3D space into 1D using a tokenization method to improve spatial reasoning," indicating the need for specialized representations. Generalization is weak: models "struggled to generalize to new shapes and primitives," affecting tasks like semantic commenting and program-to-shape mapping. Other practical challenges include producing end-to-end CAD drawings ("This is the only work we currently find that generates CAD drawings from an LLM in an end-to-end manner"), creating reliable evaluation and preference data (visual feedback and scoring pipelines that "rewarded parametric sequences that rendered visually preferred objects"), and representing CAD construction order and commands (models using JSON to capture "key modeling commands and parameters in the order of CAD construction"). Finally, the survey notes LLMs' utility for evaluation ("LLMs can also be used to compare two sources of data"), implying open problems in robust benchmarking, multimodal conditioning, and bridging discrete/global structure prediction with accurate continuous attribute estimation.

## pqac-00000026

From text 'zhang2026largelanguagemodels pages 18-20'.

Licheng Zhang, Bach Le, Naveed Akhtar, Siew-Kei Lam, and Duc Ngo. Large language models for computer-aided design: a survey. ACM Computing Surveys, 58:1-39, Feb 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3787499, doi:10.1145/3787499. This article has 19 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt identifies several concrete failure modes and challenges for LLMs in CAD. Specific geometric/spatial failures include limited spatial reasoning (models show "some spatial reasoning capability" but "it was still limited when compared to human performance") and poor generalization (models "struggled to generalize to new shapes and primitives"). Practically, most methods avoid directly producing CAD artifacts: "nearly all approaches utilize LLMs to generate intermediate representations rather than directly outputting 3D CAD models or 2D CAD drawings," since "directly generating accurate 3D or 2D CAD outputs remains a significant challenge for current models." Other key challenges/open problems highlighted are heavy reliance on multimodal inputs ("most state-of-the-art methods rely on multimodal inputs" with "text and images" most common), the prominence but closed-source dependence on certain LLM families, dataset variability (industry vs synthetic), and the need for hybrid reasoning frameworks (e.g., combining RBR, CBR, LLMs, and knowledge graphs). Major application focus is on CAD code generation and text-generation tasks, indicating opportunities to improve direct 2D/3D output, spatial/geometric reasoning, generalization to novel primitives, multimodal alignment, and dataset coverage.
