## pqac-00000027

From text 'man2505videocadalargescale pages 21-24'.

Brandon Man, Ghadi Nehme, Md Ferdous Alam, and Faez Ahmed. Videocad: a dataset and model for learning long-horizon 3d cad ui interactions from video. ArXiv, May 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.24838, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.24838. This article has 8 citations.

The excerpt documents concrete geometric and spatial reasoning failure modes that are likely relevant to LLM-based CAD systems: inaccurate (x,y) coordinate predictions during sketching that create open loops and block extrusion; closed loops with small geometric errors that lead to subtly deformed extrusions; and misclassification between primitives (e.g., line vs. arc), especially when curvature is ambiguous. These failures stem from the difficulty of resolving fine-grained geometric distinctions from image-only supervision. Key system-level challenges highlighted include the need for strong temporal conditioning and memory of past actions/states, appropriate architectural depth, and better cross-domain generalization (models perform worse on photorealistic renders vs. sketches). Proposed directions to address these problems include incorporating topological constraints, interactive fine-tuning or feedback, and richer supervision/evaluation (e.g., Chamfer-distance alignment and VQA-style probes that test geometric recognition, viewpoint awareness, forward simulation, temporal planning, and numeric inference). Open problems suggested by the excerpt are improving fine-grained geometric/topological reasoning, handling ambiguous primitives, closing the gap to photoreal inputs, and integrating interactive or topological priors into learning-based CAD agents.

## pqac-00000028

From text 'sadik2509humanintheloopquantitativeevaluation pages 1-2'.

Ahmed R. Sadik and M. Bujny. Human-in-the-loop: quantitative evaluation of 3d models generation by large language models. ArXiv, Sep 2509. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2509.07010, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2509.07010. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt stresses that practical adoption of LLM-generated CAD is limited by the "absence of rigorous evaluation protocols and structured feedback mechanisms." It proposes a human-in-the-loop pipeline that "integrates generation, evaluation, and iterative feedback into a structured pipeline." To quantify failures and guide refinement, the authors introduce a suite of metrics including "volumetric accuracy, surface alignment, dimensional fidelity, and topological intricacy." They highlight concrete failure modes to watch for: poor "dimensional accuracy, structural consistency, and geometric integrity," as well as misaligned surfaces and topological errors. The case study shows generation quality depends strongly on input semantic richness: "semantic richness and structural clarity in input prompts, with symbolic CAD instructions achieving the highest fidelity," and even that "code-level prompts achieving perfect reconstruction across all metrics." Key challenges and open problems implied by the text are establishing rigorous evaluation protocols and structured feedback loops, improving robustness across input modalities (2D views, sketches, semantic descriptions), and developing scalable quantitative metrics and workflows for iteratively refining LLM outputs toward engineering-grade CAD.

The feedback from the geometric solver is a numerical value across thirteen categories of geometric
information. An example of this feedback, FGS, is shown in Appendix (see Figure 4).This feedback
method serves as an upper limit for CAD code refinement, as it conveys the precise geometric
differences between the generated 3D object and the ground truth.

We evaluate the performance of each baseline in both zero-shot and few-shot settings. In the few-
shot setting, we provide five demonstration examples, with each example consisting of a 3D object
description followed by the corresponding code. We use the OpenAI for GPT-4, Replicate API for
CodeLlama, and Google’s GenerativeAI API for Gemini 1.5 Pro respectively.3


5.2  EVALUATION METRICS

This paper aims to evaluate how accurately VLMs generate and refine 3D objects through CAD code.
To evaluate the quality of the generated 3D object compared to the ground truth, we utilize three
evaluation metrics: (i) Point Cloud distance, (ii) Hausdorff distance, and (iii) Intersection over the
Ground Truth (IoGT). We apply the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm to rotate and translate
the generated 3D object to choose the best possible alignment with the ground truth object (Besl &
McKay, 1992). Finally, we normalize each point cloud such that it fits within a unit cube, as per prior
work (Zheng et al., 2023). In some cases, the generated CAD code does not compile, which prevents
the rendering of a valid 3D object. We report the percentage of successfully compiled code as the
“success rate.” The formulas for the evaluation metrics are described as follows:

Intersection over the Ground Truth (IoGT):  This metric used to evaluate how well the generated
3D object P aligns with the ground truth Q. As shown in Equation 10, it calculates the area of
intersection between P and Q to the area of Q.

Point Cloud distance and Hausdorff distance are based on the geometric properties (e.g., height, width,
and volume) of 3D objects, while the IoGT measures the overlap of the bounding boxes between the
generated and ground truth 3D objects. When computing these distances, we assign the maximum                      √
distance of two points within a unit cube, i.e.,   3, and an IoGT value of zero to any data point where
the code fails to compile. This approach maximally penalizes unsuccessful 3D object generations,
ensuring consistent and fair evaluation criteria across all the experiments.

refinement methods. With regards to “success-rate,” GPT-4 exhibits superior performance 96.5%
on the CADPrompt benchmark compared to Gemini 85% and CodeLlama 73.5%. While prior
work has provided valuable qualitative insights into GPT-4’s competency to producing compile-able

In addition to the three core metrics used for comparison with Text2CAD Transformer, we introduce
two supplementary metrics, F1 Score and Volumetric IoU, to further evaluate the geometric fidelity
of generated shapes. Both metrics offer complementary perspectives: F1 captures local geometric
alignment, while IoU assesses global volumetric overlap. Below, we describe each in detail.


                                       8F1 Score: We adopt a point-based F1 score to evaluate the alignment between predicted and ground-
truth meshes. For each shape, we sample 10,000 surface points and compute the bidirectional recall
and precision using a fixed distance threshold τ = 0.02 in normalized space. A predicted point is
considered correct if its nearest neighbor in the ground-truth set lies within the threshold, and vice
versa. The F1 score is then calculated as:
                                  2 · Precision · Recall
                         F1 =                                                         (3)
                                          Precision + Recall
This metric is sensitive to local geometric detail and rewards both coverage and accuracy. The
threshold τ = 0.02 is chosen to balance tolerance and precision under unit-normalized shape scales.

Volumetric IoU: To further assess 3D shape similarity, we compute the volumetric Intersection over
Union (IoU) between voxelized versions of the predicted and ground-truth meshes [47]. Both meshes
are first normalized to fit within the unit cube [0, 1]3, then voxelized using a resolution of 0.02. Let
V1 and V2 denote the occupied voxel grids. IoU is computed as:
                                               |V1 ∩V2|
                               IoU =                                                  (4)
                                               |V1 ∪V2|
This metric captures global shape overlap and is particularly useful for assessing coarse structural
fidelity. Padding is applied to align voxel grid sizes when necessary.

As shown in Figure 4, Qwen2.5-3B achieves the best performance across both F1 and IoU metrics,
with an F1 median of 0.9836 and a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868. Even our smallest model,
CodeGPT-small, attains reasonable performance (F1 median 0.9688), confirming the effectiveness
of fine-tuning on symbolic CAD tasks. To the best of our knowledge, these scores significantly
outperform existing methods across all input modalities. This holds across multiple metrics, such as
CD, F1 Score, and IoU, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance for CAD generation.

Both metrics exhibit clear scaling trends: as model size increases, accuracy improves steadily from
124M to 3B. Similar to the previous observations, the 7B model (Mistral) does not further improve
over Qwen2.5-3B, likely due to underfitting from limited data. This again highlights the tradeoff
between model capacity and data availability in symbolic 3D generation.

5  Limitation

This work focuses on the output format for generative CAD, showing that CadQuery outperforms task-
specific command sequences. In principle, our approach can be extended to other input modalities
such as images, sketches, and point clouds, by using CadQuery as the output.  It would also be
valuable to annotate additional datasets with CadQuery to test whether this improvement holds across
input types. Another limitation is data scale. Although our dataset includes 170,000 samples, we
observe a performance drop on the 7B model, likely due to underfitting. Scaling up the dataset and
fine-tuning even larger models such as 13B or 32B could further test this hypothesis, but would
require significant annotation effort and computational resources.

6  Conclusion

We introduce a large-scale dataset of 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and propose Text-to-CadQuery
as a new paradigm for CAD generation. Our method directly generates executable CadQuery code
from natural language, replacing task-specific command sequences used in prior work. This change
simplifies the modeling pipeline and improves performance across multiple geometric and model-
based evaluation metrics. Moreover, by adopting CadQuery as the output, we enable the use of
pretrained language models rather than training models from scratch. This leads to faster training and
better generalization. More importantly, our approach can naturally benefit from future advances in
foundation models, such as improved multimodal understanding, spatial reasoning, or code generation,
without modifying the training framework or output design. Our experiments demonstrate that even
the smallest model fine-tuned on Text-to-CadQuery data outperform the Text2CAD baseline. As
model size increases, we observe a clear scaling trend in accuracy and executability. Our best model
achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple metrics. These findings validate both the output
choice and our overall framework, and open new directions for extending CadQuery-based generation
to diverse inputs and larger models.

Fig. 2: Early vs. late assembly relationship prediction. Top: early prediction (ours)
specifies connectors at design time; assembly reduces to deterministic frame alignment.
Bottom: deferring connection decisions to assembly stage forces a second planning
pass that must parse all generated code, infer coordinate systems, and resolve cross-
part dimensions—a task with long context and high failure rate.


stage. Similar to following a blueprint rather than blindly fitting puzzle pieces
together, early prediction sets up a connection plan before any 3D shapes are
generated. By explicitly defining these connections in advance, we separate the
connection planning from the shape generation. This avoids the LLM’s spatial
reasoning limits during the final assembly.

    Specifically, our multi-agent system has four agents. A Design Agent re-
ceives user input (text, image, or both) and outputs a structured plan containing
part specifications and assembly relationships, which we call connectors. Each
connector defines a named attachment point carrying a local coordinate frame, a
semantic label, and a joint type with motion limits. Next, Generation Agents
produce each part independently as a FreeCAD Python script [13] based on
the Design Agent’s plan. Then, an Assembly Agent builds the final model by
aligning matched connector pairs using FreeCAD’s constraint solver. Because
the connectors were already defined by the Design Agent, this step is strictly
mathematical and does not require a VLM or LLM. Finally, a Review Agent
evaluates a generated CAD assembly by inspecting multi-view renders of each
part and the keyframe sequences of the assembly’s joint motions.

  We further improve the generation quality from two aspects. First, we in-
troduce validation steps within both Generation and Assembly Agents, accom-
panied by a cross-stage rollback mechanism. Using error feedback derived from
the validation step in each agent, this mechanism classifies the error as either a
DESIGN-level or CODE-level failure. It then re-invokes only the responsible agent,
thereby reducing redundant effort. Second, a self-evolving experience store ac-
cumulates design knowledge derived from the Review Agent into a partitioned
vector database after each task. This allows relevant knowledge to be retrieved
and referenced in subsequent tasks. Our contributions are summarized as follows:

– A Multi-Agent Framework for Articulated CAD: We propose ArtiCAD,
  the  first training-free multi-agent system to generate editable, articulated4      Y. Shui et al.


 CAD assemblies from text and images. This fills the gap between single-part
 CAD generation and non-editable 3D reconstruction.
– Design-Stage Assembly Planning: We propose predicting assembly rela-
  tionships using the Connector during the design rather than assembly stage,
  bypassing the spatial reasoning limitations of current LLMs/VLMs.
– Cross-Stage Feedback and Self-Evolution: To improve generation qual-
   ity, we introduce validation steps within the generation and assembly stages,
  accompanied by a cross-stage rollback mechanism. We also propose a self-
  evolving experience store that accumulates design knowledge for future tasks.
– New Benchmark and Downstream Applications: We introduce ArtiCAD-
  Bench, a 120-task benchmark, and conduct cross-domain evaluations on CAD-
  Prompt [1] and ACD [19]. In practice, ArtiCAD supports requirement-driven
  conceptual design and generates CAD assemblies that are directly exportable
  as URDFs for robotic simulation.


2  Related Work

2.1  Parametric CAD Generation

Early methods model discrete sketch-and-extrude sequences, with generative ap-
proaches like DeepCAD [53] (autoregressive modeling), SkexGen [57] (disentan-
gled codebooks), Text2CAD [24] (prompt-conditioned), CAD-GPT [48] (3D po-
sitional tokens), and CAD-Llama [30] (fine-tuning). Reconstruction counterparts
include TransCAD [9] and CAD-SIGNet [23]. To overcome the geometric limita-
tions of fixed vocabularies, recent methods synthesize executable programmatic
scripts (e.g., CadQuery). CAD-Recode [45] and Text-to-CadQuery [55] gener-
ate scripts from point clouds and text. CAD-Coder [17] introduces geometric
rewards, while CADCrafter [6] leverages latent diffusion. Advanced alignments
are achieved via reinforcement learning in Cadrille [25] or sequential fine-tuning
in CADmium [16].
   To mitigate execution errors, several frameworks wrap LLMs in generate–
execute–repair loops, utilizing sandbox traces (CADCodeVerify [1]), visual feed-
back (Seek-CAD [31]), evolutionary search (EvoCAD [43]), or VLM-guided edits
(CADEvolve [10]). Crucially, all these methods are restricted to producing single,
static parts without multi-part hierarchies or joints.


2.2 CAD Datasets and Assembly

Common CAD datasets provide single-part histories (DeepCAD [53], Fusion
360 Gallery [51], WHUCAD [11]) or multimodal annotations (Omni-CAD [56],
CADInstruct [38]). While assembly datasets exist (e.g., Fusion 360 and Auto-
Mate [22]), works utilizing them primarily focus on joint-axis prediction (Join-
ABLe [50]), mate-type classification (AutoMate), or next-part recommenda-
tion [32] for pre-existing components. Even CADKnitter [27], which generates a
complementary part under geometric constraints, assumes a given base model.                                                    ArtiCAD      5


To our knowledge, ArtiCAD is the first framework to synthesize complete, multi-
part articulated CAD assemblies with typed degrees of freedom from scratch
using high-level specifications.


2.3  Articulated Object Generation and Reconstruction

A separate body of work targets 3D articulated objects. Datasets like Part-
Net [39] and PartNet-Mobility [54] provide fine-grained segmentations and joint
annotations. For generation, methods learn diffusion priors (NAP [28]), use
graph-conditioned diffusion (SINGAPO [34]), leverage VLM agents to articulate
existing meshes (Articulate-Anything [26]), or synthesize geometry and articula-
tion via compact 3D/latent tokens (PhysX-Anything [5], PAct [35]). For recon-
struction, approaches build digital twins from interactions or multi-view images
(Ditto [20], PARIS [33]), recover objects via Gaussian splatting (ArtGS [37],
GaussianArt [46]), or convert static meshes to openable ones (S2O [19]).
    Critically, these methods produce non-editable mesh or Gaussian representa-
tions and generalize poorly beyond their training categories (pri

of iteratively designing the model and the prompts promptplan and promptcode are available in Listings 2 and 3,
respectively.

3.4  Verification

To cope with limitations of VLMs with regards to spatial reasoning and help in the process of translating the spec-
ification into code, we make use of a verification loop. This verification loop equates to the verification phase in
human development cycles, where the model is compared to the initial specification to ensure that the design ful-
fills all requirements. Accordingly, the QualityAssuranceEngineer takes as an input the model created by the
CadEngineer and creates various views of the model. The default is a set of seven images, taken from the top, bottom,
right, left, front, back, and an isometric one. The QualityAssuranceEngineer then compares these views of the
model with the specification and checks if the specification is met. In case there are deviations from the specification,
the QualityAssuranceEngineer provides a list of textual suggestions on what should be changed. Algorithm 3
summarizes the verification loop and the prompt promptqa is available in Listing 5.


3.5  Validation

Despite the initial interactive requirements specification, cp. Section 3.2, the specification may be incorrect or the
system may be unable to converge towards the desired model. To cope, we introduce an outer validation loop, analogous
to the validation in development processes [Verein Deutscher Ingenieure, 2021]. Here, the user is asked to confirm the
model created, and asked for specific feedback in case they are not satisfied. This human feedback is used to regenerate
the model, cp. Algorithm 4.

Algorithm 3: VLM-based verification.
Data: specification R, user feedback Fval
Result: feedbackverification Fver, verified model M
Fver ←∅;
while ⊤do
 M ←design(R, Fval + Fver) ;                                         /* Algorithm 2 */
   views ←render(model);
   Fver ←vlm.prompt(promptqa, R, views);
     if Fver = ∅then
       break;
   end
end


Algorithm 4: Human validation.
Data: specification R
Result: validated model M
Fval ←∅;
while ⊤do
 M ←design(R, Fval) ;                                                /* Algorithm 3 */
   output(M);
   Fval ←user.input();
     if Fval = ∅then
       break;
   end
end


4  Implementation, Experiments & Discussion

4.1  Implementation

We implemented a prototype in Python, relying on several well-established packages. For code-based parametric model
design, we used the Python library CadQuery [Au et al., 2024], which comes with detailed documentation3 that can be
fed into a VLM. For checking the code generated before executing it, we relied on Python’s ast module. CadQuery’s
documentation is retrieved from the web using Python’s request package in combination with BeautifulSoup44.
We render stl files within the QualityAssuranceEngineer leveraging pyvista5. As VLMs, we rely on OpenAI’s
gpt-4o-2024-11-20 as a default, interacting with the API via OpenAI’s Python package6. Note that error handling
for the VLM API was crucial for robust execution.


4.2  Experiments

We conducted experiments with visual inputs of varying complexity, both for hand-drawn sketches and photos of actual
objects. Here, the number of geometrical features to be realized serves as an indication for complexity. As hand-drawn
sketches, we included a simple rectangular block, a rectangular block with two holes, and a strongly simplified sketch
of a toy car. We also included photos, one of a plastic cap and one of an angle bracket. Figure 3 gives an overview of
these inputs on the left, and Table 1 shows the textual parts of the specifications.

To assess the benefits of the individual agents in the system, we performed ablations using the selected designs. The
second column “0-shot VLM” shows the performance of the CadEngineer by itself, without interactive requirement
specification, verification, and validation. This setup serves as a baseline, as it relies on the same prompt and utilizes
the planning step before the code generation. The designs in the third column, i.e., the first column of the “CAD team
iterations” correspond to the designs created by the CadEngineer together with the RequirementsEngineer, but

4.3  Discussion

Overall, the results of the VLM-based MAS are promising, especially when compared to the single shot generative
approach. By utilizing specialized agents that mimic typical engineering roles and a self-feedback loop in the
architecture, our method generates designs with a higher level of readiness and compliance to the requirements.
Nonetheless, there is significant variability in the results and making such VLM-based systems with self-feedback
robust is still challenging for several reasons: In line with limitations regarding spatial reasoning, VLMs still seem
to struggle especially with the orientation of surfaces, e.g., when selecting suitable workplanes for sketches. Also,
the MAS failed to generate more complex components. Note that simply increasing the number of iterations may
be insufficient, as this would pollute the context w

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive reference table with approximately 16 key references from the evidence gathered. Each row should contain: Reference ID (numbered), Authors/Year, Venue/ArXiv ID, Focus Area (e.g., code-based CAD, multi-agent, B-rep generation, benchmark, survey, spatial reasoning), and Key Quantitative Result if available. Include: 1) Barkley et al. 2025 CADSmith (multi-agent CadQuery, 100% exec, 38x CD reduction), 2) Alrashedy et al. 2025 CADCodeVerify (visual feedback CadQuery, GPT-4 96.5% success), 3) Doris et al. 2025 CAD-Coder VLM (100% VSR, IoU_best), 4) Xie & Ju 2025 Text-to-CadQuery (69.3% exact match), 5) Chen et al. 2025 CADCrafter (DPO, Med CD 0.026, IR 3.6%), 6) Tsuji et al. 2025 GenCAD-Self-Repairing (feasibility 93.1%\u219297.0%), 7) Fan et al. 2025 CADDesigner (ECIP paradigm), 8) Ocker et al. 2025 From Idea to CAD (multi-agent CadQuery), 9) Shui et al. 2026 ArtiCAD (multi-agent articulated assembly), 10) Chen et al. 2025 CADReview/ReCAD (error detection OpenSCAD, GPT-4o only 41.5% acc), 11) Sadik & Bujny 2025 HITL evaluation (CadQuery metrics), 12) Zhang et al. 2026 LLMs for CAD Survey (ACM Computing Surveys), 13) Wu et al. 2026 AI-driven CAD Survey (Computational Visual Media), 14) Mouselinos et al. 2024 Geometric Reasoning Gap, 15) Kilpel\u00e4inen 2025 Gen AI for Robotics 3D Modeling (CadQuery/OpenSCAD thesis), 16) K\u00e4ns\u00e4l\u00e4 2026 Gen AI CAD/Manufacturing (multi-agent OpenSCAD thesis)."}

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Ref. | Authors / Year | Venue / ArXiv ID | Focus Area | Key Quantitative Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Barkley, Loghmani, Farimani (2026) | arXiv:2603.26512 | Multi-agent CadQuery generation with programmatic geometric validation | 100% execution success; mean Chamfer Distance reduced from 28.37 to 0.74; median F1 0.9707→0.9846; median IoU 0.8085→0.9629; ~38× CD reduction (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000011) |
| 2 | Alrashedy, Tambwekar, Zaidi, Langwasser, Xu, Gombolay (2025) | arXiv:2410.05340 | Visual-feedback CadQuery verification / critic-revisor loop | On CADPrompt, GPT-4 + CADCodeVerify reached 96.5% success rate in few-shot; point-cloud distance improved from 0.155 to 0.127; IoGT to 0.944 (pqac-00000030) |
| 3 | Doris, Alam, Nobari, Ahmed (2026) | arXiv:2505.14646 | Vision-language CadQuery code generation | Reports 100% valid syntax rate (VSR); evaluates geometry with IOU_best on 100 samples; near-perfect example IoU_best = 0.963 (pqac-00000032) |
| 4 | Xie, Ju (2025) | arXiv:2505.06507 | Text-to-CadQuery symbolic CAD code generation | Best model achieved 69.30% top-1 exact match; F1 median 0.9836; volumetric IoU median 0.9868; Mistral variant invalid rate 1.32%; GPT-4.1 mini invalid rate 0% (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000006) |
| 5 | Chen et al. (2025) | CVPR / arXiv:2504.04753 | Image-to-parametric CAD with DPO and code checker feedback | On DeepCAD multi-view: Acc_cmd 84.62, Acc_para 73.31, Med CD 0.026, Invalid Rate 0.036; on RealCAD multi-view: Med CD 0.062, IR 0.067 (pqac-00000029) |
| 6 | Tsuji, Medina, Gupta, Alam (2025) | arXiv:2505.23287 | Self-repairing feasibility enhancement for CAD generation | Feasibility improved from 0.931 to 0.970; fixed 65.84% of baseline infeasible cases (532/808); mean MMD changed 0.180→0.200 (pqac-00000033) |
| 7 | Fan et al. (2025) | arXiv:2508.01031 | Agent-based CAD generation; ECIP coding paradigm | Primarily architectural contribution: ECIP replaces implicit CadQuery context with explicit state passing and structured error triples for better debugging; no headline benchmark number in extracted evidence (pqac-00000035) |
| 8 | Ocker, Menzel, Sadik, Rios (2025) | arXiv:2503.04417 | Multi-agent CadQuery workflow with requirement clarification, verification, and human validation | Qualitative ablation shows agent team outperforms 0-shot VLM baseline; uses 7-view QA loop and user validation; no consolidated scalar benchmark extracted (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000037) |
| 9 | Shui et al. (2026) | arXiv:2604.10992 | Multi-agent articulated CAD assembly / FreeCAD Python | Introduces ArtiCAD-Bench with 120 tasks; key idea is design-stage connector planning to bypass LLM spatial-reasoning failure in late assembly; quantitative benchmark details not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| 10 | Chen et al. (2025) | arXiv:2505.22304 | OpenSCAD program review, error detection, and correction | GPT-4o feedback accuracy only 41.54% on human-made and 31.84% on machine-made programs; invalid code rate for closed-source MLLMs exceeds 25%; ReCAD outperforms by >30% on feedback accuracy (pqac-00000034) |
| 11 | Sadik, Bujny (2025) | arXiv:2509.07010 | Human-in-the-loop quantitative evaluation of LLM 3D/CAD outputs | Uses volumetric similarity, surface similarity, Hausdorff, PCA, ICP, and final score; code-level correction prompts reportedly achieve perfect or near-perfect reconstruction, including zero Hausdorff distance in cited case (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |
| 12 | Zhang, Le, Akhtar, Lam, Ngo (2026) | ACM Computing Surveys, DOI:10.1145/3787499 | Survey of LLMs for CAD | Survey identifies limited spatial reasoning, difficulty producing perfectly accurate parametric sequences, weak generalization to new shapes/primitives, and reliance on intermediate representations rather than direct CAD output (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026) |
| 13 | Wu, Wu, Xing, Li, Fu, Chen, Liu (2026) | Computational Visual Media, DOI:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521 | Survey of AI-driven 3D CAD generation | Survey catalogs representation tradeoffs and mitigation directions: validity/watertightness checks, feedback-driven repair, simulation-in-the-loop, DSLs, and dataset limitations; includes dataset sizes such as SketchGraphs 15M, ABC 1M+, DeepCAD 178,238, Fusion 360 Gallery 8,625 (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023) |
| 14 | Mouselinos, Michalewski, Malinowski (2024) | arXiv:2402.03877 | Geometric/spatial reasoning limitations of LLMs | Finds notable geometric reasoning gap: LLMs struggle with 2D spatial relationships, hallucinate/misplace objects, and benefit from multi-agent internal dialogue; extracted evidence does not include a single headline CAD metric (pqac-00000024) |
| 15 | Kilpeläinen (2025) | Thesis / unpublished venue in retrieved evidence | CadQuery/OpenSCAD for robotics; practitioner-focused evaluation of generative CAD | Reports recurring failures: omitted boreholes/fillets, wrong primitive shapes, invalid operation sequences, and degraded validity on longer sequences; no consolidated benchmark number extracted (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| 16 | Känsälä (2026) | Thesis / unpublished venue in retrieved evidence | Generative AI systems for CAD/manufacturing; multi-agent OpenSCAD | Documents prompt sensitivity, hallucinated OpenSCAD functions, weak multimodal fidelity, and premature convergence in refinement loops; recommends richer RAG, stronger programmatic validation, and manufacturability checks; no stable headline scalar benchmark extracted (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000012) |

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a mitigation strategy matrix table organized by the four mitigation axes (A through D). Each row should be a specific mitigation strategy with columns: Mitigation Axis, Strategy, Representative Reference(s), How it Works (1-2 sentences), and Reported Effectiveness.

Axis A (Tool-Side Guardrails):
- OCCT kernel topology checks: CADSmith uses bounding-box, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, solid validity checks (100% exec success)
- DPO with code-checker feedback: CADCrafter uses automatic code checker feedback + DPO to enforce geometric validity (IR 3.6%)
- Self-repair diffusion pipeline: GenCAD-Self-Repairing uses diffusion guidance and regression to fix infeasible B-reps (fixes 65.84% infeasible)
- Watertightness/manifold checks: Wu et al. survey notes many AI-generated models contain self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges

Axis B (Prompting/Agent Strategies):
- Multi-agent with VLM judge: CADSmith Planner/Coder/Executor/Validator/Refiner + independent VLM Judge (38x CD improvement)
- Visual feedback VQA loop: CADCodeVerify generates and answers validation questions from renders (5% success rate improvement)
- Multi-agent role-based design: Ocker et al. RequirementsEngineer/CadEngineer/QualityAssuranceEngineer (iterative improvement)
- Design-stage connector planning: ArtiCAD predicts assembly relationships before generation to bypass spatial reasoning limits
- Structured error feedback: CADDesigner ECIP provides error triples (cause, location, corrective action)
- RL fine-tuning: cadrille uses GRPO for online RL on CAD tasks

Axis C (Representation Choices):
- CadQuery/Python code: Text-to-CadQuery, CAD-Coder show code-based representation outperforms command sequences
- Explicit context imperative paradigm (ECIP): CADDesigner replaces implicit CadQuery chaining with explicit state passing
- B-rep direct generation: GraphBrep, HiDiGen diffusion approaches
- Datasets: DeepCAD 178K, ABC 1M+, Fusion 360 Gallery 8.6K, SketchGraphs 15M

Axis D (Human-in-the-Loop):
- Human validation loop: Ocker et al. outer validation loop with user feedback after verification
- Quantitative HITL evaluation: Sadik & Bujny metrics pipeline (volumetric, surface, Hausdorff) with iterative correction to convergence
- Review agent + user: ArtiCAD review agent evaluates renders of each part + joint motions
- Code-level correction: Sadik & Bujny show code-level prompts achieve near-perfect reconstruction"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Mitigation Axis | Strategy | Representative Reference(s) | How it Works (1-2 sentences) | Reported Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | OCCT kernel topology checks | CADSmith (Barkley et al., 2026) (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000011) | The pipeline checks executable CadQuery/OCCT outputs against deterministic geometry facts: bounding-box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity, then uses these signals in an outer repair loop. This catches millimeter-scale dimensional and topology errors that image-only judging can miss. | 100% execution success; median F1 improved from 0.9707 to 0.9846; median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629; mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74 (pqac-00000001) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | DPO with code-checker feedback | CADCrafter (Chen et al., 2025) (pqac-00000029) | CADCrafter fine-tunes its generative model using automatic CAD code checker feedback as a preference signal, so geometrically invalid or non-compiling sequences are penalized during training. This injects non-differentiable CAD validity signals into learning. | On DeepCAD multi-view, Invalid Rate = 0.036 and Median Chamfer Distance = 0.026; on RealCAD multi-view, IR = 0.067 and Med CD = 0.062 (pqac-00000029) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | Self-repair diffusion pipeline | GenCAD-Self-Repairing (Tsuji et al., 2025) (pqac-00000033) | Infeasible command sequences are identified by failed B-rep construction, then repaired by latent-space diffusion guidance plus a regressor that maps infeasible latent vectors toward feasible ones. The corrected latent is decoded again to improve geometry feasibility. | Feasibility improved from 0.931 to 0.970; repaired 65.84% of baseline infeasible cases (532/808) (pqac-00000033) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | Watertightness / manifold validity checks | AI-driven CAD survey (Wu et al., 2026); CADEvolve discussion (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000004) | Surveyed work emphasizes explicit checks for self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges, non-watertight solids, and degenerate outputs before downstream manufacturing, slicing, or FEA. These checks are proposed as basic admissibility filters for generated CAD. | Survey identifies non-watertight and non-manifold outputs as a major blocker for manufacturing/FEA; CADEvolve treats invalid generations as a first-class error category in training/evaluation (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000004) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Multi-agent with independent VLM judge | CADSmith (Barkley et al., 2026) (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000000) | CADSmith decomposes work across Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, and Refiner agents, with an independent vision-language Judge that sees code, OCCT metrics, and three-view renders. This critic-revisor architecture reduces self-confirmation bias and supports iterative repair. | Full pipeline produced 100% execution success and ~38× lower mean Chamfer Distance than zero-shot; removing renders increased T3 mean CD from 1.42 to 49.68 (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000008) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Visual feedback VQA loop | CADCodeVerify (Alrashedy et al., 2025) (pqac-00000030) | A VLM generates validation questions about rendered geometry, answers them from the image, and uses the resulting feedback to revise CAD code. This creates a visual chain-of-thought / critic loop anchored in rendered shape rather than text alone. | GPT-4 few-shot success rate improved from 96.0% to 96.5%; point-cloud distance improved from 0.155 to 0.127; IoGT rose to 0.944 (pqac-00000030) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Multi-agent role-based design | From Idea to CAD (Ocker et al., 2025) (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000037) | The system uses specialized RequirementsEngineer, CadEngineer, and QualityAssuranceEngineer roles, plus a verification loop over seven rendered views and an outer human validation loop. The decomposition mirrors real engineering workflows and helps localize errors. | Reported qualitatively to outperform a 0-shot VLM baseline across example tasks; authors note higher readiness/compliance but also persistent failures on orientation/workplane selection for complex parts (pqac-00000037) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Design-stage connector planning for assemblies | ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) (pqac-00000036) | Instead of asking the model to infer assembly relationships after generating all part code, ArtiCAD predicts connectors, local frames, semantic labels, and joint limits up front. Final assembly then reduces to deterministic alignment with a constraint solver, bypassing long-context spatial reasoning at assembly time. | Introduces a 120-task benchmark and explicitly argues early connector prediction avoids the high failure rate of late assembly inference; quantitative benchmark details were not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Structured error feedback | CADDesigner / ECIP (Fan et al., 2025) (pqac-00000035) | ECIP replaces implicit CadQuery state with explicit state passing and emits structured error triples: (cause, location, corrective action). This gives the model machine-readable debugging feedback instead of vague runtime traces. | Reported as improving modularity, readability, and ease of debugging for agent-driven CAD generation; no scalar benchmark extracted from the cited pages (pqac-00000035) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | RL fine-tuning with online feedback | cadrille; ReACT survey context (pqac-00000016) | Recent CAD generation systems treat CAD sequence prediction as a sequential decision problem and optimize with reward signals tied to geometric fidelity and validity, rather than imitation alone. This is intended to improve long-horizon geometric decisions. | Survey identifies RL-based CAD generation as a key emerging direction; extracted pages note state-of-the-art gains but do not provide exact cadrille numbers in the available evidence (pqac-00000016) |
| C — Representation Choices | CadQuery / Python code instead of task-specific command tokens | Text-to-CadQuery; CAD-Coder (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032) | Direct Python/CadQuery generation leverages pretrained code-generation ability and exposes procedural logic, assertions, and debugging affordances unavailable in compact task-specific tokenizations. It also aligns with existing software stacks and RAG over API docs. | Text-to-CadQuery reached 69.30% top-1 exact match, F1 median 0.9836, IoU median 0.9868; CAD-Coder reports 100% valid syntax rate (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032) |
| C — Representation Choices | Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) | CADDesigner (Fan et al., 2025) (pqac-00000035) | ECIP replaces chained, implicitly stateful CadQuery calls with explicit input/output passing for every operation. That makes dependencies visible, permits normal Python control flow, and simplifies both debugging and code review. | Reported to improve code clarity, flexibility, modularity, and interpretability for LLM agents; benchmark numbers were not extracted in the cited pages (pqac-00000035) |
| C — Representation Choices | Direct B-rep generation with explicit topology | GraphBrep / HiDiGen; representation surveys (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016) | B-rep generation models operate on explicit faces, edges, vertices, and topology graphs rather than only sketch-extrude command histories. This can avoid fixed-vocabulary command bottlenecks but raises topology-learning complexity. | Surveys describe direct B-rep synthesis as a promising alternative to restricted sketch-extrude sequences, especially for complex geometry/topology; no single effectiveness number was extracted for GraphBrep/HiDiGen from the available pages (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016) |
| C — Representation Choices | Dataset/benchmark choice | DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Representation and evaluation quality are bounded by dataset coverage: DeepCAD provides command sequences, ABC provides large-scale B-reps, Fusion 360 Gallery provides human-authored build histories, and SketchGraphs provides constrained 2D sketches. Choosing the dataset determines whether the model learns code, topology, constraints, or edit history. | Reported sizes: DeepCAD 178,238; ABC 1M+; Fusion 360 Gallery 8,625 (or ~8.6K); SketchGraphs 15M (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Human validation loop after automated verification | From Idea to CAD (Ocker et al., 2025) (pqac-00000037) | After a VLM-based QA loop, the user is shown the candidate model and can provide specific corrective feedback; the system then regenerates the model using that feedback. This inserts a human checkpoint after automated geometric critique. | Demonstrated iterative improvements on examples such as wheel orientation/width corrections; authors position this as necessary because the system may fail to converge autonomously (pqac-00000037) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Quantitative HITL evaluation pipeline | Sadik & Bujny (2025) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) | The human-in-the-loop process evaluates outputs with volumetric similarity, surface similarity, Hausdorff distance, PCA alignment, ICP alignment, and a weighted final score, then iteratively revises prompts/code until convergence. This turns manual review into a measurable optimization loop. | Code-level correction prompts achieved perfect or near-perfect metrics in the cited case, including zero Hausdorff distance; paper argues structured metrics are necessary for engineering-grade review (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Review agent + user review of assemblies | ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) (pqac-00000036) | ArtiCAD’s Review Agent inspects multi-view renders of each part plus keyframe sequences of articulated joint motions, then feeds findings back into responsible upstream agents via rollback. A user can then judge the resulting assembly. | Presented as a practical safeguard for multi-part articulated assemblies; quantitative review-stage gains were not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Code-level correction as strongest supervision | Sadik & Bujny (2025) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) | Their experiments compare low-information inputs (orthographic views, sketches) to higher-information symbolic/code-level prompts, showing that direct access to parametric/source-level information drastically lowers edit distance and ambiguity. This suggests manual edits should feed back into the parametric source, not only into rendered geometry. | Code-level prompts achieved near-perfect reconstruction across metrics and outperformed image-only inputs; cited case reports zero Hausdorff distance (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |


*Table: This table organizes mitigations for LLM-generated mechanical CAD across tool-side guardrails, agent/prompting strategies, representation choices, and human-in-the-loop workflows. It highlights how each mitigation works and, where available, the reported empirical gains.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00, artifact-01]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037]

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Find recent (2023-2026) peer-reviewed and arXiv academic publications, engineering reports, and developer write-ups on the spatial reasoning, assembly, and geometric-correctness limitations of large language models (LLMs) and agentic systems when generating mechanical CAD — specifically code-based CAD (CadQuery, OpenSCAD, build123d, FreeCAD Python API), B-rep / feature-tree generation, and LLM-driven parametric assembly. Cover the following failure modes that have been empirically observed in Claude Opus 4.7 / GPT-class models generating CadQuery code: (i) parts modelled in isolation but never Boolean-unioned with the host body (e.g., mounting bosses floating off a ring, brackets not attached); (ii) wrong-direction Boolean cuts and extrudes (e.g., CadQuery `workplane(invert=True)`, `extrude(both=True)` doubling thickness, `cut`/`hole` going the wrong way); (iii) clearance / fit / tolerance values that are arithmetically inconsistent (e.g., N modules on a pitch circle that geometrically overlap, made-up clearances that don't match operating envelope); (iv) missing functional paths (e.g., no inlet for powder/fluid, motor blocks the part it drives, no fastener access); (v) vendor-part envelopes ignored or misremembered; (vi) fastener strategy underspecified (self-tap into FDM print vs. heat-set inserts vs. through-bolts). For each source give: full citation (authors, year, venue, DOI/arXiv ID), 3-5 sentence summary, and the concrete mitigation strategy it proposes or evaluates. Specifically catalogue mitigations across these axes: (A) tool-side guardrails — schema-constrained decoding, deterministic geometry-kernel verifiers (OCCT/CadQuery topology checks, watertight/manifold checks, interference / clash detection, DRC-style design rule checks for AM, FEA-in-the-loop, slicer-side printability checks); (B) prompting / agent strategies — self-consistency, self-repair / critic-revisor loops, ReAct-style tool-use with a CAD kernel, multi-view rendering fed back into the model (visual chain-of-thought / image-augmented critique), test-driven CAD (write assertions on volume, mass, bounding box, hole counts, interference-free assemblies); (C) representation choices — sketch + constraint-based vs. CSG vs. mesh, decomposition into sub-assemblies, explicit interface contracts between parts, parametric skeletons / datums, vendor-component libraries with verified envelopes (KiCad-style footprint libs but for mechanical); (D) human-in-the-loop and manual-intervention patterns — where to insert a human reviewer, what review artifacts (multi-view renders, dimensioned 2D sketches, exploded views, animated assembly), how to make manual edits feed back into the parametric source, and the cost/benefit of fully autonomous vs. supervised generation for hardware that will be 3D printed and assembled. Include landmark / canonical references (CAD-LLM, Text2CAD, DeepCAD, SkexGen, Fusion 360 Gallery, ABC dataset, SketchGraphs, CADCrafter, BlenderBench, related agentic-design benchmarks like BlocksWorld / spatial-reasoning evals) and quote any reported quantitative results (pass rate, watertightness rate, manufacturability rate, human edit distance). Aim for ~12-18 references.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

## 1. CAD-Llama: Leveraging Large Language Models for Computer-Aided Design Parametric 3D Model Generation

@article{li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge,
    author = "Li, Jiahao and Ma, Weijian and Li, Xueyang and Lou, Yunzhong and Zhou, G. and Zhou, Xiangdong",
    title = "CAD-Llama: Leveraging Large Language Models for Computer-Aided Design Parametric 3D Model Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "2025 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)",
    pages = "18563-18573",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.04481",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.04481"
}


Abstract:
Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved significant success, prompting increased interest in expanding their generative capabilities beyond general text into domain-specific areas. This study investigates the generation of parametric sequences for computer-aided design (CAD) models using LLMs. This endeavor represents an initial step towards creating parametric 3D shapes with LLMs, as CAD model parameters directly correlate with shapes in three-dimensional space. Despite the formidable generative capacities of LLMs, this task remains challenging, as these models neither encounter parametric sequences during their pretraining phase nor possess direct awareness of 3D structures. To address this, we present CAD-Llama, a framework designed to enhance pretrained LLMs for generating parametric 3D CAD models. Specifically, we develop a hierarchical annotation pipeline and a code-like format to translate parametric 3D CAD command sequences into Structured Parametric CAD Code (SPCC), incorporating hierarchical semantic descriptions. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive pretraining approach utilizing SPCC, followed by an instruction tuning process aligned with CAD-specific guidelines. This methodology aims to equip LLMs with the spatial knowledge inherent in parametric sequences. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms prior autoregressive methods and existing LLM baselines.

## 2. Automated CAD Modeling Sequence Generation from Text Descriptions via Transformer-Based Large Language Models
Valid Text Names:

    title = "Automated CAD Modeling Sequence Generation from Text Descriptions via Transformer-Based Large Language Models",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.19490",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490"
}


Abstract:
Designing complex computer-aided design (CAD) models is often time-consuming due to challenges such as computational inefficiency and the difficulty of generating precise models. We propose a novel language-guided framework for industrial design automation to address these issues, integrating large language models (LLMs) with computer-automated design (CAutoD).Through this framework, CAD models are automatically generated from parameters and appearance descriptions, supporting the automation of design tasks during the detailed CAD design phase. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) a semi-automated data annotation pipeline that leverages LLMs and vision-language large models (VLLMs) to generate high-quality parameters and appearance descriptions; (2) a Transformer-based CAD generator (TCADGen) that predicts modeling sequences via dual-channel feature aggregation; (3) an enhanced CAD modeling generation model, called CADLLM, that is designed to refine the generated sequences by incorporating the confidence scores from TCADGen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms traditional methods in both accuracy and efficiency, providing a powerful tool for automating industrial workflows and generating complex CAD models from textual prompts. The code is available at https://jianxliao.github.io/cadllm-page/
