messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000008

From text 'barkley2603cadsmithmultiagentcad pages 6-7'.

Jesse Barkley, Rumi Loghmani, and A. Farimani. Cadsmith: multi-agent cad generation with programmatic geometric validation. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes mitigation strategies used in CADSmith to address LLM-driven CAD failures. Key elements are a multi-agent pipeline (Validator/Judge and Refiner agents) that implements a self-repair iterative loop: models generate CadQuery code, the Validator receives the prompt, code, OpenCASCADE (OCCT) kernel metrics, and a three-view rendered image, then cross-references code logic, kernel measurements, and visuals to detect mismatches and produce structured feedback for the Refiner. Programmatic geometric validation uses kernel-level checks (OCCT metrics, voxelized IoU, Chamfer) to drive corrections; the system formats feedback as {"passed": bool, "feedback": "..."} and includes escalation rules to recommend fundamentally different construction approaches if repeated fixes fail. Ablations show visual feedback is critical—removing the three-view render dramatically degrades complex-part performance—because kernel metrics alone can miss false convergence or subtle gaps that make parts unmanufacturable. The full pipeline yields large execution and fidelity gains versus zero-shot, demonstrating that combined multi-agent control, vision-in-the-loop, and programmatic kernel validation form an effective self-repair strategy for text-to-CAD generation.


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'ocker2503fromideato pages 3-5'.

Felix Ocker, Stefan Menzel, Ahmed Sadik, and Thiago Rios. From idea to cad: a language model-driven multi-agent system for collaborative design. ArXiv, Mar 2503. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2503.04417, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2503.04417. This article has 24 citations.

The excerpt documents several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. It proposes a VLM-based multi-agent system with distinct roles (RequirementsEngineer, CadEngineer, QualityAssuranceEngineer) to iteratively clarify requirements, generate parametric CAD code, and verify results. Interactive clarification loops and reflexion (internal/external feedback) are used to resolve ambiguities. Prior work explored visual feedback loops (four-view visual feedback), though one study reported that visual feedback did not improve CAD-code generation. Iterative/self-repair strategies appear in frameworks that generate Python parametric CAD code and improve prompts over time based on VLM feedback; debugging and executability checks are explicitly used (LLM4CAD uses a debugger; generated code is checked for executability and VLMs are prompted to fix errors). Safeguards include requiring human confirmation before executing generated code and CadEngineer checks of documentation for potential misuse, plus QA-agent feedback loops to trigger regeneration. The excerpt does not mention DPO/RL fine-tuning, kernel-level checks, explicit watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD methodologies.


---

## pqac-00000010

From text 'kansala2026generativeaidrivensystems pages 59-62'.

L Känsälä. Generative ai-driven systems supporting computer-aided design and manufacturing. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt recommends multiple mitigations for LLM-driven CAD failures. Multi-agent architectures are valuable because they “improve relational reasoning, manufacturability assessment, and failure detection” and “provided richer diagnostic output, including explicit geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, and parameter verification,” though the refinement loop can “frequently converge prematurely” and is probabilistic. Strengthening the RAG subsystem is advised: populate ChromaDB, expand robust OpenSCAD libraries for common parts, and “incorporat[e] RAG into the manufacturing engineer agent” so decisions are grounded in documentation. Model-level improvements include “Fine-tuned LLMs trained on structured CAD datasets, parametric scripts, and engineering documentation” and replacing base64 image handling with “true vision language models that process image directly” to capture finer geometry. Procedural mitigations include “more structured refinement routines, clearer feedback mechanisms and optimized agent prompts” to improve convergence and richer programmatic checks (explicit geometric validation and manufacturability assessment). For evaluation and test-driven validation, the paper suggests improving metrics beyond voxel IoU with “higher-resolution voxelization, surface-distance metrics and a more diverse set of reference models.” These recommendations target improved feedback loops, grounded retrieval, higher-fidelity vision, and programmatic geometric validation.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'barkley2603cadsmithmultiagentcad pages 1-2'.

Jesse Barkley, Rumi Loghmani, and A. Farimani. Cadsmith: multi-agent cad generation with programmatic geometric validation. ArXiv, Mar 2603. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2603.26512. This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt describes CADSmith's mitigation strategies for LLM CAD generation failures. Key strategies include a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, Refiner) and an iterative, closed-loop refinement process with two nested correction loops: an inner loop to resolve execution errors and an outer loop for programmatic geometric validation. The outer loop uses OpenCASCADE kernel-level checks — bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity — combined with an independent vision-language Judge that performs three-view rendered inspection to provide holistic visual feedback and avoid self-confirmation bias. The system uses retrieval-augmented generation over API documentation rather than fine-tuning to keep models aligned with evolving CAD libraries. Prior related systems (e.g., Query2CAD) used visual feedback (BLIP-2) for iterative refinement but lacked the kernel-precision available here. The paper frames these mechanisms (multi-agent decomposition, execution-error self-repair, kernel-level verification, independent VLM judging, and RAG) as complementary safeguards to reduce hallucinated dimensions, API misuse, and shape/dimensional mismatches.

## pqac-00000012

From text 'kansala2026generativeaidrivensystemsa pages 59-62'.

L Känsälä. Generative ai-driven systems supporting computer-aided design and manufacturing. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt recommends several practical mitigations for LLM-driven CAD failures. Multi-agent designs with explicit critic and manufacturing stages can increase detection and correction of errors and provide richer diagnostics (explicit geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, parameter verification), but require improved feedback structure and more structured refinement routines to avoid premature convergence. Retrieval-augmented-generation should be strengthened by populating ChromaDB with CAD documentation, parametric examples and design rules and by building robust OpenSCAD libraries for common parts; RAG should also be integrated into the manufacturing engineer agent so decisions are grounded in technical documentation. Model-level mitigations include fine-tuning LLMs on structured CAD datasets and parametric scripts to improve constraint reasoning and reduce prompt sensitivity, and replacing the current base64 image pipeline with true vision-language models to extract higher-fidelity geometry from sketches. Evaluation and programmatic validation should be improved beyond coarse IoU: use higher-resolution voxelization, surface-distance metrics, and more diverse benchmarks to better detect geometric and manufacturability failures. The text does not explicitly mention DPO/RL methods, kernel-level checks, watertightness-specific routines, or explicit test-driven CAD workflows by name, though the advocated programmatic validation, diagnostics, and refinement loops support similar objectives.

URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt proposes several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. It highlights LLM-centered multi-agent architectures that act as coordinating hubs managing domain-specific agents (inspired by engineering workflows like the V-model) to separate requirements, modeling, and QA responsibilities. Vision-language models (VLMs) coupled with CAD-specific tool suites (e.g., CAD-Assistant) enable iterative, closed-loop visual feedback: the VLM interprets sketches or multimodal queries, issues CAD commands, observes the evolving design state, and adjusts future actions. Programmatic geometric validation is emphasized via multiple LLM-based strategies to ensure geometric and semantic correctness, and explicit AI-based error detection/correction pipelines are noted (e.g., CADReview’s ReCAD repairer). Integration of physical simulation and test signals (e.g., PhysicsX) supplies early validation and optimization, reducing downstream testing burden. Structured program representations (DSLs) and learned generative models (VAE/GRU) for shape assembly also improve correctness by constraining outputs. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss DPO/RL fine-tuning, kernel-level CAD checks, watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD methodologies, so those mitigation techniques are not covered here.

## pqac-00000014

From text 'wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 30-32'.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt suggests several mitigation directions for CAD-generation failures. Primary proposals include automated detection and correction of design errors via trained models with real-time feedback during the design process, and incorporation of physical simulation data early to reduce testing time and improve accuracy. The text highlights kernel-level interoperability issues (proprietary CAD kernels and naming/parameter handling) and suggests exchanging via standard formats and reconstruction as a current workaround, though this complicates learning/tasks. Fundamental model-validity checks are emphasized: many AI-generated models contain geometric/topological flaws (self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges) that make them non-watertight and unusable for downstream manufacturing or FEA, indicating the need for programmatic validity/watertightness verification. The authors note the importance of embedding geometric constraints into generation (currently only partially addressed: sketch-level constraints or traditional solvers) to enable rule-based editing and parametric updates. They also call out poor support for iterative, user-driven refinement. Finally, the excerpt points to dataset limitations as a barrier to more complex, robust model generation and repair pipelines.

## pqac-00000015

From text 'wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 27-28'.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. Key approaches include synthesizing symbolic CAD programs (DSLs or parametric scripts) that are executed by CAD kernels, which improves traceability and allows programmatic validation. Iterative, feedback-driven pipelines are emphasized: systems analyze rendered outputs (e.g., via VQA) and re-prompt LLMs when validation scores fall below thresholds, enabling self-repair and multi-turn refinement. VLM-based visual inspection (CADCodeVerify) asks geometric-attribute questions (e.g., “is the hole centered?”) and returns corrective feedback to resolve specification violations. Multi-agent / tool-based architectures are proposed where LLMs coordinate specialized modules (perception, planning, verification), mirroring engineering workflows and enabling division-of-labor for verification and refinement. Fine-tuning strategies are noted (e.g., fine-tuning GPT-3.5 on prompt-program pairs) to improve fidelity. The excerpt explicitly mentions compilation/execution via off-the-shelf CAD kernels but does not explicitly discuss DPO/RL training, dedicated kernel-level automated checks, watertightness verification, or explicit test-driven CAD frameworks—though the described validation loops and programmatic execution imply opportunities to integrate such checks.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Cancer remains one of the most challenging diseases to diagnose and treat, requiring the development of innovative therapeutic and diagnostic strategies. Cancer progression includes enzyme dysregulation, which orchestrates key biological processes ranging from metabolic reprogramming, epigenetic modifications to drug metabolism and immune evasion. Enzymes drive and modulate their roles in the tumor microenvironment, influencing how cancer cells adapt to stress, resist treatments, and evade immune surveillance. This review paper examines recent advances in enzyme engineering and its potential in cancer treatment. Computational tools, including artificial intelligence, have contributed significantly to enzyme optimization, enabling improvements in catalytic efficiency, stability, and specificity through structural modeling, functional annotation and generative design. Enzyme engineering can be optimized for targeted therapies, minimizing off-target effects while maximizing therapeutic potential. Challenges such as enzyme stability, delivery mechanisms, and immunogenicity persist, but recent advances offer promising solutions. Therefore, we also review the integration of computational approaches and experimental advances in enzyme engineering, thereby offering insights into future directions for optimizing enzyme engineering strategies in cancer treatment and diagnosis. In essence, this review provides an up-to-date synthesis linking the fundamental biological understanding of enzymes in cancer with the rapidly evolving field of enzyme engineering and the powerful capabilities offered by computational tools, highlighting both promising advances and remaining hurdles such as benchmarking and interpretability of machine learning offered solutions.</jats:p>

Abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) have strong capabilities in solving diverse natural language processing tasks. However, the safety and security issues of LLM systems have become the major obstacle to their widespread application. Many studies have extensively investigated risks in LLM systems and developed the corresponding mitigation strategies. Leading-edge enterprises such as OpenAI, Google, Meta, and Anthropic have also made lots of efforts on responsible LLMs. Therefore, there is a growing need to organize the existing studies and establish comprehensive taxonomies for the community. In this paper, we delve into four essential modules of an LLM system, including an input module for receiving prompts, a language model trained on extensive corpora, a toolchain module for development and deployment, and an output module for exporting LLM-generated content. Based on this, we propose a comprehensive taxonomy, which systematically analyzes potential risks associated with each module of an LLM system and discusses the corresponding mitigation strategies. Furthermore, we review prevalent benchmarks, aiming to facilitate the risk assessment of LLM systems. We hope that this paper can help LLM participants embrace a systematic perspective to build their responsible LLM systems.

## pqac-00000016

From text 'wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 6-7'.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt contrasts representation choices for CAD generation: CSG and CAD command sequences (cmdseq) versus code-based domain-specific languages (DSLs) and sketch-based workflows (e.g., sketch + extrusion). It notes that common operations such as sketch and extrusion cover many industrial cases but are insufficient for lofts, sweeps, shells, and complex multi-body assemblies, causing coverage gaps where many shapes cannot be well expressed. DSLs are highlighted as more flexible: "Parameters, geometric constraints, and procedural logic can all be explicitly encoded", making them compatible with LLMs and modern AI pipelines. However, DSL research is limited by the lack of large standardized databases and unified syntax. The CAD interpreter converts commands/DSL code into geometric operations and enforces constraints; constraints are split into dimensional and geometric types (distances/angles/radii vs. parallelism/tangency/symmetry) and are solved by numerical, graph-based, decomposition-based, or learning-augmented methods. Representative datasets listed include SketchGraph (15M sketches, sketch+constraint, graph, real), ABC (1M+, B-Rep + Mesh, real), Fusion 360 Gallery (8,625, cmdseq, B-Rep + Mesh, real), and DeepCAD (178,238, cmdseq, B-Rep, real).

## pqac-00000017

From text 'heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 22-24'.

Negar Heidari and Alexandros Iosifidis. Geometric deep learning for computer-aided design: a survey. IEEE Access, 13:119305-119334, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695. This article has 47 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt contrasts two main representation choices for CAD generation: command-sequence (sketch-and-extrude style) pipelines and direct B-Rep synthesis, and it also highlights B-Rep/graph encodings (e.g., UV-grid or face-adjacency graphs). Command-sequence approaches commonly target a small operation set ("Sketch and Extrude"), which limits model expressiveness and can produce invalid topologies for complex, long sequences (DeepCAD noted as an example). Direct B-Rep synthesis (examples: SolidGen, CADParser) avoids command-sequence constraints and is presented as a promising alternative. For representation of B-Rep data, methods like UV-Net use UV-grid sampling but suffer from permutation-invariance issues; graph-based self-supervision (UV-Net using GCL) and investigating invariances are recommended. Dataset issues: SketchGraphs (sketches) and ABC (3D models) are both from Onshape but are not paired, limiting paired sketch→B-Rep training; Fusion 360 segmentation is used frequently but often on varying subsets, complicating comparisons. The field also faces a scarcity of large annotated benchmarks and practical reproducibility barriers due to reliance on proprietary CAD kernels (e.g., Parasolid) versus more accessible OpenCASCADE.

The excerpt discusses representation choices for learning-based CAD generation primarily in terms of B-Rep (solid geometry) and procedural/command-sequence or parametric procedural representations. It notes that standard CAD formats like B-Reps or command sequences are not directly suitable for common neural architectures and require transformation into AI-friendly formats. B-Reps carry rich geometric/topological detail but present learning challenges due to the diversity of face and edge types. Procedural/command-sequence representations and parametric procedural formats are more structured for stepwise generation, but many available sequence-based datasets concentrate on short sequences (<20 steps), limiting model exposure to complex workflows; WHUCAD is noted for including longer sequences (>30 steps). Dataset coverage and scale vary: SketchGraphs (15M sketches) and ABC (1M+ models) are very large; DeepCAD (178k) and Fusion 360 Gallery (8.6k) are smaller; CADParser augmentation yields ~40k models; image-aligned datasets like CADDreamer produced 29k train /1k test samples, and Text2CAD produced ~600k train /32k validation. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss CSG or sketch-and-extrude representations in detail.

## pqac-00000020

From text 'lin2025asurveyon pages 9-11'.

Ruiquan Lin, Yunwei Ji, Wanting Ding, Tianxiang Wu, Yaosheng Zhu, and Mengxi Jiang. A survey on deep learning in 3d cad reconstruction. Applied Sciences, 15:6681, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/app15126681, doi:10.3390/app15126681. This article has 9 citations.

The excerpt discusses multiple CAD representation choices relevant to neural-network and LLM-driven CAD generation. Sequence (operation) representations record geometric operations in execution order and form instruction sequences (example: DeepCAD) suitable for learning construction histories and parametric/model-generation tasks. Procedural modeling and the macro-parameter method (MPA) are related approaches emphasizing rule/scripted generation and cross-platform semantic consistency. Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) builds models by Boolean operations on primitives, is represented as an expression/tree of Boolean primitives and ops (difference being important), and captures construction logic rather than explicit boundaries; it is concise and flexible but requires Boolean evaluation to produce final geometry. Boundary representation (B-rep) records vertices, edges, faces and topological relationships, emphasizing detailed surface geometry and high-precision modeling. Sketch representations include vector graphics (precise geometric elements) and graph-structured sketches (nodes = elements, edges = constraints), which map naturally to GNNs (e.g., SketchGraphs). The excerpt also references dataset resources and a table summarizing datasets' attributes; it explicitly names DeepCAD and SketchGraphs and mentions D2CSG for learning compact CSG expressions. The excerpt does not provide detailed entries for ABC or Fusion 360 Gallery within these pages.

Wenzheng Wu, Kang Wu, Siyuan Xing, Yifei Li, Xiao-Ming Fu, Falai Chen, and Ligang Liu. Ai-driven generation of 3d cad models: a survey. Computational Visual Media, pages 1-40, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521, doi:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521. This article has 0 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt surveys CAD representation choices relevant to LLM-assisted CAD generation. It highlights three procedural/sequential options: constructive solid geometry (CSG) and command-sequence / DSL-style procedural modeling, 2D sketch-based workflows (sketches composed of primitives and geometric constraints) often paired with "Sketch-and-Extrude" operations, and direct geometric/B-Rep representations. Sketches capture design intent via geometric constraints and can be turned into 3D solids by extrusion, revolution, or sweeping; several studies adopt Sketch-and-Extrude. B-Rep is identified as the industry-standard CAD representation, encoding topology (faces, edges, vertices) plus parametric surface/curve definitions, making it suitable for precise CAD. Alternative pure-geometry choices include point clouds (which lack topology and need conversion), meshes (widely used with many algorithms but are discrete approximations requiring high precision and high computational cost for CAD-grade accuracy), implicit/SDF representations (level-set formulations), and subdivision surfaces (smooth but lacking simple analytical limit-surface expressions, limiting use in precision CAD). The excerpt does not provide information about datasets like DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, or SketchGraphs.

The excerpt highlights recurring geometric and spatial-reasoning failure modes and associated mitigation strategies in LLM/VLM CAD work. It explicitly notes “well-known spatial reasoning limitations of VLMs,” and describes common runtime failure modes: generated CAD code can be non-executable or produce objects with low similarity to ground truth, triggering iterative refinement loops (“If the code is not executable, refinement is performed until it is.”; “generated objects’ computing similarity to the ground truth is evaluated.”). Several systems implement automated verification and feedback cycles (e.g., question-answer based evaluation and human-free feedback) and rely on feeding execution errors back into LLMs for correction. Other challenges include mapping raw geometric data into parametric or sketch-extrude representations (“map input point clouds into CAD sketch-extrude sequences represented as Python code.”), building multimodal representations (rendering B-rep from multiple views), and obtaining/curating high-quality training data (filters on model-generated data; BlendNet fine-tuning). The text frames CAD code generation as a central application but emphasizes the need for robust executability, verification, spatial reasoning improvements, multimodal integration, and better datasets/benchmarks to address current failure modes.

## pqac-00000025

From text 'zhang2026largelanguagemodels pages 16-18'.

Licheng Zhang, Bach Le, Naveed Akhtar, Siew-Kei Lam, and Duc Ngo. Large language models for computer-aided design: a survey. ACM Computing Surveys, 58:1-39, Feb 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3787499, doi:10.1145/3787499. This article has 19 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights several geometric and spatial reasoning failure modes and related challenges for LLM-based CAD. A key failure mode is limited numeric and parametric precision: "LLMs still face difficulties in producing perfectly accurate parametric sequences," which motivates approaches that accept partial (keys/values) rather than fully executable sequences. Spatial reasoning is a recognized difficulty; one work "mapped the 3D space into 1D using a tokenization method to improve spatial reasoning," indicating the need for specialized representations. Generalization is weak: models "struggled to generalize to new shapes and primitives," affecting tasks like semantic commenting and program-to-shape mapping. Other practical challenges include producing end-to-end CAD drawings ("This is the only work we currently find that generates CAD drawings from an LLM in an end-to-end manner"), creating reliable evaluation and preference data (visual feedback and scoring pipelines that "rewarded parametric sequences that rendered visually preferred objects"), and representing CAD construction order and commands (models using JSON to capture "key modeling commands and parameters in the order of CAD construction"). Finally, the survey notes LLMs' utility for evaluation ("LLMs can also be used to compare two sources of data"), implying open problems in robust benchmarking, multimodal conditioning, and bridging discrete/global structure prediction with accurate continuous attribute estimation.

The excerpt identifies several concrete failure modes and challenges for LLMs in CAD. Specific geometric/spatial failures include limited spatial reasoning (models show "some spatial reasoning capability" but "it was still limited when compared to human performance") and poor generalization (models "struggled to generalize to new shapes and primitives"). Practically, most methods avoid directly producing CAD artifacts: "nearly all approaches utilize LLMs to generate intermediate representations rather than directly outputting 3D CAD models or 2D CAD drawings," since "directly generating accurate 3D or 2D CAD outputs remains a significant challenge for current models." Other key challenges/open problems highlighted are heavy reliance on multimodal inputs ("most state-of-the-art methods rely on multimodal inputs" with "text and images" most common), the prominence but closed-source dependence on certain LLM families, dataset variability (industry vs synthetic), and the need for hybrid reasoning frameworks (e.g., combining RBR, CBR, LLMs, and knowledge graphs). Major application focus is on CAD code generation and text-generation tasks, indicating opportunities to improve direct 2D/3D output, spatial/geometric reasoning, generalization to novel primitives, multimodal alignment, and dataset coverage.

## pqac-00000027

From text 'man2505videocadalargescale pages 21-24'.

Brandon Man, Ghadi Nehme, Md Ferdous Alam, and Faez Ahmed. Videocad: a dataset and model for learning long-horizon 3d cad ui interactions from video. ArXiv, May 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.24838, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.24838. This article has 8 citations.

The excerpt documents concrete geometric and spatial reasoning failure modes that are likely relevant to LLM-based CAD systems: inaccurate (x,y) coordinate predictions during sketching that create open loops and block extrusion; closed loops with small geometric errors that lead to subtly deformed extrusions; and misclassification between primitives (e.g., line vs. arc), especially when curvature is ambiguous. These failures stem from the difficulty of resolving fine-grained geometric distinctions from image-only supervision. Key system-level challenges highlighted include the need for strong temporal conditioning and memory of past actions/states, appropriate architectural depth, and better cross-domain generalization (models perform worse on photorealistic renders vs. sketches). Proposed directions to address these problems include incorporating topological constraints, interactive fine-tuning or feedback, and richer supervision/evaluation (e.g., Chamfer-distance alignment and VQA-style probes that test geometric recognition, viewpoint awareness, forward simulation, temporal planning, and numeric inference). Open problems suggested by the excerpt are improving fine-grained geometric/topological reasoning, handling ambiguous primitives, closing the gap to photoreal inputs, and integrating interactive or topological priors into learning-based CAD agents.

The excerpt stresses that practical adoption of LLM-generated CAD is limited by the "absence of rigorous evaluation protocols and structured feedback mechanisms." It proposes a human-in-the-loop pipeline that "integrates generation, evaluation, and iterative feedback into a structured pipeline." To quantify failures and guide refinement, the authors introduce a suite of metrics including "volumetric accuracy, surface alignment, dimensional fidelity, and topological intricacy." They highlight concrete failure modes to watch for: poor "dimensional accuracy, structural consistency, and geometric integrity," as well as misaligned surfaces and topological errors. The case study shows generation quality depends strongly on input semantic richness: "semantic richness and structural clarity in input prompts, with symbolic CAD instructions achieving the highest fidelity," and even that "code-level prompts achieving perfect reconstruction across all metrics." Key challenges and open problems implied by the text are establishing rigorous evaluation protocols and structured feedback loops, improving robustness across input modalities (2D views, sketches, semantic descriptions), and developing scalable quantitative metrics and workflows for iteratively refining LLM outputs toward engineering-grade CAD.

The feedback from the geometric solver is a numerical value across thirteen categories of geometric
information. An example of this feedback, FGS, is shown in Appendix (see Figure 4).This feedback
method serves as an upper limit for CAD code refinement, as it conveys the precise geometric
differences between the generated 3D object and the ground truth.

We evaluate the performance of each baseline in both zero-shot and few-shot settings. In the few-
shot setting, we provide five demonstration examples, with each example consisting of a 3D object
description followed by the corresponding code. We use the OpenAI for GPT-4, Replicate API for
CodeLlama, and Google’s GenerativeAI API for Gemini 1.5 Pro respectively.3


5.2  EVALUATION METRICS

This paper aims to evaluate how accurately VLMs generate and refine 3D objects through CAD code.
To evaluate the quality of the generated 3D object compared to the ground truth, we utilize three
evaluation metrics: (i) Point Cloud distance, (ii) Hausdorff distance, and (iii) Intersection over the
Ground Truth (IoGT). We apply the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm to rotate and translate
the generated 3D object to choose the best possible alignment with the ground truth object (Besl &
McKay, 1992). Finally, we normalize each point cloud such that it fits within a unit cube, as per prior
work (Zheng et al., 2023). In some cases, the generated CAD code does not compile, which prevents
the rendering of a valid 3D object. We report the percentage of successfully compiled code as the
“success rate.” The formulas for the evaluation metrics are described as follows:

6  EXPERIMENT RESULTS

We utilize CADPrompt to quantitatively evaluate the capabilities of CADCodeVerify approach across
various VLMs. The following are our key findings:

GPT-4 exhibits the highest capacity to generate compile-able code on CADPrompt. We report
our primary results in Table 2, comparing the distance and success-rate for three LLMs and three
refinement methods. With regards to “success-rate,” GPT-4 exhibits superior performance 96.5%
on the CADPrompt benchmark compared to Gemini 85% and CodeLlama 73.5%. While prior
work has provided valuable qualitative insights into GPT-4’s competency to producing compile-able

Interestingly, the 7B Mistral model underperforms the 3B Qwen2.5 model across accuracy metrics,
reaching performance levels similar to Gemma-3-1B. We attribute this to insufficient training data,
which limits the large model’s ability to fully learn the task-specific generation patterns. However,
the Mistral model achieves the lowest Invalid Rate (1.32), suggesting that larger pretrained models
are better at generating syntactically correct and executable Python code. To further verify this, we
fine-tuned a commercial model, GPT-4.1 mini, on a 10k subset of our data. This model achieved a
0% Invalid Rate—producing fully executable code—but its accuracy matched that of the 7B model,
confirming that advanced models are more reliable in execution but still require sufficient data to
learn precise 3D generation.

In addition to the three core metrics used for comparison with Text2CAD Transformer, we introduce
two supplementary metrics, F1 Score and Volumetric IoU, to further evaluate the geometric fidelity
of generated shapes. Both metrics offer complementary perspectives: F1 captures local geometric
alignment, while IoU assesses global volumetric overlap. Below, we describe each in detail.


                                       8F1 Score: We adopt a point-based F1 score to evaluate the alignment between predicted and ground-
truth meshes. For each shape, we sample 10,000 surface points and compute the bidirectional recall
and precision using a fixed distance threshold τ = 0.02 in normalized space. A predicted point is
considered correct if its nearest neighbor in the ground-truth set lies within the threshold, and vice
versa. The F1 score is then calculated as:
                                  2 · Precision · Recall
                         F1 =                                                         (3)
                                          Precision + Recall
This metric is sensitive to local geometric detail and rewards both coverage and accuracy. The
threshold τ = 0.02 is chosen to balance tolerance and precision under unit-normalized shape scales.

Volumetric IoU: To further assess 3D shape similarity, we compute the volumetric Intersection over
Union (IoU) between voxelized versions of the predicted and ground-truth meshes [47]. Both meshes
are first normalized to fit within the unit cube [0, 1]3, then voxelized using a resolution of 0.02. Let
V1 and V2 denote the occupied voxel grids. IoU is computed as:
                                               |V1 ∩V2|
                               IoU =                                                  (4)
                                               |V1 ∪V2|
This metric captures global shape overlap and is particularly useful for assessing coarse structural
fidelity. Padding is applied to align voxel grid sizes when necessary.

As shown in Figure 4, Qwen2.5-3B achieves the best performance across both F1 and IoU metrics,
with an F1 median of 0.9836 and a volumetric IoU median of 0.9868. Even our smallest model,
CodeGPT-small, attains reasonable performance (F1 median 0.9688), confirming the effectiveness
of fine-tuning on symbolic CAD tasks. To the best of our knowledge, these scores significantly
outperform existing methods across all input modalities. This holds across multiple metrics, such as
CD, F1 Score, and IoU, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance for CAD generation.

Both metrics exhibit clear scaling trends: as model size increases, accuracy improves steadily from
124M to 3B. Similar to the previous observations, the 7B model (Mistral) does not further improve
over Qwen2.5-3B, likely due to underfitting from limited data. This again highlights the tradeoff
between model capacity and data availability in symbolic 3D generation.

5  Limitation

This work focuses on the output format for generative CAD, showing that CadQuery outperforms task-
specific command sequences. In principle, our approach can be extended to other input modalities
such as images, sketches, and point clouds, by using CadQuery as the output.  It would also be
valuable to annotate additional datasets with CadQuery to test whether this improvement holds across
input types. Another limitation is data scale. Although our dataset includes 170,000 samples, we
observe a performance drop on the 7B model, likely due to underfitting. Scaling up the dataset and
fine-tuning even larger models such as 13B or 32B could further test this hypothesis, but would
require significant annotation effort and computational resources.

6  Conclusion

We introduce a large-scale dataset of 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and propose Text-to-CadQuery
as a new paradigm for CAD generation. Our method directly generates executable CadQuery code
from natural language, replacing task-specific command sequences used in prior work. This change
simplifies the modeling pipeline and improves performance across multiple geometric and model-
based evaluation metrics. Moreover, by adopting CadQuery as the output, we enable the use of
pretrained language models rather than training models from scratch. This leads to faster training and
better generalization. More importantly, our approach can naturally benefit from future advances in
foundation models, such as improved multimodal understanding, spatial reasoning, or code generation,
without modifying the training framework or output design. Our experiments demonstrate that even
the smallest model fine-tuned on Text-to-CadQuery data outperform the Text2CAD baseline. As
model size increases, we observe a clear scaling trend in accuracy and executability. Our best model
achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple metrics. These findings validate both the output
choice and our overall framework, and open new directions for extending CadQuery-based generation
to diverse inputs and larger models.

Fig. 2: Early vs. late assembly relationship prediction. Top: early prediction (ours)
specifies connectors at design time; assembly reduces to deterministic frame alignment.
Bottom: deferring connection decisions to assembly stage forces a second planning
pass that must parse all generated code, infer coordinate systems, and resolve cross-
part dimensions—a task with long context and high failure rate.


stage. Similar to following a blueprint rather than blindly fitting puzzle pieces
together, early prediction sets up a connection plan before any 3D shapes are
generated. By explicitly defining these connections in advance, we separate the
connection planning from the shape generation. This avoids the LLM’s spatial
reasoning limits during the final assembly.

    Specifically, our multi-agent system has four agents. A Design Agent re-
ceives user input (text, image, or both) and outputs a structured plan containing
part specifications and assembly relationships, which we call connectors. Each
connector defines a named attachment point carrying a local coordinate frame, a
semantic label, and a joint type with motion limits. Next, Generation Agents
produce each part independently as a FreeCAD Python script [13] based on
the Design Agent’s plan. Then, an Assembly Agent builds the final model by
aligning matched connector pairs using FreeCAD’s constraint solver. Because
the connectors were already defined by the Design Agent, this step is strictly
mathematical and does not require a VLM or LLM. Finally, a Review Agent
evaluates a generated CAD assembly by inspecting multi-view renders of each
part and the keyframe sequences of the assembly’s joint motions.

  We further improve the generation quality from two aspects. First, we in-
troduce validation steps within both Generation and Assembly Agents, accom-
panied by a cross-stage rollback mechanism. Using error feedback derived from
the validation step in each agent, this mechanism classifies the error as either a
DESIGN-level or CODE-level failure. It then re-invokes only the responsible agent,
thereby reducing redundant effort. Second, a self-evolving experience store ac-
cumulates design knowledge derived from the Review Agent into a partitioned
vector database after each task. This allows relevant knowledge to be retrieved
and referenced in subsequent tasks. Our contributions are summarized as follows:

– A Multi-Agent Framework for Articulated CAD: We propose ArtiCAD,
  the  first training-free multi-agent system to generate editable, articulated4      Y. Shui et al.


 CAD assemblies from text and images. This fills the gap between single-part
 CAD generation and non-editable 3D reconstruction.
– Design-Stage Assembly Planning: We propose predicting assembly rela-
  tionships using the Connector during the design rather than assembly stage,
  bypassing the spatial reasoning limitations of current LLMs/VLMs.
– Cross-Stage Feedback and Self-Evolution: To improve generation qual-
   ity, we introduce validation steps within the generation and assembly stages,
  accompanied by a cross-stage rollback mechanism. We also propose a self-
  evolving experience store that accumulates design knowledge for future tasks.
– New Benchmark and Downstream Applications: We introduce ArtiCAD-
  Bench, a 120-task benchmark, and conduct cross-domain evaluations on CAD-
  Prompt [1] and ACD [19]. In practice, ArtiCAD supports requirement-driven
  conceptual design and generates CAD assemblies that are directly exportable
  as URDFs for robotic simulation.


2  Related Work

2.1  Parametric CAD Generation

Early methods model discrete sketch-and-extrude sequences, with generative ap-
proaches like DeepCAD [53] (autoregressive modeling), SkexGen [57] (disentan-
gled codebooks), Text2CAD [24] (prompt-conditioned), CAD-GPT [48] (3D po-
sitional tokens), and CAD-Llama [30] (fine-tuning). Reconstruction counterparts
include TransCAD [9] and CAD-SIGNet [23]. To overcome the geometric limita-
tions of fixed vocabularies, recent methods synthesize executable programmatic
scripts (e.g., CadQuery). CAD-Recode [45] and Text-to-CadQuery [55] gener-
ate scripts from point clouds and text. CAD-Coder [17] introduces geometric
rewards, while CADCrafter [6] leverages latent diffusion. Advanced alignments
are achieved via reinforcement learning in Cadrille [25] or sequential fine-tuning
in CADmium [16].
   To mitigate execution errors, several frameworks wrap LLMs in generate–
execute–repair loops, utilizing sandbox traces (CADCodeVerify [1]), visual feed-
back (Seek-CAD [31]), evolutionary search (EvoCAD [43]), or VLM-guided edits
(CADEvolve [10]). Crucially, all these methods are restricted to producing single,
static parts without multi-part hierarchies or joints.


2.2 CAD Datasets and Assembly

Common CAD datasets provide single-part histories (DeepCAD [53], Fusion
360 Gallery [51], WHUCAD [11]) or multimodal annotations (Omni-CAD [56],
CADInstruct [38]). While assembly datasets exist (e.g., Fusion 360 and Auto-
Mate [22]), works utilizing them primarily focus on joint-axis prediction (Join-
ABLe [50]), mate-type classification (AutoMate), or next-part recommenda-
tion [32] for pre-existing components. Even CADKnitter [27], which generates a
complementary part under geometric constraints, assumes a given base model.                                                    ArtiCAD      5


To our knowledge, ArtiCAD is the first framework to synthesize complete, multi-
part articulated CAD assemblies with typed degrees of freedom from scratch
using high-level specifications.


2.3  Articulated Object Generation and Reconstruction

A separate body of work targets 3D articulated objects. Datasets like Part-
Net [39] and PartNet-Mobility [54] provide fine-grained segmentations and joint
annotations. For generation, methods learn diffusion priors (NAP [28]), use
graph-conditioned diffusion (SINGAPO [34]), leverage VLM agents to articulate
existing meshes (Articulate-Anything [26]), or synthesize geometry and articula-
tion via compact 3D/latent tokens (PhysX-Anything [5], PAct [35]). For recon-
struction, approaches build digital twins from interactions or multi-view images
(Ditto [20], PARIS [33]), recover objects via Gaussian splatting (ArtGS [37],
GaussianArt [46]), or convert static meshes to openable ones (S2O [19]).
    Critically, these methods produce non-editable mesh or Gaussian representa-
tions and generalize poorly beyond their training categories (pri

3.4  Verification

To cope with limitations of VLMs with regards to spatial reasoning and help in the process of translating the spec-
ification into code, we make use of a verification loop. This verification loop equates to the verification phase in
human development cycles, where the model is compared to the initial specification to ensure that the design ful-
fills all requirements. Accordingly, the QualityAssuranceEngineer takes as an input the model created by the
CadEngineer and creates various views of the model. The default is a set of seven images, taken from the top, bottom,
right, left, front, back, and an isometric one. The QualityAssuranceEngineer then compares these views of the
model with the specification and checks if the specification is met. In case there are deviations from the specification,
the QualityAssuranceEngineer provides a list of textual suggestions on what should be changed. Algorithm 3
summarizes the verification loop and the prompt promptqa is available in Listing 5.


3.5  Validation

Despite the initial interactive requirements specification, cp. Section 3.2, the specification may be incorrect or the
system may be unable to converge towards the desired model. To cope, we introduce an outer validation loop, analogous
to the validation in development processes [Verein Deutscher Ingenieure, 2021]. Here, the user is asked to confirm the
model created, and asked for specific feedback in case they are not satisfied. This human feedback is used to regenerate
the model, cp. Algorithm 4.

4  Implementation, Experiments & Discussion

4.1  Implementation

We implemented a prototype in Python, relying on several well-established packages. For code-based parametric model
design, we used the Python library CadQuery [Au et al., 2024], which comes with detailed documentation3 that can be
fed into a VLM. For checking the code generated before executing it, we relied on Python’s ast module. CadQuery’s
documentation is retrieved from the web using Python’s request package in combination with BeautifulSoup44.
We render stl files within the QualityAssuranceEngineer leveraging pyvista5. As VLMs, we rely on OpenAI’s
gpt-4o-2024-11-20 as a default, interacting with the API via OpenAI’s Python package6. Note that error handling
for the VLM API was crucial for robust execution.


4.2  Experiments

We conducted experiments with visual inputs of varying complexity, both for hand-drawn sketches and photos of actual
objects. Here, the number of geometrical features to be realized serves as an indication for complexity. As hand-drawn
sketches, we included a simple rectangular block, a rectangular block with two holes, and a strongly simplified sketch
of a toy car. We also included photos, one of a plastic cap and one of an angle bracket. Figure 3 gives an overview of
these inputs on the left, and Table 1 shows the textual parts of the specifications.

To assess the benefits of the individual agents in the system, we performed ablations using the selected designs. The

the planning step before the code generation. The designs in the third column, i.e., the first column of the “CAD team
iterations” correspond to the designs created by the CadEngineer together with the RequirementsEngineer, but

4.3  Discussion

Overall, the results of the VLM-based MAS are promising, especially when compared to the single shot generative
approach. By utilizing specialized agents that mimic typical engineering roles and a self-feedback loop in the
architecture, our method generates designs with a higher level of readiness and compliance to the requirements.
Nonetheless, there is significant variability in the results and making such VLM-based systems with self-feedback
robust is still challenging for several reasons: In line with limitations regarding spatial reasoning, VLMs still seem
to struggle especially with the orientation of surfaces, e.g., when selecting suitable workplanes for sketches. Also,
the MAS failed to generate more complex components. Note that simply increasing the number of iterations may

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive reference table with approximately 16 key references from the evidence gathered. Each row should contain: Reference ID (numbered), Authors/Year, Venue/ArXiv ID, Focus Area (e.g., code-based CAD, multi-agent, B-rep generation, benchmark, survey, spatial reasoning), and Key Quantitative Result if available. Include: 1) Barkley et al. 2025 CADSmith (multi-agent CadQuery, 100% exec, 38x CD reduction), 2) Alrashedy et al. 2025 CADCodeVerify (visual feedback CadQuery, GPT-4 96.5% success), 3) Doris et al. 2025 CAD-Coder VLM (100% VSR, IoU_best), 4) Xie & Ju 2025 Text-to-CadQuery (69.3% exact match), 5) Chen et al. 2025 CADCrafter (DPO, Med CD 0.026, IR 3.6%), 6) Tsuji et al. 2025 GenCAD-Self-Repairing (feasibility 93.1%\u219297.0%), 7) Fan et al. 2025 CADDesigner (ECIP paradigm), 8) Ocker et al. 2025 From Idea to CAD (multi-agent CadQuery), 9) Shui et al. 2026 ArtiCAD (multi-agent articulated assembly), 10) Chen et al. 2025 CADReview/ReCAD (error detection OpenSCAD, GPT-4o only 41.5% acc), 11) Sadik & Bujny 2025 HITL evaluation (CadQuery metrics), 12) Zhang et al. 2026 LLMs for CAD Survey (ACM Computing Surveys), 13) Wu et al. 2026 AI-driven CAD Survey (Computational Visual Media), 14) Mouselinos et al. 2024 Geometric Reasoning Gap, 15) Kilpel\u00e4inen 2025 Gen AI for Robotics 3D Modeling (CadQuery/OpenSCAD thesis), 16) K\u00e4ns\u00e4l\u00e4 2026 Gen AI CAD/Manufacturing (multi-agent OpenSCAD thesis)."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Ref. | Authors / Year | Venue / ArXiv ID | Focus Area | Key Quantitative Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Barkley, Loghmani, Farimani (2026) | arXiv:2603.26512 | Multi-agent CadQuery generation with programmatic geometric validation | 100% execution success; mean Chamfer Distance reduced from 28.37 to 0.74; median F1 0.9707→0.9846; median IoU 0.8085→0.9629; ~38× CD reduction (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000011) |
| 2 | Alrashedy, Tambwekar, Zaidi, Langwasser, Xu, Gombolay (2025) | arXiv:2410.05340 | Visual-feedback CadQuery verification / critic-revisor loop | On CADPrompt, GPT-4 + CADCodeVerify reached 96.5% success rate in few-shot; point-cloud distance improved from 0.155 to 0.127; IoGT to 0.944 (pqac-00000030) |
| 3 | Doris, Alam, Nobari, Ahmed (2026) | arXiv:2505.14646 | Vision-language CadQuery code generation | Reports 100% valid syntax rate (VSR); evaluates geometry with IOU_best on 100 samples; near-perfect example IoU_best = 0.963 (pqac-00000032) |
| 4 | Xie, Ju (2025) | arXiv:2505.06507 | Text-to-CadQuery symbolic CAD code generation | Best model achieved 69.30% top-1 exact match; F1 median 0.9836; volumetric IoU median 0.9868; Mistral variant invalid rate 1.32%; GPT-4.1 mini invalid rate 0% (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000006) |
| 5 | Chen et al. (2025) | CVPR / arXiv:2504.04753 | Image-to-parametric CAD with DPO and code checker feedback | On DeepCAD multi-view: Acc_cmd 84.62, Acc_para 73.31, Med CD 0.026, Invalid Rate 0.036; on RealCAD multi-view: Med CD 0.062, IR 0.067 (pqac-00000029) |
| 6 | Tsuji, Medina, Gupta, Alam (2025) | arXiv:2505.23287 | Self-repairing feasibility enhancement for CAD generation | Feasibility improved from 0.931 to 0.970; fixed 65.84% of baseline infeasible cases (532/808); mean MMD changed 0.180→0.200 (pqac-00000033) |
| 7 | Fan et al. (2025) | arXiv:2508.01031 | Agent-based CAD generation; ECIP coding paradigm | Primarily architectural contribution: ECIP replaces implicit CadQuery context with explicit state passing and structured error triples for better debugging; no headline benchmark number in extracted evidence (pqac-00000035) |
| 8 | Ocker, Menzel, Sadik, Rios (2025) | arXiv:2503.04417 | Multi-agent CadQuery workflow with requirement clarification, verification, and human validation | Qualitative ablation shows agent team outperforms 0-shot VLM baseline; uses 7-view QA loop and user validation; no consolidated scalar benchmark extracted (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000037) |
| 9 | Shui et al. (2025) | arXiv:2505.22304 | OpenSCAD program review, error detection, and correction | GPT-4o feedback accuracy only 41.54% on human-made and 31.84% on machine-made programs; invalid code rate for closed-source MLLMs exceeds 25%; ReCAD outperforms by >30% on feedback accuracy (pqac-00000034) |

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a mitigation strategy matrix table organized by the four mitigation axes (A through D). Each row should be a specific mitigation strategy with columns: Mitigation Axis, Strategy, Representative Reference(s), How it Works (1-2 sentences), and Reported Effectiveness.

Axis A (Tool-Side Guardrails):
- OCCT kernel topology checks: CADSmith uses bounding-box, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, solid validity checks (100% exec success)
- DPO with code-checker feedback: CADCrafter uses automatic code checker feedback + DPO to enforce geometric validity (IR 3.6%)
- Self-repair diffusion pipeline: GenCAD-Self-Repairing uses diffusion guidance and regression to fix infeasible B-reps (fixes 65.84% infeasible)
- Watertightness/manifold checks: Wu et al. survey notes many AI-generated models contain self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges

Axis B (Prompting/Agent Strategies):
- Multi-agent with VLM judge: CADSmith Planner/Coder/Executor/Validator/Refiner + independent VLM Judge (38x CD improvement)
- Visual feedback VQA loop: CADCodeVerify generates and answers validation questions from renders (5% success rate improvement)
- Multi-agent role-based design: Ocker et al. RequirementsEngineer/CadEngineer/QualityAssuranceEngineer (iterative improvement)
- Design-stage connector planning: ArtiCAD predicts assembly relationships before generation to bypass spatial reasoning limits
- Structured error feedback: CADDesigner ECIP provides error triples (cause, location, corrective action)
- RL fine-tuning: cadrille uses GRPO for online RL on CAD tasks

Axis C (Representation Choices):
- CadQuery/Python code: Text-to-CadQuery, CAD-Coder show code-based representation outperforms command sequences
- Explicit context imperative paradigm (ECIP): CADDesigner replaces implicit CadQuery chaining with explicit state passing
- B-rep direct generation: GraphBrep, HiDiGen diffusion approaches
- Datasets: DeepCAD 178K, ABC 1M+, Fusion 360 Gallery 8.6K, SketchGraphs 15M

Axis D (Human-in-the-Loop):
- Human validation loop: Ocker et al. outer validation loop with user feedback after verification
- Quantitative HITL evaluation: Sadik & Bujny metrics pipeline (volumetric, surface, Hausdorff) with iterative correction to convergence
- Review agent + user: ArtiCAD review agent evaluates renders of each part + joint motions
- Code-level correction: Sadik & Bujny show code-level prompts achieve near-perfect reconstruction"}

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Mitigation Axis | Strategy | Representative Reference(s) | How it Works (1-2 sentences) | Reported Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | OCCT kernel topology checks | CADSmith (Barkley et al., 2026) (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000011) | The pipeline checks executable CadQuery/OCCT outputs against deterministic geometry facts: bounding-box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity, then uses these signals in an outer repair loop. This catches millimeter-scale dimensional and topology errors that image-only judging can miss. | 100% execution success; median F1 improved from 0.9707 to 0.9846; median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629; mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74 (pqac-00000001) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | DPO with code-checker feedback | CADCrafter (Chen et al., 2025) (pqac-00000029) | CADCrafter fine-tunes its generative model using automatic CAD code checker feedback as a preference signal, so geometrically invalid or non-compiling sequences are penalized during training. This injects non-differentiable CAD validity signals into learning. | On DeepCAD multi-view, Invalid Rate = 0.036 and Median Chamfer Distance = 0.026; on RealCAD multi-view, IR = 0.067 and Med CD = 0.062 (pqac-00000029) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | Self-repair diffusion pipeline | GenCAD-Self-Repairing (Tsuji et al., 2025) (pqac-00000033) | Infeasible command sequences are identified by failed B-rep construction, then repaired by latent-space diffusion guidance plus a regressor that maps infeasible latent vectors toward feasible ones. The corrected latent is decoded again to improve geometry feasibility. | Feasibility improved from 0.931 to 0.970; repaired 65.84% of baseline infeasible cases (532/808) (pqac-00000033) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | Watertightness / manifold validity checks | AI-driven CAD survey (Wu et al., 2026); CADEvolve discussion (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000004) | Surveyed work emphasizes explicit checks for self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges, non-watertight solids, and degenerate outputs before downstream manufacturing, slicing, or FEA. These checks are proposed as basic admissibility filters for generated CAD. | Survey identifies non-watertight and non-manifold outputs as a major blocker for manufacturing/FEA; CADEvolve treats invalid generations as a first-class error category in training/evaluation (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000004) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Multi-agent with independent VLM judge | CADSmith (Barkley et al., 2026) (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000000) | CADSmith decomposes work across Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, and Refiner agents, with an independent vision-language Judge that sees code, OCCT metrics, and three-view renders. This critic-revisor architecture reduces self-confirmation bias and supports iterative repair. | Full pipeline produced 100% execution success and ~38× lower mean Chamfer Distance than zero-shot; removing renders increased T3 mean CD from 1.42 to 49.68 (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000008) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Visual feedback VQA loop | CADCodeVerify (Alrashedy et al., 2025) (pqac-00000030) | A VLM generates validation questions about rendered geometry, answers them from the image, and uses the resulting feedback to revise CAD code. This creates a visual chain-of-thought / critic loop anchored in rendered shape rather than text alone. | GPT-4 few-shot success rate improved from 96.0% to 96.5%; point-cloud distance improved from 0.155 to 0.127; IoGT rose to 0.944 (pqac-00000030) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Multi-agent role-based design | From Idea to CAD (Ocker et al., 2025) (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000037) | The system uses specialized RequirementsEngineer, CadEngineer, and QualityAssuranceEngineer roles, plus a verification loop over seven rendered views and an outer human validation loop. The decomposition mirrors real engineering workflows and helps localize errors. | Reported qualitatively to outperform a 0-shot VLM baseline across example tasks; authors note higher readiness/compliance but also persistent failures on orientation/workplane selection for complex parts (pqac-00000037) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Design-stage connector planning for assemblies | ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) (pqac-00000036) | Instead of asking the model to infer assembly relationships after generating all part code, ArtiCAD predicts connectors, local frames, semantic labels, and joint limits up front. Final assembly then reduces to deterministic alignment with a constraint solver, bypassing long-context spatial reasoning at assembly time. | Introduces a 120-task benchmark and explicitly argues early connector prediction avoids the high failure rate of late assembly inference; quantitative benchmark details were not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Structured error feedback | CADDesigner / ECIP (Fan et al., 2025) (pqac-00000035) | ECIP replaces implicit CadQuery state with explicit state passing and emits structured error triples: (cause, location, corrective action). This gives the model machine-readable debugging feedback instead of vague runtime traces. | Reported as improving modularity, readability, and ease of debugging for agent-driven CAD generation; no scalar benchmark extracted from the cited pages (pqac-00000035) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | RL fine-tuning with online feedback | cadrille; ReACT survey context (pqac-00000016) | Recent CAD generation systems treat CAD sequence prediction as a sequential decision problem and optimize with reward signals tied to geometric fidelity and validity, rather than imitation alone. This is intended to improve long-horizon geometric decisions. | Survey identifies RL-based CAD generation as a key emerging direction; extracted pages note state-of-the-art gains but do not provide exact cadrille numbers in the available evidence (pqac-00000016) |
| C — Representation Choices | CadQuery / Python code instead of task-specific command tokens | Text-to-CadQuery; CAD-Coder (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032) | Direct Python/CadQuery generation leverages pretrained code-generation ability and exposes procedural logic, assertions, and debugging affordances unavailable in compact task-specific tokenizations. It also aligns with existing software stacks and RAG over API docs. | Text-to-CadQuery reached 69.30% top-1 exact match, F1 median 0.9836, IoU median 0.9868; CAD-Coder reports 100% valid syntax rate (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032) |
| C — Representation Choices | Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) | CADDesigner (Fan et al., 2025) (pqac-00000035) | ECIP replaces chained, implicitly stateful CadQuery calls with explicit input/output passing for every operation. That makes dependencies visible, permits normal Python control flow, and simplifies both debugging and code review. | Reported to improve code clarity, flexibility, modularity, and interpretability for LLM agents; benchmark numbers were not extracted in the cited pages (pqac-00000035) |
| C — Representation Choices | Direct B-rep generation with explicit topology | GraphBrep / HiDiGen; representation surveys (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016) | B-rep generation models operate on explicit faces, edges, vertices, and topology graphs rather than only sketch-extrude command histories. This can avoid fixed-vocabulary command bottlenecks but raises topology-learning complexity. | Surveys describe direct B-rep synthesis as a promising alternative to restricted sketch-extrude sequences, especially for complex geometry/topology; no single effectiveness number was extracted for GraphBrep/HiDiGen from the available pages (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016) |
| C — Representation Choices | Dataset/benchmark choice | DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Representation and evaluation quality are bounded by dataset coverage: DeepCAD provides command sequences, ABC provides large-scale B-reps, Fusion 360 Gallery provides human-authored build histories, and SketchGraphs provides constrained 2D sketches. Choosing the dataset determines whether the model learns code, topology, constraints, or edit history. | Reported sizes: DeepCAD 178,238; ABC 1M+; Fusion 360 Gallery 8,625 (or ~8.6K); SketchGraphs 15M (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Human validation loop after automated verification | From Idea to CAD (Ocker et al., 2025) (pqac-00000037) | After a VLM-based QA loop, the user is shown the candidate model and can provide specific corrective feedback; the system then regenerates the model using that feedback. This inserts a human checkpoint after automated geometric critique. | Demonstrated iterative improvements on examples such as wheel orientation/width corrections; authors position this as necessary because the system may fail to converge autonomously (pqac-00000037) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Quantitative HITL evaluation pipeline | Sadik & Bujny (2025) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) | The human-in-the-loop process evaluates outputs with volumetric similarity, surface similarity, Hausdorff distance, PCA alignment, ICP alignment, and a weighted final score, then iteratively revises prompts/code until convergence. This turns manual review into a measurable optimization loop. | Code-level correction prompts achieved perfect or near-perfect metrics in the cited case, including zero Hausdorff distance; paper argues structured metrics are necessary for engineering-grade review (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Review agent + user review of assemblies | ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) (pqac-00000036) | ArtiCAD’s Review Agent inspects multi-view renders of each part plus keyframe sequences of articulated joint motions, then feeds findings back into responsible upstream agents via rollback. A user can then judge the resulting assembly. | Presented as a practical safeguard for multi-part articulated assemblies; quantitative review-stage gains were not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Code-level correction as strongest supervision | Sadik & Bujny (2025) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) | Their experiments compare low-information inputs (orthographic views, sketches) to higher-information symbolic/code-level prompts, showing that direct access to parametric/source-level information drastically lowers edit distance and ambiguity. This suggests manual edits should feed back into the parametric source, not only into rendered geometry. | Code-level prompts achieved near-perfect reconstruction across metrics and outperformed image-only inputs; cited case reports zero Hausdorff distance (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |


*Table: This table organizes mitigations for LLM-generated mechanical CAD across tool-side guardrails, agent/prompting strategies, representation choices, and human-in-the-loop workflows. It highlights how each mitigation works and, where available, the reported empirical gains.*
