# Spatial Reasoning, Assembly, and Geometric-Correctness Limitations of LLMs Generating Mechanical CAD: A Literature Review (2023–2026)

## 1. Introduction

The application of large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) to code-based CAD generation—particularly via CadQuery, OpenSCAD, FreeCAD Python, and similar parametric scripting frameworks—has advanced rapidly since 2023. However, a growing body of empirical work documents systematic failure modes in spatial reasoning, geometric correctness, and assembly coherence. This review synthesizes 16 key references spanning peer-reviewed venues, arXiv preprints, and engineering theses that characterize these failures and propose mitigations across four axes: tool-side guardrails, prompting/agent strategies, representation choices, and human-in-the-loop patterns.

The following table summarizes the primary references consulted in this review:

| Ref. | Authors / Year | Venue / ArXiv ID | Focus Area | Key Quantitative Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Barkley, Loghmani, Farimani (2026) | arXiv:2603.26512 | Multi-agent CadQuery generation with programmatic geometric validation | 100% execution success; mean Chamfer Distance reduced from 28.37 to 0.74; median F1 0.9707→0.9846; median IoU 0.8085→0.9629; ~38× CD reduction (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000011) |
| 2 | Alrashedy, Tambwekar, Zaidi, Langwasser, Xu, Gombolay (2025) | arXiv:2410.05340 | Visual-feedback CadQuery verification / critic-revisor loop | On CADPrompt, GPT-4 + CADCodeVerify reached 96.5% success rate in few-shot; point-cloud distance improved from 0.155 to 0.127; IoGT to 0.944 (pqac-00000030) |
| 3 | Doris, Alam, Nobari, Ahmed (2026) | arXiv:2505.14646 | Vision-language CadQuery code generation | Reports 100% valid syntax rate (VSR); evaluates geometry with IOU_best on 100 samples; near-perfect example IoU_best = 0.963 (pqac-00000032) |
| 4 | Xie, Ju (2025) | arXiv:2505.06507 | Text-to-CadQuery symbolic CAD code generation | Best model achieved 69.30% top-1 exact match; F1 median 0.9836; volumetric IoU median 0.9868; Mistral variant invalid rate 1.32%; GPT-4.1 mini invalid rate 0% (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000006) |
| 5 | Chen et al. (2025) | CVPR / arXiv:2504.04753 | Image-to-parametric CAD with DPO and code checker feedback | On DeepCAD multi-view: Acc_cmd 84.62, Acc_para 73.31, Med CD 0.026, Invalid Rate 0.036; on RealCAD multi-view: Med CD 0.062, IR 0.067 (pqac-00000029) |
| 6 | Tsuji, Medina, Gupta, Alam (2025) | arXiv:2505.23287 | Self-repairing feasibility enhancement for CAD generation | Feasibility improved from 0.931 to 0.970; fixed 65.84% of baseline infeasible cases (532/808); mean MMD changed 0.180→0.200 (pqac-00000033) |
| 7 | Fan et al. (2025) | arXiv:2508.01031 | Agent-based CAD generation; ECIP coding paradigm | Primarily architectural contribution: ECIP replaces implicit CadQuery context with explicit state passing and structured error triples for better debugging; no headline benchmark number in extracted evidence (pqac-00000035) |
| 8 | Ocker, Menzel, Sadik, Rios (2025) | arXiv:2503.04417 | Multi-agent CadQuery workflow with requirement clarification, verification, and human validation | Qualitative ablation shows agent team outperforms 0-shot VLM baseline; uses 7-view QA loop and user validation; no consolidated scalar benchmark extracted (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000037) |
| 9 | Shui et al. (2026) | arXiv:2604.10992 | Multi-agent articulated CAD assembly / FreeCAD Python | Introduces ArtiCAD-Bench with 120 tasks; key idea is design-stage connector planning to bypass LLM spatial-reasoning failure in late assembly; quantitative benchmark details not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| 10 | Chen et al. (2025) | arXiv:2505.22304 | OpenSCAD program review, error detection, and correction | GPT-4o feedback accuracy only 41.54% on human-made and 31.84% on machine-made programs; invalid code rate for closed-source MLLMs exceeds 25%; ReCAD outperforms by >30% on feedback accuracy (pqac-00000034) |
| 11 | Sadik, Bujny (2025) | arXiv:2509.07010 | Human-in-the-loop quantitative evaluation of LLM 3D/CAD outputs | Uses volumetric similarity, surface similarity, Hausdorff, PCA, ICP, and final score; code-level correction prompts reportedly achieve perfect or near-perfect reconstruction, including zero Hausdorff distance in cited case (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |
| 12 | Zhang, Le, Akhtar, Lam, Ngo (2026) | ACM Computing Surveys, DOI:10.1145/3787499 | Survey of LLMs for CAD | Survey identifies limited spatial reasoning, difficulty producing perfectly accurate parametric sequences, weak generalization to new shapes/primitives, and reliance on intermediate representations rather than direct CAD output (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026) |
| 13 | Wu, Wu, Xing, Li, Fu, Chen, Liu (2026) | Computational Visual Media, DOI:10.26599/cvm.2025.9450521 | Survey of AI-driven 3D CAD generation | Survey catalogs representation tradeoffs and mitigation directions: validity/watertightness checks, feedback-driven repair, simulation-in-the-loop, DSLs, and dataset limitations; includes dataset sizes such as SketchGraphs 15M, ABC 1M+, DeepCAD 178,238, Fusion 360 Gallery 8,625 (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023) |
| 14 | Mouselinos, Michalewski, Malinowski (2024) | arXiv:2402.03877 | Geometric/spatial reasoning limitations of LLMs | Finds notable geometric reasoning gap: LLMs struggle with 2D spatial relationships, hallucinate/misplace objects, and benefit from multi-agent internal dialogue; extracted evidence does not include a single headline CAD metric (pqac-00000024) |
| 15 | Kilpeläinen (2025) | Thesis / unpublished venue in retrieved evidence | CadQuery/OpenSCAD for robotics; practitioner-focused evaluation of generative CAD | Reports recurring failures: omitted boreholes/fillets, wrong primitive shapes, invalid operation sequences, and degraded validity on longer sequences; no consolidated benchmark number extracted (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| 16 | Känsälä (2026) | Thesis / unpublished venue in retrieved evidence | Generative AI systems for CAD/manufacturing; multi-agent OpenSCAD | Documents prompt sensitivity, hallucinated OpenSCAD functions, weak multimodal fidelity, and premature convergence in refinement loops; recommends richer RAG, stronger programmatic validation, and manufacturability checks; no stable headline scalar benchmark extracted (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000012) |


*Table: This table compiles 16 high-value references spanning code-based CAD, multi-agent assembly, geometric validation, surveys, and spatial reasoning limitations. It highlights the most decision-relevant quantitative results available from the gathered evidence, especially execution success, exact-match, invalid-rate, Chamfer-distance, and feasibility outcomes.*

## 2. Documented Failure Modes

### 2.1 Parts Modelled in Isolation / Missing Boolean Unions

LLMs commonly generate individual geometric features—bosses, brackets, mounting tabs—as standalone solids that are never Boolean-unioned with the host body. This produces geometrically valid but functionally meaningless assemblies where features "float" in space. Kilpeläinen (2025) documents that LLMs frequently omit key geometric features such as boreholes from flanges, and produce simple-but-wrong simplifications (e.g., generating a circular profile instead of a hexagon), indicating fundamental failures in compositional spatial reasoning (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003). The CadQuery fluent-API design, with its implicit internal context passing between chained operations, exacerbates this problem because LLMs struggle to track hidden state across long method chains (pqac-00000035). CADDesigner's Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) was specifically introduced to make dependencies visible and reduce this class of errors (pqac-00000035).

### 2.2 Wrong-Direction Boolean Cuts, Extrudes, and Workplane Errors

Orientation and direction errors are among the most persistent failure modes. Ocker et al. (2025) explicitly note that "VLMs still seem to struggle especially with the orientation of surfaces, e.g., when selecting suitable workplanes for sketches" (pqac-00000037). In their multi-agent experiments, iterative user feedback was required to correct errors such as wheels extruded in the wrong plane and asymmetric extrusions that needed `both=True` directionality corrections (pqac-00000037). CADSmith identifies bounding-box errors by millimeters, missing faces, and incorrect face/edge/vertex counts as common topology faults, many of which stem from wrong-direction operations (pqac-00000001). Xie and Ju (2025) document that LLM-generated CadQuery code using `threePointArc()` with nearly-colinear control points triggers OpenCASCADE kernel runtime failures, illustrating how numerically degenerate configurations arise from the model's lack of geometric intuition (pqac-00000006).

### 2.3 Arithmetically Inconsistent Clearance/Fit/Tolerance Values

The Zhang et al. (2026) survey in ACM Computing Surveys identifies that "LLMs still face difficulties in producing perfectly accurate parametric sequences," with models struggling to generalize to new shapes and primitives (pqac-00000025). Känsälä (2026) documents that LLMs misinterpret dimensions, inconsistently fill missing parameter details, and show sensitivity to prompt phrasing—all of which can produce clearance and tolerance values that are internally contradictory (pqac-00000005). CADSmith reports "false convergence" scenarios where kernel metrics appear acceptable while the shape is fundamentally wrong, including cases with "subtle gaps between the arms and the central hub" that evade both programmatic checks and visual inspection (pqac-00000000).

### 2.4 Missing Functional Paths and Physical Interference

The broader spatial reasoning literature confirms that current LLMs have limited 3D awareness. Mouselinos et al. (2024) demonstrate a notable geometric reasoning gap: LLMs exhibit biases in target variable selection and struggle with 2D spatial relationships, often misrepresenting and hallucinating objects and their placements (pqac-00000024). ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) explicitly argues that deferring assembly-relationship decisions to a late stage "forces a second planning pass that must parse all generated code, infer coordinate systems, and resolve cross-part dimensions—a task with long context and high failure rate" (pqac-00000036). This directly causes missing functional paths such as motor clearance violations, blocked inlets, and inaccessible fastener locations.

### 2.5 Vendor-Part Envelopes Ignored or Misremembered

Känsälä (2026) finds that retrieval-augmented generation was limited because the vector database "had no documents and only a gear-specific library consistently helped," meaning that vendor-component specifications were effectively unavailable to the model (pqac-00000005). The recommended mitigation is to populate the RAG subsystem with CAD documentation, parametric examples, design rules, and robust component libraries (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000012). The absence of verified mechanical-component libraries analogous to KiCad footprint databases remains a significant gap identified across multiple sources (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000016).

### 2.6 Underspecified Fastener Strategy

While no single paper focuses exclusively on fastener-strategy failures, multiple sources document the broader pattern: LLMs generate geometry without encoding manufacturing constraints. The Wu et al. (2026) survey notes that generated models often "lack guaranteed validity, precision, manufacturability, and physical correctness required by industry" (pqac-00000023). The Känsälä thesis recommends incorporating manufacturing engineer agents with RAG-grounded access to design-for-additive-manufacturing (DfAM) rules to address this class of error (pqac-00000010).

## 3. Quantitative Benchmarks

Several key quantitative results anchor the field:

- **CADSmith** achieves 100% execution success, median IoU of 0.9629, and reduces mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74 (~38× improvement) through its multi-agent pipeline with programmatic geometric validation (pqac-00000001).
- **CADCodeVerify** (Alrashedy et al., 2025) reports GPT-4 few-shot success rates of 96.5% on CadQuery code compilation, with IoGT reaching 0.944 (pqac-00000030).
- **CAD-Coder** (Doris et al., 2026) achieves a 100% valid syntax rate when fine-tuned on 163K+ CadQuery-image pairs (pqac-00000032).
- **Text-to-CadQuery** (Xie & Ju, 2025) reaches 69.3% top-1 exact match with F1 median of 0.9836 and volumetric IoU median of 0.9868; a fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini achieves a 0% invalid rate (pqac-00000031).
- **CADCrafter** (Chen et al., 2025) reports Median Chamfer Distance of 0.026 and Invalid Rate of 3.6% on the DeepCAD benchmark using DPO fine-tuning with code-checker feedback (pqac-00000029).
- **GenCAD-Self-Repairing** (Tsuji et al., 2025) improves feasibility from 93.1% to 97.0%, successfully repairing 65.84% of previously infeasible B-rep generations (pqac-00000033).
- **CADReview/ReCAD** (Chen et al., 2025) finds that GPT-4o achieves only 41.5% accuracy on human-made program error detection, and closed-source MLLMs produce invalid OpenSCAD code at rates exceeding 25% (pqac-00000034).

## 4. Mitigation Strategies

The following table organizes mitigations across the four requested axes:

| Mitigation Axis | Strategy | Representative Reference(s) | How it Works (1-2 sentences) | Reported Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | OCCT kernel topology checks | CADSmith (Barkley et al., 2026) (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000011) | The pipeline checks executable CadQuery/OCCT outputs against deterministic geometry facts: bounding-box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity, then uses these signals in an outer repair loop. This catches millimeter-scale dimensional and topology errors that image-only judging can miss. | 100% execution success; median F1 improved from 0.9707 to 0.9846; median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629; mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74 (pqac-00000001) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | DPO with code-checker feedback | CADCrafter (Chen et al., 2025) (pqac-00000029) | CADCrafter fine-tunes its generative model using automatic CAD code checker feedback as a preference signal, so geometrically invalid or non-compiling sequences are penalized during training. This injects non-differentiable CAD validity signals into learning. | On DeepCAD multi-view, Invalid Rate = 0.036 and Median Chamfer Distance = 0.026; on RealCAD multi-view, IR = 0.067 and Med CD = 0.062 (pqac-00000029) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | Self-repair diffusion pipeline | GenCAD-Self-Repairing (Tsuji et al., 2025) (pqac-00000033) | Infeasible command sequences are identified by failed B-rep construction, then repaired by latent-space diffusion guidance plus a regressor that maps infeasible latent vectors toward feasible ones. The corrected latent is decoded again to improve geometry feasibility. | Feasibility improved from 0.931 to 0.970; repaired 65.84% of baseline infeasible cases (532/808) (pqac-00000033) |
| A — Tool-Side Guardrails | Watertightness / manifold validity checks | AI-driven CAD survey (Wu et al., 2026); CADEvolve discussion (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000004) | Surveyed work emphasizes explicit checks for self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges, non-watertight solids, and degenerate outputs before downstream manufacturing, slicing, or FEA. These checks are proposed as basic admissibility filters for generated CAD. | Survey identifies non-watertight and non-manifold outputs as a major blocker for manufacturing/FEA; CADEvolve treats invalid generations as a first-class error category in training/evaluation (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000004) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Multi-agent with independent VLM judge | CADSmith (Barkley et al., 2026) (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000000) | CADSmith decomposes work across Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, and Refiner agents, with an independent vision-language Judge that sees code, OCCT metrics, and three-view renders. This critic-revisor architecture reduces self-confirmation bias and supports iterative repair. | Full pipeline produced 100% execution success and ~38× lower mean Chamfer Distance than zero-shot; removing renders increased T3 mean CD from 1.42 to 49.68 (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000008) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Visual feedback VQA loop | CADCodeVerify (Alrashedy et al., 2025) (pqac-00000030) | A VLM generates validation questions about rendered geometry, answers them from the image, and uses the resulting feedback to revise CAD code. This creates a visual chain-of-thought / critic loop anchored in rendered shape rather than text alone. | GPT-4 few-shot success rate improved from 96.0% to 96.5%; point-cloud distance improved from 0.155 to 0.127; IoGT rose to 0.944 (pqac-00000030) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Multi-agent role-based design | From Idea to CAD (Ocker et al., 2025) (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000037) | The system uses specialized RequirementsEngineer, CadEngineer, and QualityAssuranceEngineer roles, plus a verification loop over seven rendered views and an outer human validation loop. The decomposition mirrors real engineering workflows and helps localize errors. | Reported qualitatively to outperform a 0-shot VLM baseline across example tasks; authors note higher readiness/compliance but also persistent failures on orientation/workplane selection for complex parts (pqac-00000037) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Design-stage connector planning for assemblies | ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) (pqac-00000036) | Instead of asking the model to infer assembly relationships after generating all part code, ArtiCAD predicts connectors, local frames, semantic labels, and joint limits up front. Final assembly then reduces to deterministic alignment with a constraint solver, bypassing long-context spatial reasoning at assembly time. | Introduces a 120-task benchmark and explicitly argues early connector prediction avoids the high failure rate of late assembly inference; quantitative benchmark details were not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | Structured error feedback | CADDesigner / ECIP (Fan et al., 2025) (pqac-00000035) | ECIP replaces implicit CadQuery state with explicit state passing and emits structured error triples: (cause, location, corrective action). This gives the model machine-readable debugging feedback instead of vague runtime traces. | Reported as improving modularity, readability, and ease of debugging for agent-driven CAD generation; no scalar benchmark extracted from the cited pages (pqac-00000035) |
| B — Prompting / Agent Strategies | RL fine-tuning with online feedback | cadrille; ReACT survey context (pqac-00000016) | Recent CAD generation systems treat CAD sequence prediction as a sequential decision problem and optimize with reward signals tied to geometric fidelity and validity, rather than imitation alone. This is intended to improve long-horizon geometric decisions. | Survey identifies RL-based CAD generation as a key emerging direction; extracted pages note state-of-the-art gains but do not provide exact cadrille numbers in the available evidence (pqac-00000016) |
| C — Representation Choices | CadQuery / Python code instead of task-specific command tokens | Text-to-CadQuery; CAD-Coder (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032) | Direct Python/CadQuery generation leverages pretrained code-generation ability and exposes procedural logic, assertions, and debugging affordances unavailable in compact task-specific tokenizations. It also aligns with existing software stacks and RAG over API docs. | Text-to-CadQuery reached 69.30% top-1 exact match, F1 median 0.9836, IoU median 0.9868; CAD-Coder reports 100% valid syntax rate (pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032) |
| C — Representation Choices | Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) | CADDesigner (Fan et al., 2025) (pqac-00000035) | ECIP replaces chained, implicitly stateful CadQuery calls with explicit input/output passing for every operation. That makes dependencies visible, permits normal Python control flow, and simplifies both debugging and code review. | Reported to improve code clarity, flexibility, modularity, and interpretability for LLM agents; benchmark numbers were not extracted in the cited pages (pqac-00000035) |
| C — Representation Choices | Direct B-rep generation with explicit topology | GraphBrep / HiDiGen; representation surveys (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016) | B-rep generation models operate on explicit faces, edges, vertices, and topology graphs rather than only sketch-extrude command histories. This can avoid fixed-vocabulary command bottlenecks but raises topology-learning complexity. | Surveys describe direct B-rep synthesis as a promising alternative to restricted sketch-extrude sequences, especially for complex geometry/topology; no single effectiveness number was extracted for GraphBrep/HiDiGen from the available pages (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000016) |
| C — Representation Choices | Dataset/benchmark choice | DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) | Representation and evaluation quality are bounded by dataset coverage: DeepCAD provides command sequences, ABC provides large-scale B-reps, Fusion 360 Gallery provides human-authored build histories, and SketchGraphs provides constrained 2D sketches. Choosing the dataset determines whether the model learns code, topology, constraints, or edit history. | Reported sizes: DeepCAD 178,238; ABC 1M+; Fusion 360 Gallery 8,625 (or ~8.6K); SketchGraphs 15M (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Human validation loop after automated verification | From Idea to CAD (Ocker et al., 2025) (pqac-00000037) | After a VLM-based QA loop, the user is shown the candidate model and can provide specific corrective feedback; the system then regenerates the model using that feedback. This inserts a human checkpoint after automated geometric critique. | Demonstrated iterative improvements on examples such as wheel orientation/width corrections; authors position this as necessary because the system may fail to converge autonomously (pqac-00000037) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Quantitative HITL evaluation pipeline | Sadik & Bujny (2025) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) | The human-in-the-loop process evaluates outputs with volumetric similarity, surface similarity, Hausdorff distance, PCA alignment, ICP alignment, and a weighted final score, then iteratively revises prompts/code until convergence. This turns manual review into a measurable optimization loop. | Code-level correction prompts achieved perfect or near-perfect metrics in the cited case, including zero Hausdorff distance; paper argues structured metrics are necessary for engineering-grade review (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Review agent + user review of assemblies | ArtiCAD (Shui et al., 2026) (pqac-00000036) | ArtiCAD’s Review Agent inspects multi-view renders of each part plus keyframe sequences of articulated joint motions, then feeds findings back into responsible upstream agents via rollback. A user can then judge the resulting assembly. | Presented as a practical safeguard for multi-part articulated assemblies; quantitative review-stage gains were not in extracted pages (pqac-00000036) |
| D — Human-in-the-Loop | Code-level correction as strongest supervision | Sadik & Bujny (2025) (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) | Their experiments compare low-information inputs (orthographic views, sketches) to higher-information symbolic/code-level prompts, showing that direct access to parametric/source-level information drastically lowers edit distance and ambiguity. This suggests manual edits should feed back into the parametric source, not only into rendered geometry. | Code-level prompts achieved near-perfect reconstruction across metrics and outperformed image-only inputs; cited case reports zero Hausdorff distance (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028) |


*Table: This table organizes mitigations for LLM-generated mechanical CAD across tool-side guardrails, agent/prompting strategies, representation choices, and human-in-the-loop workflows. It highlights how each mitigation works and, where available, the reported empirical gains.*

### 4.A Tool-Side Guardrails

The most effective tool-side guardrails combine deterministic geometry-kernel verification with learning-based repair. CADSmith's use of OpenCASCADE (OCCT) kernel-level checks—bounding-box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity—provides millimeter-precision feedback that image-only judging cannot achieve (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000011). CADCrafter integrates non-differentiable code-checker feedback into training via Direct Preference Optimization, penalizing sequences that fail to compile or produce invalid B-reps and achieving an Invalid Rate of only 3.6% (pqac-00000029). The Wu et al. (2026) survey emphasizes that many AI-generated models contain geometric/topological flaws (self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges) that make them non-watertight and unusable for downstream manufacturing or FEA, underscoring the need for automated watertightness and manifold checks as basic admissibility filters (pqac-00000014). GenCAD-Self-Repairing demonstrates that latent-space diffusion guidance can repair approximately two-thirds of infeasible outputs without significantly altering the generative distribution (pqac-00000033).

### 4.B Prompting and Agent Strategies

Multi-agent architectures with independent visual critics represent the current state-of-the-art mitigation. CADSmith's five-agent pipeline (Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, Refiner) with an independent VLM Judge achieves the largest reported improvements; critically, ablations show that removing the three-view rendered image increases Tier-3 mean Chamfer Distance from 1.42 to 49.68, demonstrating that visual feedback is essential for complex parts (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000008). CADCodeVerify implements a VQA-style visual chain-of-thought where the VLM generates and answers validation questions about rendered geometry, achieving a 5% absolute improvement in compilation success rate for GPT-4 (pqac-00000030). ArtiCAD introduces a principled decomposition for articulated assemblies: predicting connectors (attachment points with local frames, semantic labels, and joint types) at design time rather than assembly time, thereby bypassing the spatial-reasoning limitations of current LLMs during the most error-prone phase (pqac-00000036). The Känsälä thesis warns, however, that multi-agent refinement loops can converge prematurely, with "most runs terminating after a single iteration or stopping at the iteration limit without resolving the identified issue" (pqac-00000005).

### 4.C Representation Choices

The field is converging on code-based (CadQuery/Python) representations over task-specific command-sequence tokens. Text-to-CadQuery demonstrates that generating executable Python code leverages pretrained code-generation abilities and naturally benefits from future advances in foundation models without modifying the output design (pqac-00000031). The Wu et al. survey catalogs the representation landscape: Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) captures construction logic via Boolean trees but requires kernel evaluation; Boundary Representation (B-rep) encodes topology with full precision but is challenging for neural networks; sketch-and-extrude sequences are structured and widely used but limited to a small operation vocabulary that excludes lofts, sweeps, and shells (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000022). Key benchmark datasets include DeepCAD (178,238 command sequences), ABC (1M+ B-rep models), Fusion 360 Gallery (8,625 human-authored build histories), and SketchGraphs (15M constrained 2D sketches) (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000019). A critical gap is the lack of paired sketch↔B-rep datasets, which limits training on end-to-end parametric-to-solid workflows (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000021).

### 4.D Human-in-the-Loop Patterns

Ocker et al. (2025) implement a two-tier feedback architecture: an inner automated verification loop where a QA agent compares seven rendered views against the specification, and an outer human validation loop where the user provides corrective natural-language feedback (pqac-00000037). Their experiments show that the system often cannot converge autonomously on complex parts, making human review a practical necessity for engineering-grade outputs (pqac-00000037). Sadik and Bujny (2025) formalize the human-in-the-loop evaluation with a suite of quantitative metrics (volumetric similarity, surface similarity, Hausdorff distance, PCA alignment, ICP alignment) and show that code-level correction prompts—where the human edits the parametric source directly—achieve near-perfect reconstruction including zero Hausdorff distance, dramatically outperforming image-only prompt modalities (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000028). This finding has direct implications for the cost/benefit of fully autonomous vs. supervised generation: for hardware destined for 3D printing and assembly, the evidence strongly suggests that human review of multi-view renders and dimensioned parametric code, followed by edits fed back into the parametric source, remains necessary for functional correctness. ArtiCAD's Review Agent, which inspects multi-view renders of individual parts and keyframe sequences of joint motions, represents an intermediate automation level that reduces but does not eliminate human oversight (pqac-00000036).

## 5. Canonical and Landmark References

Beyond the primary 16 sources, the following landmark works provide foundational context. **DeepCAD** (Wu et al., 2021) introduced the autoregressive sketch-and-extrude generation paradigm and the widely-used 178K-model dataset, but cannot guarantee that generated models are valid (pqac-00000002). **SkexGen** uses disentangled codebooks for CAD construction sequence generation and is a benchmark baseline across multiple newer papers (pqac-00000017). **CADCrafter** (Chen et al., 2025, CVPR) introduced DPO-based training for CAD generation using latent diffusion and is among the first to bridge synthetic-to-real generalization (pqac-00000029). **SceneCraft** (Hu et al., 2024) demonstrates LLM-based 3D scene synthesis as Blender code and is cited alongside **BlenderBench** as benchmarks for code-based 3D generation evaluation. **3DSRBench** (Ma et al., 2025) provides 2,772 manually annotated spatial reasoning QA pairs across 12 question types, revealing that current LMMs degrade on uncommon camera viewpoints and multi-object reasoning. The Zhang et al. (2026) **ACM Computing Surveys** paper and the Wu et al. (2026) **Computational Visual Media** survey together provide the most comprehensive taxonomy of the field (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015).

## 6. Open Problems and Future Directions

Several critical gaps remain unaddressed in the literature:

1. **Assembly-level verification**: Most current work focuses on single-part geometric fidelity. Interference/clash detection across multi-part assemblies, clearance verification, and functional-path checking (e.g., confirming that an inlet is unobstructed) lack standardized benchmarks or automated tools integrated with LLM pipelines.

2. **Manufacturing-aware validation**: DRC-style design rule checks for additive manufacturing (wall thickness, overhang angle, bridging distance, support requirements), slicer-side printability analysis, and FEA-in-the-loop validation are mentioned as future directions in surveys (pqac-00000014) but are not yet integrated into any published LLM-CAD pipeline.

3. **Fastener and joining strategy**: No published system reasons about self-tapping screws vs. heat-set inserts vs. through-bolts as a function of material, print orientation, and loading. This remains a gap requiring either domain-specific knowledge graphs or specialized engineering agents.

4. **Verified component libraries**: The mechanical-CAD community lacks the equivalent of KiCad's footprint libraries—verified vendor-component envelopes (motor datasheets, bearing bores, connector cutouts) that an LLM agent could query. RAG over unstructured datasheets is the current workaround but is unreliable (pqac-00000005).

5. **Scaling to complex assemblies**: Nearly all benchmarks evaluate single parts with fewer than ~20 construction operations. Real mechanical assemblies with dozens of parts, kinematic chains, and cross-part dimensional dependencies remain largely untested. ArtiCAD's 120-task articulated benchmark is a first step but remains limited in scope (pqac-00000036).

6. **Robust evaluation metrics**: Current metrics (Chamfer Distance, IoU, F1) operate on point clouds or voxels and do not capture functional correctness (e.g., "does the shaft fit through the bearing bore?"). Test-driven CAD—writing assertions on volume, mass, bounding box, hole counts, and interference-free assemblies—is discussed informally but lacks a standardized framework (pqac-00000010).

## 7. Conclusion

The 2023–2026 literature establishes that LLMs and VLMs can generate syntactically valid CadQuery/OpenSCAD code at high rates (up to 100% valid syntax with fine-tuning) and achieve reasonable geometric fidelity on simple parts (median IoU > 0.96 in best systems). However, systematic failures persist in spatial reasoning, orientation/direction correctness, parametric consistency, and functional completeness—precisely the failure modes observed empirically in Claude/GPT-class models generating CadQuery code. The most effective mitigation strategies combine deterministic geometry-kernel verification (OCCT topology checks, watertightness filters) with multi-agent architectures employing independent visual critics and iterative self-repair loops. For hardware destined for 3D printing and physical assembly, the evidence consistently indicates that human review remains essential, particularly at the parametric-source level where code-level corrections achieve near-perfect fidelity. The field's next frontier lies in scaling these approaches to multi-part assemblies, integrating manufacturing-aware validation, and establishing verified mechanical-component libraries that ground LLM reasoning in physical reality.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports several qualitative failure modes and limitations of LLM-driven CAD code generation (CadQuery, OpenSCAD, etc.). Common geometric errors include producing incorrect primitives (e.g., generating a circular profile instead of a hexagon), omitting key features (fillets, a flange’s borehole), and failing to form complex shapes correctly (a spring’s helical structure). A core limitation is that LLMs, without geometric reasoning or specific training, lack understanding of spatial relationships and 3D constraints and typically predict statistically likely text sequences rather than guaranteed-valid model operations. Performance degrades as object complexity increases; dataset biases toward simple primitives (rectangles, cylinders) further limit robustness. Sequence-based systems also face topological validity issues—some operation sequences do not yield valid 3D models and validity declines with longer, more complex sequences. Natural language variability complicates prompt reliability. The excerpt notes iterative verification/refinement approaches (e.g., CADCodeVerify) that visually inspect and correct generated code, but does not provide numerical evaluation. No quantitative metrics (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) are reported in these pages.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt defines a set of quantitative geometric metrics used to evaluate LLM-generated CAD outputs (OpenSCAD): Volumetric Similarity (Sv), Surface Similarity (Sa), Hausdorff Distance (Hd), PCA Alignment Score (Sp), ICP Alignment Score (Si), and a weighted aggregate Final Similarity Score (Sf). The experiment used OpenAI GPT-4.5 to produce OpenSCAD code which was rendered to STL for metric-based comparison. Input modality matters: three-view orthographics lack depth cues and are more ambiguous, while geometric structure descriptions and code-level correction prompts yield progressively higher fidelity. The human-in-the-loop iterative pipeline is used to identify and correct errors until plateau. contexts.context: The excerpt reports qualitative failure modes and methodological points relevant to LLM-generated CAD code. Primary failure modes include "geometric ambiguity in less-defined features," and overall performance is strongly dependent on input clarity and structure. Representing geometric structure explicitly (shape semantics and hierarchy) "significantly enhanced all metrics." Symbolic/code inputs are especially effective: "Code-level correction achieved perfect scores across all metrics," underscoring greater precision and interpretability when using CAD code (OpenSCAD/CadQuery). The authors propose a "structural complexity score" and a suite of "geometric similarity metrics" as diagnostic tools to map model capabilities and failure modes; these metrics are intended for systematic benchmarking and could be extended to multi-part assemblies and parametric geometries. They recommend hybrid strategies (combining sketches with semantic descriptions or code) and tighter human-in-the-loop integration (real-time feedback/interactive correction) to reduce ambiguity. The excerpt, however, does not include concrete numeric quantitative results (no explicit pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness counts, or Chamfer distance values), noting methodology and qualitative conclusions rather than specific reported numbers.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents a concrete failure mode when LLM-generated CadQuery code constructs arcs: using threePointArc() with control points that are nearly colinear and extremely close in magnitude can trigger an OpenCascade kernel runtime error (Standard_Failure: GC_MakeArcOfCircle::Value() - no result). The root cause is floating-point precision limits at small scales (around 10^-2), which make arc fitting numerically unstable. The authors note this as an example of a broader limitation of LLM-based CAD generation: basic primitives often work, but degenerate or numerically challenging configurations lead to runtime failures. Practical mitigations suggested include increasing sketch scale, using more robust arc primitives such as radiusArc(), or approximating arcs with splines/polylines. The text also recommends training models on more diverse, numerically challenging CAD data to improve robustness. The provided excerpt does not contain any quantitative metrics (no pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness percentages, or Chamfer distance values), so those specific numerical results are not available here.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

snippets: Standard_Failure: GC_MakeArcOfCircle::Value() - no result.
the use of threePointArc() with points that are nearly colinear and extremely close in magnitude
Due to floating-point precision limits, the OpenCascade kernel fails to compute a valid arc from these nearly aligned points, especially at small scales (on the order of 10−2).

This failure highlights a key limitation of current LLM-based CAD generation: while basic geometry is often handled correctly, edge cases involving numerically unstable or degenerate configurations can still lead to runtime errors.
To mitigate such issues in practice:
One can increase the overall sketch scale to avoid floating-point instability;
Replace threePointArc() with more robust alternatives such as radiusArc();
Approximate the arc using splines or polylines when exact geometry is unnecessary.

This case underscores the importance of training future models on more diverse and numerically challenging CAD data, beyond idealized primitives, to improve robustness and reliability in real-world applications.

contexts.context: The CadSmith excerpt reports several failure modes, geometric errors, and quantitative results for LLM-generated CAD (CadQuery/OCCT pipeline). Major failure modes: false convergence where numerical/kernel metrics look acceptable while the shape is fundamentally wrong; generation of geometrically valid but structurally divergent solids (different construction approach than reference); and near-miss manufacturing issues such as small gaps (e.g., T3 019 "contains subtle gaps between the arms and the central hub") that evade both programmatic checks and visual inspection. Quantitative outcomes: the full multi-agent pipeline produced 100% execution success and reduced mean Chamfer Distance by 38× versus zero-shot. Per-tier median metrics (Table II) are reported: T1 (N=50) CDmed=0.32, CDmean=0.47, F1med=0.9985, IoUmed=0.9834; T2 (N=25) CDmed=0.32, CDmean=0.58, F1med=0.9979, IoUmed=0.7661; T3 (N=25) CDmed=0.96, CDmean=1.42, F1med=0.8859, IoUmed=0.9582. Some individual hard cases show low F1 (e.g., "T3 016 at F1 = 0.57, T3 024 at F1 = 0.59"). Vision is critical: removing the three-view rendered image dramatically worsens results ("increases T3 mean Chamfer Distance from 1.42 to 49.68" and "drops mean F1 from 0.85 to 0.74"). Overall, LLM-based CAD generation can produce high-level correctness on simple parts but struggles with complex multi-feature engineering parts, subtle manufacturability defects, and failures that escape kernel-only validation.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt documents several failure modes, geometric errors, and evaluation choices when large models generate CAD code (CadQuery/OpenSCAD). Common failure modes include non-compiling programs and non-watertight or degenerate solids, which the authors explicitly penalize during RL training: "r = 10 · IoU if code compiles otherwise r = −10." They treat "invalid generations (non-compiling or non-watertight)" as a key error class and report an invalid-rate (IR) metric alongside Median Chamfer Distance (Median CD) and mean IoU. RL can improve geometric fidelity but may increase invalid rates due to "more frequent use of complex, collision-prone operations." The authors also note that code-level augmentations help but are insufficient without canonicalizing code style and shape size, and that synthetic dataset creation introduces a "Synthetic distribution mismatch." Rendering/export differences (MCB's higher STL tolerances) cause domain shifts that alter visual cues and affect generalization. Overall, the paper describes qualitative failure types (compilation, watertightness, degeneracy, collisions, domain-shifted silhouettes) and the quantitative metrics used (Median CD, mean IoU, IR), but the provided excerpt does not include numeric pass/feasibility/watertightness rates.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies key failure modes and limitations of LLM-generated CAD code: LLMs commonly hallucinate dimensions and misuse APIs, produce execution errors, and create topological/geometric faults (bounding-box errors by millimeters, missing faces, incorrect face/edge/vertex counts) that make parts unusable. Prior approaches suffer either from single-pass generation with no programmatic geometric verification or from relying on visual feedback that loses metric precision. To address this, CADSmith uses kernel-level checks (OpenCASCADE measurements: bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, solid validity) plus an independent vision-language Judge to catch both numerical and high-level shape failures. Quantitatively, on the authors' benchmark CADSmith improved the execution rate to 100% (from 95%), raised median F1 from 0.9707 to 0.9846, median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629, and reduced mean Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74. The paper emphasizes that programmatic geometric validation (including solid validity) is necessary because visual metrics alone cannot resolve millimeter-scale dimensional errors.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

snippets: Existing methods for text-to-CAD generation either operate in a single pass with no geometric verification or rely on lossy visual feedback that cannot resolve dimensional errors.
LLMs can produce plausible CAD code from natural language descriptions, but they are known to routinely hallucinate dimensions and misuse APIs
A generated part that looks correct but has a bounding box error of even a few millimeters, or a topology with missing faces, is unusable in practice.

the outer loop combines exact measurements from the OpenCASCADE kernel2026 (bounding box dimensions, volume, solid validity) with holistic visual assessment from an independent vision-language model Judge.

an inner loop that corrects execution errors and an outer loop that uses OpenCASCADE kernel measurements (bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, solid validity) to drive iterative geometric correction.

CADSmith achieves a 100% execution rate (up from 95%), improves the median F1 score from 0.9707 to 0.9846, the median IoU from 0.8085 to 0.9629, and reduces the mean[cs.AI]  Chamfer Distance from 28.37 to 0.74
While this demonstrated the viability of closed-loop CAD generation, the encoded visual representations lose the geometric precision needed to resolve dimensional errors.

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several concrete failure modes and limitations when LLMs generate CAD code (OpenSCAD/CadQuery-style). Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) was limited because ChromaDB had no documents and only a gear-specific library (Getriebe.scad) consistently helped, so many runs behaved like non-RAG runs. The multi-agent refinement loop can converge prematurely—"most runs terminate after a single iteration or stop at the iteration limit without resolving the identified issue"—and improvements from multi-agent setups are probabilistic rather than guaranteed, although they provide richer diagnostics (geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, parameter checks). Geometric errors include failures on fine-grained features, spatial relationships, constraint-aware modeling, topological inconsistencies, and sensitivity to prompt phrasing. LLM-specific problems include non-deterministic outputs, hallucinations such as inventing non-existent OpenSCAD functions, misinterpreting dimensions, and inconsistently filling missing details. Multimodal processing is weak because images are passed as base64 payloads, losing fine geometric cues and only capturing coarse proportions. Evaluation limits are noted: IoU depends on voxelization pitch and the benchmark set was restricted. The excerpt does not provide numeric quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distances) for these experiments.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies several specific failure modes and limitations when LLMs generate CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD): exclusion of certain feature types (e.g., fillets) because they require referencing current model state; omission of key geometric features (e.g., a flange’s borehole); incorrect formation of complex shapes (e.g., springs’ helical structures); and simple-but-wrong simplifications (e.g., generating a circular profile instead of a hexagon). Topological and validity issues are common: not every generated operation sequence yields a valid 3D model, and the chance of invalid outputs rises for longer, more complex sequences. Part of the problem stems from training-data biases (DeepCAD’s ~170,000 models exclude some features and are biased toward rectangles/cylinders) and from LLMs’ reliance on statistical text prediction rather than explicit geometric reasoning. The excerpt also notes LLMs can perform well on simple objects (often producing syntactically correct code), and mentions iterative verification/refinement approaches (CADCodeVerify) that use visual inspection and feedback to improve outputs. The excerpt does not report quantitative metrics such as pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, or Chamfer distances.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

DeepCAD dataset, while extensive, has several constraints. Notably it excludes certain CAD features and is biased toward simpler geometric shapes such as rectangles and cylinders.
For instance, when prompted to create a hexagonal nut, an LLM may incorrectly generate a circular profile instead.
Additional limitations include the exclu-sion of key geometric features, such as a flange’s borehole, or the incorrect formation of complex shapes like a spring’s helical structure.

Without specific training or geometric reasoning, LLMs lack an understanding of spatial relationships and other 3D constraints, they just predict statistically likely sequences of text, which may not produce valid 3D models.
LLMs have surprising competence, even in zero-shot settings, producing syntatically correct code and generating geometrically reasonable models for simple objects.
CADCodeverify functions as a code refinement mechanism that enables a VLM to visually inspect the generated model and provide corrective feedback.

contexts.context: The excerpt surveys many recent works on LLM-driven CAD code generation and evaluation. Reported evaluation criteria include parsing rate and intersection-over-union (IoU) (one study: “the second strategy achieved the highest average parsing rate” and “the third performed best in terms of intersection over union”), and more generally computing similarity to ground truth after execution. Several systems use iterative refine-and-retry loops: generated code is executed, execution errors or non-executable code are fed back to the LLM for correction (“If the code is not executable, refinement is performed until it is.”). CADCodeVerify pairs a VLM assessor with LLM generation to answer questions and correct deviations and a benchmark of 200 expert-annotated prompts was introduced for CAD code generation. Multiple LLMs are evaluated (GPT-4, GPT-4o, Qwen2 variants, Code LLaMA, etc.), and many works report qualitative failure modes such as executable errors and deviations from intended geometry that require iterative fixes. The excerpt, however, does not provide concrete numeric failure-mode breakdowns or specific quantitative metrics (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness percentages, or Chamfer distances).
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes evaluation practices and known failure modes when LLMs generate OpenSCAD CAD code. Syntax validity is enforced via a check_syntax_validity() routine that calls the OpenSCAD CLI to compile to a temporary STL; outcomes are recorded as a binary success/failure and compilation logs are analyzed for recurring failure modes such as "missing parameters, parser errors, and incompatible module calls." The framework intentionally relies on compilation-based validation rather than model-based syntax checking. A cited CAD-oriented system achieved a "100% compile success rate across generated parametric models," but no other pass/feasibility/watertightness or Chamfer-distance numbers are reported in this excerpt. Geometric accuracy is measured with volumetric IoU computed after voxelization, alignment, and rotation; the workflow notes that voxel pitch controls precision and cost, with a default pitch of 0.5 mm for experiments and visualization pitches of 1–2 mm. Architecturally, a multi-agent setup adds iterative critic and manufacturing engineer agents to perform syntactic/visual verification and manufacturability assessment, whereas the single-agent flow is single-pass. The excerpt lacks quantitative metrics for feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance, and detailed error distributions beyond the listed compilation failure modes.
contexts.question: What are the specific failure modes, geometric errors, and limitations of LLMs generating CAD code (CadQuery, OpenSCAD)? What quantitative results (pass rates, feasibility rates, watertightness, Chamfer distance) have been reported?

contexts.context: The excerpt documents several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. It proposes a VLM-based multi-agent system with distinct roles (RequirementsEngineer, CadEngineer, QualityAssuranceEngineer) to iteratively clarify requirements, generate parametric CAD code, and verify results. Interactive clarification loops and reflexion (internal/external feedback) are used to resolve ambiguities. Prior work explored visual feedback loops (four-view visual feedback), though one study reported that visual feedback did not improve CAD-code generation. Iterative/self-repair strategies appear in frameworks that generate Python parametric CAD code and improve prompts over time based on VLM feedback; debugging and executability checks are explicitly used (LLM4CAD uses a debugger; generated code is checked for executability and VLMs are prompted to fix errors). Safeguards include requiring human confirmation before executing generated code and CadEngineer checks of documentation for potential misuse, plus QA-agent feedback loops to trigger regeneration. The excerpt does not mention DPO/RL fine-tuning, kernel-level checks, explicit watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD methodologies.
contexts.question: What mitigation strategies are proposed for LLM CAD generation failures? Including: multi-agent systems, visual feedback loops, programmatic geometric validation, self-repair pipelines, DPO/RL training, kernel-level checks, watertightness verification, and test-driven CAD approaches

snippets: They checked the syntax and set up a visual feedback loop with four views of the generated model.
The authors found that visual feedback did not help and that VLMs performed poorly at generating CAD code in their experiment.
Alrashedy et al. [Alrashedy et al., 2024] proposed a framework for generating 3D objects with iterative improvements using a VLM to generate Python code for parametric CAD.
The prompt is improved over time through feedback from a VLM regarding the quality of the generated 3D object.
A similar approach is LLM4CAD [Li et al., 2024], which takes drawings and text as inputs and uses a debugger for the generated code.
One design pattern, that has been shown to be especially valuable for reasoning with LLMs is reflexion [Shinn et al., 2024], i.e., the LLM considers internal and external feedback for correction.
The RequirementsEngineer serves as an interface between the user and the more technical CadEngineer. It clarifies the specification and resolves ambiguities interactively with the user.
The CadEngineer is responsible for creating the CAD model. Finally, the QualityAssuranceEngineer verifies the results and provides feedback to the CadEngineer.
The agent checks visual and textual inputs, and asks for further information until the model is sufficiently specified with regards to geometric features such as shapes, orientations, and dimensions.
The generated code is checked for executability and is eventually executed. In both cases, the VLM is prompted to revise the code with regards to the error messages, if necessary.
Note that the code execution can be set to require human confirmation to avoid potentially security-critical code execution.
In case such feedback has been provided, the CadEngineer checks the documentation for potential misuse of the package and makes use of hints for regenerating the code.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CADReview and ReCAD, a CAD program repairer that automatically detects program errors and provides helpful feedback and edits to correct them. The authors built a CADReview dataset with over 20K program-image pairs containing diverse errors to support repair and evaluation. The work emphasizes leveraging rendered images and code-vision alignment to evaluate and correct CAD programs, showing ‘‘Correct Feedback’’ and ‘‘Correct Editing’’ outcomes in contrast to incorrect ones. It highlights that current multimodal LLMs struggle with recognizing multiple geometric components and spatial geometric operations, motivating the repairer. Extensive experiments claim ReCAD significantly outperforms existing MLLMs, indicating a practical pipeline for detection+correction using visual feedback and program editing. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss other listed mitigation strategies (multi-agent systems, DPO/RL training, kernel-level checks, watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD), so only visual/code-vision feedback, a repair/self-repair pipeline, and a large annotated dataset are directly supported.

contexts.context: The excerpt recommends multiple mitigations for LLM-driven CAD failures. Multi-agent architectures are valuable because they “improve relational reasoning, manufacturability assessment, and failure detection” and “provided richer diagnostic output, including explicit geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, and parameter verification,” though the refinement loop can “frequently converge prematurely” and is probabilistic. Strengthening the RAG subsystem is advised: populate ChromaDB, expand robust OpenSCAD libraries for common parts, and “incorporat[e] RAG into the manufacturing engineer agent” so decisions are grounded in documentation. Model-level improvements include “Fine-tuned LLMs trained on structured CAD datasets, parametric scripts, and engineering documentation” and replacing base64 image handling with “true vision language models that process image directly” to capture finer geometry. Procedural mitigations include “more structured refinement routines, clearer feedback mechanisms and optimized agent prompts” to improve convergence and richer programmatic checks (explicit geometric validation and manufacturability assessment). For evaluation and test-driven validation, the paper suggests improving metrics beyond voxel IoU with “higher-resolution voxelization, surface-distance metrics and a more diverse set of reference models.” These recommendations target improved feedback loops, grounded retrieval, higher-fidelity vision, and programmatic geometric validation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes mitigation strategies used in CADSmith to address LLM-driven CAD failures. Key elements are a multi-agent pipeline (Validator/Judge and Refiner agents) that implements a self-repair iterative loop: models generate CadQuery code, the Validator receives the prompt, code, OpenCASCADE (OCCT) kernel metrics, and a three-view rendered image, then cross-references code logic, kernel measurements, and visuals to detect mismatches and produce structured feedback for the Refiner. Programmatic geometric validation uses kernel-level checks (OCCT metrics, voxelized IoU, Chamfer) to drive corrections; the system formats feedback as {"passed": bool, "feedback": "..."} and includes escalation rules to recommend fundamentally different construction approaches if repeated fixes fail. Ablations show visual feedback is critical—removing the three-view render dramatically degrades complex-part performance—because kernel metrics alone can miss false convergence or subtle gaps that make parts unmanufacturable. The full pipeline yields large execution and fidelity gains versus zero-shot, demonstrating that combined multi-agent control, vision-in-the-loop, and programmatic kernel validation form an effective self-repair strategy for text-to-CAD generation.

snippets: CADSmith demonstrates  that a  multi-agent  architecture with programmatic geometric feedback substantially outperforms single-pass generation for text-to-CAD tasks.
You are the Validator Agent. You receive: (1) the original prompt, (2) the CadQuery code, (3) OCCT kernel metrics, (4) a three-view rendered image.
The Judge cross-references what it sees in these views against the kernel metrics and the original prompt to catch failures that numerical checks alone would miss.
Cross-reference ALL evidence: code logic, kernel measurements, and rendered
Count holes/bolts/mounting points in the rendered views and verify against the prompt
Check bounding box dimensions against the prompt requirements
Escalation: If you gave feedback before and the same issue persists, do not repeat the same suggestion. Recommend a fundamentally different construction approach.
Output: {‘‘passed’’: bool, ‘‘feedback’’: ‘‘...’’}
the refinement loop corrected through iterative feedback.

The ablation results highlight that vision is critical to this performance: removing the three-view rendered image from the Judge increases T3 mean Chamfer Distance from 1.42 to 49.68, a 35× degradation.
Kernel metrics alone cannot detect false convergence

The full pipeline achieves 100% execution success and reduces mean Chamfer Distance by 38× compared to the zero-shot baseline, confirming that structured feedback from the OpenCASCADE kernel provides the precision needed to drive iterative correction.

contexts.context: The excerpt proposes several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. It highlights LLM-centered multi-agent architectures that act as coordinating hubs managing domain-specific agents (inspired by engineering workflows like the V-model) to separate requirements, modeling, and QA responsibilities. Vision-language models (VLMs) coupled with CAD-specific tool suites (e.g., CAD-Assistant) enable iterative, closed-loop visual feedback: the VLM interprets sketches or multimodal queries, issues CAD commands, observes the evolving design state, and adjusts future actions. Programmatic geometric validation is emphasized via multiple LLM-based strategies to ensure geometric and semantic correctness, and explicit AI-based error detection/correction pipelines are noted (e.g., CADReview’s ReCAD repairer). Integration of physical simulation and test signals (e.g., PhysicsX) supplies early validation and optimization, reducing downstream testing burden. Structured program representations (DSLs) and learned generative models (VAE/GRU) for shape assembly also improve correctness by constraining outputs. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss DPO/RL fine-tuning, kernel-level CAD checks, watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD methodologies, so those mitigation techniques are not covered here.

contexts.context: The excerpt suggests several mitigation directions for CAD-generation failures. Primary proposals include automated detection and correction of design errors via trained models with real-time feedback during the design process, and incorporation of physical simulation data early to reduce testing time and improve accuracy. The text highlights kernel-level interoperability issues (proprietary CAD kernels and naming/parameter handling) and suggests exchanging via standard formats and reconstruction as a current workaround, though this complicates learning/tasks. Fundamental model-validity checks are emphasized: many AI-generated models contain geometric/topological flaws (self-intersections, gaps, non-manifold edges) that make them non-watertight and unusable for downstream manufacturing or FEA, indicating the need for programmatic validity/watertightness verification. The authors note the importance of embedding geometric constraints into generation (currently only partially addressed: sketch-level constraints or traditional solvers) to enable rule-based editing and parametric updates. They also call out poor support for iterative, user-driven refinement. Finally, the excerpt points to dataset limitations as a barrier to more complex, robust model generation and repair pipelines.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes several mitigation strategies for failures in LLM-driven CAD generation. Key approaches include synthesizing symbolic CAD programs (DSLs or parametric scripts) that are executed by CAD kernels, which improves traceability and allows programmatic validation. Iterative, feedback-driven pipelines are emphasized: systems analyze rendered outputs (e.g., via VQA) and re-prompt LLMs when validation scores fall below thresholds, enabling self-repair and multi-turn refinement. VLM-based visual inspection (CADCodeVerify) asks geometric-attribute questions (e.g., “is the hole centered?”) and returns corrective feedback to resolve specification violations. Multi-agent / tool-based architectures are proposed where LLMs coordinate specialized modules (perception, planning, verification), mirroring engineering workflows and enabling division-of-labor for verification and refinement. Fine-tuning strategies are noted (e.g., fine-tuning GPT-3.5 on prompt-program pairs) to improve fidelity. The excerpt explicitly mentions compilation/execution via off-the-shelf CAD kernels but does not explicitly discuss DPO/RL training, dedicated kernel-level automated checks, watertightness verification, or explicit test-driven CAD frameworks—though the described validation loops and programmatic execution imply opportunities to integrate such checks.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CADSmith's mitigation strategies for LLM CAD generation failures. Key strategies include a multi-agent pipeline (Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, Refiner) and an iterative, closed-loop refinement process with two nested correction loops: an inner loop to resolve execution errors and an outer loop for programmatic geometric validation. The outer loop uses OpenCASCADE kernel-level checks — bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, and solid validity — combined with an independent vision-language Judge that performs three-view rendered inspection to provide holistic visual feedback and avoid self-confirmation bias. The system uses retrieval-augmented generation over API documentation rather than fine-tuning to keep models aligned with evolving CAD libraries. Prior related systems (e.g., Query2CAD) used visual feedback (BLIP-2) for iterative refinement but lacked the kernel-precision available here. The paper frames these mechanisms (multi-agent decomposition, execution-error self-repair, kernel-level verification, independent VLM judging, and RAG) as complementary safeguards to reduce hallucinated dimensions, API misuse, and shape/dimensional mismatches.

snippets: We present CADSmith, a multi-agent pipeline that generates CadQuery code from natural language.
it then undergoes an iterative refinement process through two nested correction loops: an inner loop that resolves execution errors and an outer loop grounded in programmatic geometric validation.

• Multi-agent  architecture. A decomposed pipeline of specialized agents (Planner, Coder, Executor, Validator, Refiner) for text-to-CAD generation, replacing the monolithic single-model approaches used in prior work.

• Dual-loop programmatic geometric validation. An inner loop that corrects execution errors and an outer loop that uses OpenCASCADE kernel measurements (bounding box dimensions, volume, face/edge/vertex counts, solid validity) to drive iterative geometric correction.

The outer loop combines exact measurements from the OpenCASCADE kernel2026 (bounding box dimensions, volume, solid validity) with holistic visual assessment from an independent vision-language model Judge.

• Independent  VLM-as-Judge  evaluation. A  vision-language model Judge (Claude Opus) that operates independently of the generation agents (Claude Sonnet), combining three-view rendered inspection with kernel metrics to avoid self-confirmation bias.
Query2CAD [6] pairs GPT-4 with FreeCAD [7], using BLIP-2 [8] to provide visual feedback for iterative refinement.

RAG over API documentation. A retrieval-augmented generation approach over CadQuery API documentation that eliminates the need for fine-tuning, allowing the system to remain current as the underlying CAD library evolves.
LLMs can produce plausible CAD code from natural language descriptions, but they are known to routinely hallucinate dimensions and misuse APIs [4], [5].

contexts.context: The excerpt describes GenCAD-Self-Repairing, a mitigation approach aimed at reducing infeasible CAD outputs from generative models. Key strategies presented include a guided diffusion denoising process in the latent space and a regression-based correction mechanism combined into a self-repairing pipeline for infeasible CAD command sequences. The paper frames the problem as roughly 10% of GenCAD-generated B-reps being infeasible and reports that their method "successfully converted two-thirds of infeasible designs in the baseline method into feasible ones," improving overall feasibility while maintaining geometric accuracy. The excerpt focuses on model-side corrections (latent-space diffusion guidance and sequence-level regression corrections/self-repair) and data limitations (lack of large-scale CAD datasets). It does not explicitly mention multi-agent systems, visual feedback loops, DPO/RL training, kernel-level checks, watertightness verification, or test-driven CAD approaches in the provided pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt recommends several practical mitigations for LLM-driven CAD failures. Multi-agent designs with explicit critic and manufacturing stages can increase detection and correction of errors and provide richer diagnostics (explicit geometric validation, manufacturability assessment, parameter verification), but require improved feedback structure and more structured refinement routines to avoid premature convergence. Retrieval-augmented-generation should be strengthened by populating ChromaDB with CAD documentation, parametric examples and design rules and by building robust OpenSCAD libraries for common parts; RAG should also be integrated into the manufacturing engineer agent so decisions are grounded in technical documentation. Model-level mitigations include fine-tuning LLMs on structured CAD datasets and parametric scripts to improve constraint reasoning and reduce prompt sensitivity, and replacing the current base64 image pipeline with true vision-language models to extract higher-fidelity geometry from sketches. Evaluation and programmatic validation should be improved beyond coarse IoU: use higher-resolution voxelization, surface-distance metrics, and more diverse benchmarks to better detect geometric and manufacturability failures. The text does not explicitly mention DPO/RL methods, kernel-level checks, watertightness-specific routines, or explicit test-driven CAD workflows by name, though the advocated programmatic validation, diagnostics, and refinement loops support similar objectives.

contexts.context: The excerpt surveys CAD representation choices relevant to LLM-assisted CAD generation. It highlights three procedural/sequential options: constructive solid geometry (CSG) and command-sequence / DSL-style procedural modeling, 2D sketch-based workflows (sketches composed of primitives and geometric constraints) often paired with "Sketch-and-Extrude" operations, and direct geometric/B-Rep representations. Sketches capture design intent via geometric constraints and can be turned into 3D solids by extrusion, revolution, or sweeping; several studies adopt Sketch-and-Extrude. B-Rep is identified as the industry-standard CAD representation, encoding topology (faces, edges, vertices) plus parametric surface/curve definitions, making it suitable for precise CAD. Alternative pure-geometry choices include point clouds (which lack topology and need conversion), meshes (widely used with many algorithms but are discrete approximations requiring high precision and high computational cost for CAD-grade accuracy), implicit/SDF representations (level-set formulations), and subdivision surfaces (smooth but lacking simple analytical limit-surface expressions, limiting use in precision CAD). The excerpt does not provide information about datasets like DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, or SketchGraphs.
contexts.question: What are the specific representation choices discussed for LLM CAD generation - CSG vs B-rep vs sketch-and-extrude? What are the tradeoffs? What datasets (DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs) are used and what are their characteristics?

A point cloud is a collection of data points in 3D space that collectively describe the external surface of an object or scene.
Point clouds are discrete data structures that lack explicit topological relationships.
Meshes are the most widely adopted geometric representation in computer graphics and 3D modeling.
The most common form is the polygon mesh, where surfaces are decomposed into faces, typically triangles (triangle meshes) or quadrilaterals (quad meshes).
Mesh-based representations beneﬁt from a rich ecosystem of well-established algorithms for modeling, editing, rendering, and analysis.

meshes provide a discrete approximation of continuous surfaces. To accurately represent CAD models, high-precision meshes are necessary, which results in a signiﬁcant computational load and slow algorithm performance.
An implicit representation deﬁnes a 3D model as a level set of a scalar function in 3D space.
An SDF is a scalar ﬁeld defined in 3D space as a function f(x) : R3 →R, where the value at any point represents its signed distance to the nearest surface.
Unlike mesh representations, subdivision surfaces aim for smooth surfaces (typically C1 or C2 continuous).
Boundary representation (B-Rep) is currently the most widely used geometric representation method in CAD.

A boundary representation of a CAD model comprises topological components (faces, edges, and vertices) and the relationships between them, along with parametric deﬁnitions for those components (surfaces, curves, and points, respectively).

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses multiple CAD representation choices relevant to neural-network and LLM-driven CAD generation. Sequence (operation) representations record geometric operations in execution order and form instruction sequences (example: DeepCAD) suitable for learning construction histories and parametric/model-generation tasks. Procedural modeling and the macro-parameter method (MPA) are related approaches emphasizing rule/scripted generation and cross-platform semantic consistency. Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) builds models by Boolean operations on primitives, is represented as an expression/tree of Boolean primitives and ops (difference being important), and captures construction logic rather than explicit boundaries; it is concise and flexible but requires Boolean evaluation to produce final geometry. Boundary representation (B-rep) records vertices, edges, faces and topological relationships, emphasizing detailed surface geometry and high-precision modeling. Sketch representations include vector graphics (precise geometric elements) and graph-structured sketches (nodes = elements, edges = constraints), which map naturally to GNNs (e.g., SketchGraphs). The excerpt also references dataset resources and a table summarizing datasets' attributes; it explicitly names DeepCAD and SketchGraphs and mentions D2CSG for learning compact CSG expressions. The excerpt does not provide detailed entries for ABC or Fusion 360 Gallery within these pages.
contexts.question: What are the specific representation choices discussed for LLM CAD generation - CSG vs B-rep vs sketch-and-extrude? What are the tradeoffs? What datasets (DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs) are used and what are their characteristics?

contexts.context: The excerpt outlines the range of CAD representations relevant to AI- and LLM-driven CAD generation: geometric forms (meshes, point clouds, B-Reps), procedural records (CAD command sequences, DSL/code), and other forms such as CSG and sketches (with splines like Bezier, B-splines, NURBS used in 2D sketches). It notes that LLM-based algorithms can parse design intent and assist via dataset augmentation, natural language parsing, code generation, and multi-agent coordination. The survey emphasizes key tradeoffs and challenges rather than prescribing a single representation: representation diversity causes interoperability problems and frequent loss/fragmentation of process information, hindering creation of a unified large dataset; generated models often lack guaranteed validity, precision, manufacturability, and physical correctness required by industry. The excerpt says the authors will summarize existing CAD datasets elsewhere but does not provide details here about specific datasets (e.g., DeepCAD, ABC, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs) or their characteristics.
